title: Cohomological properness of sheaves of sets...

pages.title: Cohomological properness of sheaves of sets...
pages.html:
XIII Cohomological properness
of sheaves of sets and
of sheaves of non-commutative groups

by Mme M. Raynaud1

1cm

The aim of this exposé is to use étale cohomology to generalize certain results of IX and X. It also shows how one can extend to sheaves of not necessarily commutative groups the results of SGA 5 II which still have a meaning for such sheaves. The notions of étale cohomology set forth in SGA 4 are assumed known.

The principal result (4) gives an important example of a non-proper morphism \(f\colon~U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}~S\), which is “cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant1\)”, that is to say such that, for certain sheaves of groups \(F\) on \(U\) (in the sense of the étale topology), the formation of \(f_*F\) and \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*F\) commutes with every base change \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}~S\). This property is indeed satisfied by the open subset \(U\) of a scheme \(X\) proper over \(S\), the complement of a divisor \(D\) with normal crossings relative to \(S\), at least if one requires \(F\) to be constant finite, of order prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\). If one no longer assumes \(F\) to be of order prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\), one has an analogous result on replacing \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*F\) by the subsheaf \(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}f_*F\) obtained by restricting oneself to considering the torsors under \(F\) “tamely ramified over \(X\) relative to \(S\)”. In particular this makes it possible to show that the tamely ramified fundamental group of an algebraic curve proper and smooth over a separably closed field, with a finite number of closed points removed, is topologically of finite type (12).

Section 4 is devoted to the homotopy exact sequence and to the Künneth formula.

Finally an appendix gives useful variants of Abhyankar's lemma proved in X.6.

pages.title: Reminders on the theory of stacks
pages.html:
0 Reminders on the theory of stacks

In what follows we shall use the theory of stacks expounded in [1] and [2]. We restrict ourselves to the case of the étale site of a scheme. Given a scheme \(X\), let us denote by \(X_\mathrm{\acute{e}t}\) the étale site of \(X\). Let us recall that a stack \(F\) over \(X\) is a fibred category over \(X_\mathrm{\acute{e}t}\) such that, for every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\) and for every pair of objects \(x\), \(y\) of the fibre \(F_{X'}\), the presheaf \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{X'}(x,y)\) is a sheaf, and such that, for every surjective étale morphism \(X'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\), every object of \(F_{X''}\) equipped with a descent datum relative to \(X'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) is the inverse image of an object of \(F_{X'}\).

One denotes by \(F(X')\) the category of cartesian sections of \(F/X'\). More generally, if \(\mathbf{Sch}_X\) is the category of schemes over \(X\) endowed with the étale topology, the stack \(F\) can be extended to a stack \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(\mathbf{Sch}_X\) and, for every morphism \(f\colon X' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), one still denotes by \(F(X')\) the category of cartesian sections of this stack \(\mathscr{F}\) over \(X'\).

A gerbe is a stack such that, for every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\) and for every pair of objects \(x\), \(y\) of \(F_{X'}\), every morphism from \(x\) to \(y\) is an isomorphism, that \(x\) and \(y\) are locally isomorphic, and such that the set of objects \(X'\) of \(X_\mathrm{\acute{e}t}\) such that \(F_{X'}\) is non-empty is a refinement of \(X_\mathrm{\acute{e}t}\). For example the stack of torsors under a sheaf of groups is a gerbe which, moreover, has a cartesian section. Conversely a gerbe which has a section, i.e. such that there exists an object \(x\) of \(F_X\), is equivalent to the stack of torsors under the sheaf of groups \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_X(x)\).

One has an obvious notion of subgerbe and of maximal subgerbe of a stack \(F\). Given a cartesian section \(x\) of \(F(X)\), there exists a unique maximal subgerbe \(G_x\) of \(F\) such that \(x\) factors through \(G_x\). One calls \(G_x\) the subgerbe generated by \(x\); it is by definition a trivial gerbe. The presheaf \(SF\) defined by

\[ SF(X') = \{ \text{maximal subgerbes of }F|X' \} \]

is a sheaf called the sheaf of maximal subgerbes of \(F\). Let \(O\) be the presheaf defined by

\[ O(X') = \{ \text{classes of objects of }F_{X'}\text{ mod. isomorphism} \} \text{.} \]

By associating to every object \(x\) of \(F_{X'}\) the maximal subgerbe of \(F|X'\) generated by \(x\), one obtains a morphism

\[ O \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} SF \text{;} \]

according to [2], this morphism makes \(SF\) a sheaf associated to \(O\).

A stack \(F\) is said to be constructible (resp. ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-finite, \(\mathbb{L}\) being a set of prime numbers) if, for every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\) and for every object \(x\) of \(F_{X'}\), the sheaf \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{X'}(x)\) [2] is so. One says that a stack is \(1\)-constructible if it is constructible and if the sheaf of maximal subgerbes is constructible.

pages.title: Cohomological properness
pages.html:
1 Cohomological properness

0

Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a morphism of \(S\)-schemes. If \(S'\) is an \(S\)-scheme, one considers the following diagram, in which all the squares are cartesian:

\[ \tag{0.1} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{{X}\ar[d]_f&{X'}\ar[l]_h\ar[d]^{f'}\\{Y}\ar[d]&{Y'}\ar[l]_g\ar[d]\\{S}&{\,S'.}\ar[l]} \end{array} \]

If \(Y_1\) is a scheme étale over \(Y\), one sets \(X_1 = X \times_Y Y_1\), \(Y_1' = Y' \times_Y Y_1\), and one considers the cartesian square

\[ \tag{0.2} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{{X_1}\ar[d]_{f_1}&{X_1'}\ar[l]_{h_1}\ar[d]^{f_1'}\\{Y_1}&{\,Y_1'.}\ar[l]^{g_1}} \end{array} \]
Definition 1

Let \(F\) be a stack over \(X\). One says that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 1\)) if, for every \(S\)-scheme \(S'\), the canonical functor (defined in the obvious way by the universal property of the inverse image of stacks):

\[ g^* f_* F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_* h^* F \qquad\text{ (cf. 1.0.1)} \]

is faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence of categories).

If there is no possible confusion regarding \(S\), in particular if \(S=Y\), one says cohomologically proper instead of cohomologically proper relative to \(S\).

2

Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets on \(X\); let \(\Phi\) be the stack in discrete categories associated with \(F\), i.e. the stack whose fibre over any scheme \(X_1\) étale over \(X\) is the discrete category having \(F(X_1)\) as its set of objects. One says that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 0\)) if \((\Phi, f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 1\)).

The canonical morphism

\[ \tag{2.1} { g^* f_* F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_* h^* F } \]

gives, by passage to the associated stacks in discrete categories, the canonical morphism

\[ g^* f_* \Phi \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_* h^* \Phi \text{.} \]

Consequently, to say that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 0\)) amounts to saying that, for every \(S\)-scheme \(S'\), the morphism (2.1) is injective (resp. bijective).

3

Let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups on \(X\) and \(\Phi\) the stack of torsors on \(X\) with group \(F\) [1]. One says that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)) if \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)). The condition of cohomological properness can be made explicit as follows.

Proposition 3.1

The notations are those of (0.1) and (0.2). Let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups on \(X\). One denotes by \(F'\) (resp. \(F_1\), resp. \(F_1'\), etc.) the inverse image of \(F\) on \(X'\) (resp. on \(X_1\), resp. on \(X_1'\), etc.). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)).

  2. (ii)

    For every morphism \(S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\), and for every torsor \(P\) on \(X_1\) with group \(F_1\), if \({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1\) denotes the group twisted from \(F_1\) by \(P\) [1], the canonical morphism

    \[ a_0 \colon g_1^*(f_{1 *}({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_{1 *}({\vphantom{F}}^{P'\mkern-6mu}{F}'_1) \]

    is injective (resp. \(a_0\) is bijective and the canonical morphism

    \[ a_1 \colon g^*(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^1 f'_* F' \]

    is injective, resp. \(a_0\) and \(a_1\) are bijective).

  3. (ii bis)

    For every morphism \(S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\), for every torsor \(P\) on \(X_1\) with group \(F_1\), and for every torsor \(R\) under \({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1\), the canonical morphism

    \[ \alpha_0 \colon g_1^*(f_{1 *}R) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_{1 *} R' \]

    is injective (resp. \(\alpha_0\) is bijective, resp. the morphisms \(\alpha_0\) and

    \[ \alpha_1 \colon g_1^*(\mathrm{R}^1 f_{1 *}({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^1 f'_{1 *}({\vphantom{F}}^{P'\mkern-6mu}{F}'_1) \]

    are bijective).

Proof

(i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii bis). According to [1] every torsor \(R\) with group \({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1\) is of the form \(R = Q \overset{F_1}{\wedge} P^\circ\), where \(Q\) is a torsor with group \(F_1\) and \(P^\circ\) the opposite of \(P\). One then has \(R' \simeq Q' \overset{F'_1}{\wedge} {P'}^\circ\). Let \(\Phi\) be the stack of torsors under \(F\) and let \(x\), \(y\) (resp. \(x'\), \(y'\)) be the objects of the fibre category \((g^* f_* \Phi)_{Y_1'}\) (resp. \((f'_* \Phi')_{Y_1'}\)) associated with \(P\), \(Q\) (resp. \(P'\), \(Q'\)). One has the relation

\[ Q\overset{F_1}{\wedge} P^\circ \simeq \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{F_1}(P,Q) \text{,} \]

and it follows that one has canonical isomorphisms

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{Y'_1}(x,y) \simeq g_1^* f_{1 *}(Q \overset{F_1}{\wedge} P^\circ) \text{,}\quad \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{Y_1'}(x',y') \simeq f'_{1 *}(Q' \overset{F'_1}{\wedge}{P'}^\circ)\text{.} \]

Consequently the morphism \(\alpha_0\) is identified with the morphism

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{Y'_1}(x,y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{Y'_1}(x',y') \text{,} \]

whence it follows that, if \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), \(\alpha_0\) is injective and that, if \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), \(\alpha_0\) is bijective.

Suppose now that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), i.e. that the canonical morphism

\[ \varphi \colon g^* f_* \Phi \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_* \Phi' \]

is an equivalence. Let \(G\) be the sheaf of maximal sub-gerbes of the stack \(f_* \Phi\) [1]; one then has an isomorphism \(G \simeq \mathrm{R}^1 f_* F\). Since \(g^* G\) is the sheaf of maximal sub-gerbes of \(g^* f_* \Phi\) [2], the morphism \(\alpha_1\) is obtained from \(\varphi|Y'_1\) by taking the sheaves of maximal sub-gerbes, and hence is an isomorphism.

(ii bis)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii). It suffices to show that, if the morphisms \(\alpha_0\) are bijective, then the morphisms \(a_1\) are injective. Let \(Y'_1\) be a scheme étale over \(Y'\), \(s\) and \(t\) two elements of \(g^*(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F)(Y'_1)\) having the same image in \(\mathrm{R}^1 f'_* F'(Y'_1)\), and let us show that one has \(s=t\). The assertion is local for the étale topology of \(Y'_1\) and, taking into account the definition of the inverse image \(g^*(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F)\), one may suppose that \(Y'_1\) is the inverse image of a scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\) and that \(s\) and \(t\) come from torsors \(P\) and \(Q\) on \(X_1\). The hypothesis made on \(s\) and \(t\) then means that the inverse images \(P'\) and \(Q'\) of \(P\) and \(Q\) on \(X'_1\) are isomorphic locally for the étale topology of \(Y'_1\). If one sets \(R = \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{F_1}(P,Q)\), the fact that the morphism

\[ g_1^* f_{1 *} R \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_{1 *} R' \]

is bijective proves that \(P\) and \(Q\) are isomorphic locally for the étale topology of \(Y_1\), and hence that one has \(s=t\).

(ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i). To prove that \(\varphi\) is faithful (resp. fully faithful), it suffices to show that, if \(Y_1\) is a scheme étale over \(Y\), if \(P\), \(Q\) are two torsors on \(X_1\) with group \(F_1\), if \(x\), \(y\) (resp. \(x'\), \(y'\)) are the objects of \((g^* f_* \Phi)_{Y'_1}\) (resp. \((f'_* \Phi')_{Y'_1}\)) associated with \(P\), \(Q\) (resp. \(P'\), \(Q'\)), the morphism

\[ a \colon \operatorname{Hom}(x,y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(x',y') \]

is injective (resp. bijective). Now \(a\) is identified with the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(Y'_1,g^*_1 f_{1 *} (Q \overset{F_1}{\wedge} P^\circ)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(Y'_1, f'_{1 *}(Q' \overset{F'_1}{\wedge} {P'}^\circ))\text{.} \]

If one has \(\operatorname{Hom}(x,y) \neq \emptyset\), then \(Q \overset{F_1}{\wedge} P^\circ\) is a torsor under \({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1\) locally trivial on \(Y_1\); it follows that \(f_{1 *}(Q \overset{F_1}{\wedge} P^\circ)\) is a torsor under \(f_{1*}({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1)\), and that \(g^*_1 f_{1 *} (Q \overset{F_1}{\wedge} P^\circ)\) is a trivial torsor. The morphism \(a\) is then identified with the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(Y_1, g^*_1 f_{1 *}({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0 (Y'_1, f'_{1 *}({\vphantom{F}}^{P'\mkern-6mu}{F}'_1)) \text{.} \]

The same holds if one has \(\operatorname{Hom}(x',y') \neq \emptyset\) and if \(a_1\) is injective, for then \(Q' \overset{F'_1}{\wedge} {P'}^\circ\) is trivial, and it follows from the injectivity of \(a_1\) that \(P\) and \(Q\) are locally isomorphic on \(Y_1\). One concludes that, if \(a_0\) is injective (resp. if \(a_0\) is bijective and \(a_1\) injective), \(\varphi\) is faithful (resp. fully faithful).

It remains to show that, if \(a_0\) and \(a_1\) are bijective, the functor \(\varphi\) is essentially surjective. Let \(Y''\) be a scheme étale over \(Y'\), \(X'' = X' \times_{Y'} Y''\), and let \(P''\) be a torsor on \(X''\) of group \(F'' = F'|X''\). We shall show that there exists an element \(x\) of \((g^* f_* \Phi)_{Y''}\) whose image in \((f'_* \Phi')_{Y''}\) is isomorphic to \(P''\). Let \(p''\) be the class of \(P''\). From the fact that \(a_1\) is surjective it follows that one can find a surjective étale morphism \(Y''_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y''\), an étale morphism \(Y_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) such that one has a morphism \(Y''_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'_1\) and a torsor \(P_1\) on \(X_1\) of group \(F_1\) whose inverse image \(P''_1\) on \(X''_1\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of \(P''\). Using the fact that \(\varphi\) is fully faithful, one sees that the object \(x_1\) of \((g^* f_* \Phi)_{Y''_1}\) which corresponds to \(P''_1\) is endowed with a descent datum relative to \(Y''_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y''\), and hence comes from an element \(x\) of \((g^* f_* \Phi)_{Y''}\). Since the image of \(x\) in \((f'_* \Phi')_{Y''}\) is \(P''\), this proves that \(\varphi\) is essentially surjective and completes the proof.

Example 4

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a proper morphism. It follows from [2] that, for every ind-finite stack \(F\) on \(X\), the pair \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper (relative to \(Y\)) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\). In particular, for every sheaf of sets (resp. every sheaf of groups, resp. every ind-finite sheaf of groups) \(F\) on \(X\), \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 1\)).

Remarks 5

a) Let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups on \(X\) such that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\)). If one considers \(F\) as a sheaf of sets, \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\)), but the converse is false.

For example, let \(Y\) be the spectrum of a strictly local discrete valuation ring with closed point \(t\), with generic point \(s\), \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a non-empty scheme over \(Y\) whose closed fibre is empty, \(F\) a non-trivial constant sheaf of groups on \(X\) and \(P\) a torsor under \(F\) such that one has \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_s, {\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}|X_s)=1\). Then \(({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F},f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(Y\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) when one considers \({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}\) as a sheaf of sets. If one considers \({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}\) as a sheaf of groups, one has an isomorphism \({\vphantom{F}}^{P^\circ\mkern-7mu}({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}) \simeq F\); since the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(X,F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(X_t,F|X_t)=1 \]

is not injective, this proves that \(({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F},f)\) is not cohomologically proper relative to \(Y\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\).

b) Suppose \(f\) coherent (i.e. quasi-compact and quasi-separated). Let \(F\) be a stack on \(X\). For every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y'\), one denotes by \(\overline{Y}\) (resp. \(\overline{Y'}\)) the strict localization of \(Y\) (resp. \(Y'\)) at \(\overline{y}\), and one sets \(\overline{X} = X \times_Y \overline{Y}\), \(\overline{X'} = X' \times_{Y'} \overline{Y'}\), etc. In order that \((F,f)\) be cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)), it is necessary and sufficient that, for every \(S\)-scheme \(S'\) and for every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y'\), the canonical functor

\[ \overline{F}(\overline{X})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{F'}(\overline{X'}) \]

be faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence).

Indeed, if \(S'\) is an \(S\)-scheme, in order that the functor

\[ g^* f_* F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_* F' \]

be faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for the functor induced on the fibres at the various geometric points \(\overline{y}'\) of \(\overline{Y'}\) [2]. The assertion thus follows from the computation of the geometric fibres of the direct image of a stack by a coherent morphism [2].

c) Let \(F\) be a stack on \(X\). The fact that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)) is local on \(Y\) for the étale topology.

Let \(S'\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(F'\) the inverse image of \(F\) on \(X'\) (cf. (0.1)). If \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), the same holds for \((F', f')\). But, if \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\)), it does not necessarily hold for \((F', f')\).

For example, let \(S'\) be a discrete valuation ring, \(f' \colon E_{S'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) the affine space over \(S'\), \(x\) a closed point of \(E_{S'}\) above the generic point of \(S'\) and \(F'\) the sheaf of sets on \(E_{S'}\) whose restriction to \(E_{S'}-\{x\}\) is the constant sheaf with one element and whose fibre at a geometric point above \(x\) has two elements. Then \((F',f')\) is not cohomologically proper relative to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\). Let \(S = S'[Z]\), \(f \colon E_S \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) the affine space over \(S\) and \(T\) a closed part of \(X = E_S\) which does not meet the closed set \(Z=0\) and such that \(f(T)\) contains the generic point of \(S\). Let \(G\) be the inverse image of \(F'\) on \(X\) and \(F\) the sheaf on \(X\) obtained by extending \(G|X-T\) by the empty set. Then \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), but this no longer holds after the base change \(S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) defined by \(Z=0\).

d) Let \(F\) be a stack on \(X\) such that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(Y\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)). Then, for every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\), the canonical functor

\[ (f_* F)_{\overline{y}} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(X_{\overline{y}}) \]

is faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence of categories).

Proposition 6

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) and \(g \colon Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) be two \(S\)-morphisms, \(\Phi\) a stack over \(X\).

  1. 1)

    Suppose that \((\Phi,f)\) and \((f_* \Phi, g)\) are cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)). Then the same holds for \((\Phi, gf)\).

  2. 2)

    Suppose that \((\Phi, gf)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. that \((\Phi, gf)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and \((\Phi,f)\) cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), resp. that \((\Phi, gf)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\) and \((\Phi, f)\) cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\)). Then \((f_* \Phi, g)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 1\)).

For every \(S\)-scheme \(S'\), we consider the following diagram, all of whose squares are cartesian:

\[ \tag{6.1} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{{X'}\ar[r]^{f'}\ar[d]_h&{Y'}\ar[r]^{g'}\ar[d]_k&{Z'}\ar[r]\ar[d]_{m}&{S'}\ar[d]\\{X}\ar[r]^f&{Y}\ar[r]^g&{Z}\ar[r]&{\,S.}} \end{array} \]

The canonical morphism

\[ m^* (g_* f_* \Phi) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g'_* f'_* (h^* \Phi) \]

is identified with the composite of the canonical morphisms

\[ m^*(g_* f_* \Phi) \xrightarrow{i} g'_*(k^*f_* \Phi) \xrightarrow{j} g'_* f'_*(h^* \Phi). \]
  1. 1)

    The hypothesis implies that \(i\) and \(j\) are faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. equivalences); hence the same holds for \(ji\).

  2. 2)

    The hypothesis implies that \(ji\) is faithful (resp. that \(ji\) is fully faithful and \(j\) faithful, resp. that \(ji\) is an equivalence and \(j\) fully faithful); it follows that \(i\) is faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence).

Corollary 7

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) and \(g \colon Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) be two \(S\)-morphisms, and let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups over \(X\). Suppose that \((F,gf)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. that \((F,gf)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\)). Then \((f_* F, g)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 0\)).

Let us resume the notation of (6.1) and, for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\), let us denote by \(f_1\), \(F_1\) the respective inverse images of \(f\), \(F\) by the morphism \(Y_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\). Let \(\Phi\) be the stack of torsors under \(F\) and \(\Psi\) the stack of torsors under \(f_* F\). We have a canonical functor

\[ \varphi \colon \Psi \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_* \Phi \text{,} \]

obtained by associating to every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\) and to every torsor \(P\) over \(Y_1\) with group \(f_{1 *}F_1\) the torsor \(\widetilde{P}\) over \(X_1\) deduced from \(f_1^* P\) by the extension of the structural group \(f_1^* f_{1 *}F_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F_1\). The functor \(\varphi\) is fully faithful. Indeed, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are two torsors over \(Y_1\) with group \(f_{1 *} F_1\), we have a canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Isom}}_{f_{1 *}F_1}(P,Q) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_{1*}(\operatorname{\mathbf{Isom}}_{F_1}(\widetilde{P},\widetilde{Q})) \]

which is an isomorphism since this is so locally. From this we deduce that the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{Isom}_{f_{1 *}F_1}(P,Q) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Isom}_{F_1}(\widetilde{P},\widetilde{Q}) \]

is an isomorphism, hence that \(\varphi\) is fully faithful.

We have a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{g'_* k^* \Psi}\ar[r]^i\ar[d]_{g'_* k^*(\varphi)}&{m^*(g_* \Psi)}\ar[d]^{m^* g_*(\varphi)}\\{g'_* k^*(f_* \Phi)}\ar[r]^j&{m^*(g_* f_* \Phi)\text{,}}} \]

where \(i\) and \(j\) are the base-change morphisms. It follows from 6 2) that \(j\) is faithful (resp. fully faithful). Since \(g'_* k^* (\varphi)\) and \(m^* g_* (\varphi)\) are fully faithful, we deduce from the diagram above that \(i\) is faithful (resp. fully faithful).

Corollary 8

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a coherent \(S\)-morphism, \(g \colon Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) a proper \(S\)-morphism, \(\Phi\) an ind-finite stack over \(X\) [2]. Suppose that \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)), then the same holds for \((\Phi, gf)\).

Since \(f\) is coherent, \(f_* \Phi\) is an ind-finite stack (SGA 4 IX 1.6 (ii)). The corollary therefore follows from 6 1) and 4.

Corollary 9

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be an integral \(S\)-morphism, \(g \colon Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) an \(S\)-morphism. If \(F\) is a sheaf of sets over \(X\), then in order that \((f_* F, g)\) be cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\)), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for \((F, gf)\). If \(F\) is a sheaf of groups over \(X\), then in order that \((f_* F, g)\) be cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for \((F, gf)\).

The assertion concerning the case of a sheaf of sets follows from 6 and from the fact that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). Let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups over \(X\) and \(\Phi\) the stack of torsors under \(F\). By SGA 4 VIII 5.8, every torsor under \(F\) is locally trivial over \(Y\). It follows that the stack \(f_* \Phi\) is equivalent to the stack of torsors under \(f_* F\), the equivalence being obtained by associating to every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\) and to every torsor \(P\) over \(X_1 = X \times_Y Y_1\) with group \(F|X_1\) the torsor \(f_* P\) with group \(f_* F | Y_1\). Since \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), the corollary therefore follows from 6.

.5cm

Definitions 10

Let \(E\) be a category and consider a diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=.6cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]^p&{\Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[r]^{p_1}\ar@<-2pt>[r]_{p_2}&{\Phi_2}} \text{,} \]

where \(\Phi\), \(\Phi_1\), \(\Phi_2\) are fibred categories over \(E\) and the arrows are morphisms of fibred categories, and let

\[ a \colon p_1 p \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_2 p \]

be an isomorphism of functors.

One says that the above diagram is exact if the following condition is satisfied

a) For every pair of objects \(x\), \(y\) of \(\Phi\) and every morphism \(f \colon p(x) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} p(y)\) such that \(p_1(f) = p_2(f)\) (\(p_1p\) and \(p_2p\) being identified by means of \(a\)), there exists a unique morphism \(g \colon x \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} y\) such that \(p(g)=f\).

Consider the diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]^p&{\Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[rr]^{p_1,p_2}\ar@<-2pt>[rr]&&{\Phi_2}\ar@<4pt>[rrr]^{p_{12},p_{23},p_{31}}\ar[rrr]\ar@<-4pt>[rrr]&&&{\Phi_3}} \text{,} \]

where \(\Phi\), \(\Phi_i\), \(1 \leqslant i \leqslant 3\), are fibred categories over \(E\) and the arrows are morphisms of fibred categories. Suppose given isomorphisms of functors

\[ a \colon p_1 p \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_2 p \]
\[ a_1 \colon p_{31} p_2 \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_{12} p_1 \text{,}\quad a_2 \colon p_{12} p_2 \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_{23} p_1 \text{,}\quad a_3 \colon p_{23} p_2 \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_{31}p_1 \]

such that the following diagram is commutative:

\[ \xymatrix@C=1.2cm{{p_{23} p_1 p}\ar[r]^{\mathrm{id}.a}&{p_{23} p_2 p}\ar[r]^{a_3.\mathrm{id}}&{p_{31} p_1 p}\ar[d]^{\mathrm{id}.a}\\{p_{12} p_2 p}\ar[u]^{a_2.\mathrm{id}}&{p_{12} p_1 p}\ar[l]^{\mathrm{id}.a}&{p_{31} p_2 p}\ar[l]^{a_1.\mathrm{id}}} \text{.} \]

One identifies \(p_1 p\) and \(p_2 p\), \(p_{31} p_2\) and \(p_{12} p_1\), etc.

One says that the above diagram is exact if the following conditions are satisfied:

  1. a)

    Analogous to condition a) of 10.1.

  2. b)

    For every object \(x_1\) of \(\Phi_1\) and for every isomorphism \(u \colon p_1(x_1) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_2(x_1)\) such that

    \[ \tag{10.2.1} { p_{23}(u) p_{31}(u) = p_{12}(u)^{-1} \text{,} } \]

    there exists an object \(x\) of \(\Phi\) such that one has an isomorphism \(i \colon p(x) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} x_1\) making commutative the diagram

    \[ \tag{10.2.2} \begin{array}{c} { \xymatrix{{p_1p(x)}\ar@{=}[r]\ar[d]_{p_1(i)}&{p_2p(x)}\ar[d]^{p_2(i)}\\{p_1(x_1)}\ar[r]^{\sim}_u&{p_2(x_1)}} } \end{array} \]

One defines in the obvious way the notion of morphism of exact diagrams of fibred categories over a category \(E\).

10.4

We shall use in particular the notion of exact diagram in the case where \(E\) is a site and \(\Phi\), \(\Phi_i\), \(1 \leqslant i \leqslant 3\), are stacks over \(E\).

Let \(f \colon E \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E'\) be a morphism of sites and let

\[ \tag{10.4.1} { \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]^p&{\Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[rr]^{p_1,p_2}\ar@<-2pt>[rr]&&{\Phi_2}\ar@<4pt>[rrr]^{p_{12},p_{23},p_{31}}\ar[rrr]\ar@<-4pt>[rrr]&&&{\Phi_3}} } \]

be an exact diagram of stacks over \(E\). One obtains by direct image a diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{f_* \Phi}\ar[r]&{f_* \Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{f_* \Phi_2}\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]&{f_* \Phi_3}} \]

which is obviously exact.

If \(h \colon E'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E\) is a morphism of sites, one has likewise an exact diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{h^* \Phi}\ar[r]^{p''}&{h^* \Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[rr]^{p''_1,p''_2}\ar@<-2pt>[rr]&&{h^* \Phi_2}\ar@<4pt>[rrr]^{p''_{12},p''_{23},p''_{31}}\ar[rrr]\ar@<-4pt>[rrr]&&&{h^* \Phi_3}} \]

Let us first verify condition a) of 10.2. Let \(F''\) be an object of \(E''\), \(x''\) and \(y''\) two objects of \((h^* \Phi)_{F''}\), \(x''_1\) and \(y''_1\) their respective images in \(h^* \Phi_1\) and \(x''_2\), \(y''_2\) their images in \(h^* \Phi_2\). Let \(u''_1 \colon x''_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} y''_1\) be a morphism such that \(p''_1(u''_1) = p''_2(u''_1)\), and let us prove that \(u''_1\) comes from a unique morphism \(u'' \colon x'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} y''\). The question being local on \(F''\), one may suppose that one has an object \(F_1\) of \(E\), a morphism from \(F''\) to the inverse image \(F''_1\) of \(F_1\) by \(h\), objects \(x\), \(y\) of \(\Phi_{F_1}\) whose inverse images on \(F''\) are \(x''\) and \(y''\). Let \(x_1\), \(y_1\) (resp. \(x_2\), \(y_2\)) be the images of \(x\), \(y\) in \(\Phi_1\) (resp. \(\Phi_2\)). One may suppose that \(u''_1\) comes from a morphism \(u_1 \colon x_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} y_1\), such that \(p_1(u_1) = p_2(u_1)\). In view of the exactness of (10.4.1), one obtains a unique morphism \(u \colon x \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} y\) whose inverse image by \(h\) is the sought morphism \(u''\).

Condition b) of 10.2 is verified in an analogous manner. Let \(x''_1\) be an object of \((h^* \Phi_1)_{F''}\), \(u'' \colon p''_1(x''_1) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} p''_2(x''_1)\) a morphism satisfying the relation

\[ p''_{23}(u'')p''_{31}(u'')=p''_{12}(u'')^{-1} \text{,} \]

and let us prove that there exists an object \(x''\) of \((h^* \Phi)_{F''}\) and an isomorphism \(i'' \colon p''(x'') \simeq x''_1\) making commutative a diagram analogous to (10.2.2). Since the question is local on \(F''\), one may suppose that one has an object \(F_1\), a morphism \(F'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F''_1\) as above, and an object \(x_1\) of \((\Phi_1)_{F_1}\) whose inverse image in \((h^* \Phi_1)_{F''}\) is \(x''_1\). Likewise one may suppose that \(u''\) comes from a morphism \(u \colon p_1(x_1) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} p_2(x_1)\) satisfying (10.2.2). The existence of an object \(x\) of \(\Phi_{F_1}\), whose inverse image by \(h\) is an element \(x''\) answering the question, then results from the exactness of (10.4.1).

Examples 11

  1. 1)

    Let \(f \colon X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be a descent morphism for the category of étale sheaves on variable schemes (for example a universally submersive morphism (SGA 4 VIII 9.3)). Let \(X_2 = X_1 \times_X X_1\), let \(g \colon X_2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be the canonical projection and \(F\) a sheaf of sets on \(X\). It then follows from loc. cit. that we have an exact sequence of sheaves of sets

    \[ \tag{11.1} { \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{F}\ar[r]&{f_* f^* F}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{g_* g^* F \text{.}}} } \]

    If \(\Phi\) is the stack in discrete categories associated to \(F\) and \(\Phi_3\) the final stack on \(X\), i.e. the stack all of whose fibres are reduced to a single element having as its only morphism the identity morphism, to say that the sequence (11.1) is exact amounts to saying that this is so for the diagram of stacks

    \[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]&{f_* f^* \Phi}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{g_* g^* \Phi}\ar[r]\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]&{\Phi_3}} \text{.} \]
  2. 2)

    Let \(f \colon X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be a morphism of effective descent for the category of étale sheaves on variable schemes (for example a proper surjective morphism, or a surjective integral morphism, or a faithfully flat morphism locally of finite presentation (SGA 4 VIII 9.4)). Let \(X_2 = X_1 \times_X X_1\), let \(g \colon X_2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be the canonical projection, \(X_3 = X_1 \times_X X_1 \times_X X_1\), \(h \colon X_3 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism. Let \(\Phi\) be a stack on \(X\), \(\Phi_1 = f_* f^* \Phi\), \(\Phi_2 = g_* g^* \Phi\), \(\Phi_3 = h_* h^* \Phi\). We then have an exact diagram

    \[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]&{\Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\Phi_2}\ar[r]\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]&{\Phi_3 \text{,}}} \]

    where the arrows are the canonical morphisms associated to the projections.

    Indeed, consider \(\Phi\) as a stack on the category \(\mathbf{Sch}_X\) of schemes over \(X\), equipped with the étale topology. Then, by [2], \(\Phi\) is also a stack for the finest topology on \(\mathbf{Sch}_X\) for which the covering morphisms are the morphisms of effective descent for the category of étale sheaves. The exactness of the diagram above follows at once from this.

Proposition 12

Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(f\colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) an \(S\)-morphism.

  1. 1)

    Let

    \[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]&{\Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\Phi_2}} \]

    be an exact diagram of stacks on \(X\). Suppose that \((\Phi_1, f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and that \((\Phi_2, f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\). Then \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

  2. 2)

    Let

    \[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]&{\Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\Phi_2}\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]&{\Phi_3}} \]

    be an exact diagram of stacks on \(X\). Suppose that \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), that \((\Phi_2, f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and that \((\Phi_3, f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\). Then \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\).

For every \(S\)-scheme \(S'\), we consider the following commutative diagram, all of whose squares are cartesian:

\[ \xymatrix{{X'}\ar[r]^{f'}\ar[d]_h&{Y'}\ar[r]\ar[d]_g&{S'}\ar[d]\\{X}\ar[r]^f&{Y}\ar[r]&{S}} \]

Let us prove 2), the proof of 1) being analogous. Since the direct image and inverse image functors transform an exact diagram of stacks into an exact diagram (10.4), we have the following morphism of exact diagrams of stacks:

\[ \xymatrix{{g^* f_* \Phi}\ar[r]^\pi\ar[d]_{\varphi}&{g^* f_* \Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[r]^{\pi_1}\ar@<-2pt>[r]_{\pi_2}\ar[d]_{\varphi_1}&{g^* f_* \Phi_2}\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]\ar[d]_{\varphi_2}&{g^* f_* \Phi_3}\ar[d]_{\varphi_3}\\{f'_* h^* \Phi}\ar[r]&{f'_* h^* \Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{f'_* h^* \Phi_2}\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]&{f'_* h^* \Phi_3}} \text{.} \]

By hypothesis \(\varphi_1\) is an equivalence of categories, \(\varphi_2\) is fully faithful and \(\varphi_3\) faithful. It therefore follows from the preceding diagram that \(\varphi\) is an equivalence.

Proposition 13

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be an \(S\)-morphism.

  1. 1)

    Let

    \[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{F}\ar[r]&{G}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{H}} \]

    be an exact diagram of sheaves of sets on \(X\). Suppose that \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and that \((H,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\). Then \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

  2. 2)

    Let \(F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) be a monomorphism of sheaves of groups on \(X\). If \(Y_1\) is a scheme étale over \(Y\), we set \(X_1 = Y_1 \times_Y X\), and we denote by \(f_1\) (resp. \(F_1\), resp. \(G_1\)) the inverse image of \(f\) (resp. \(F\), resp. \(G\)) on \(Y_1\) (cf. 0.2). Suppose that \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 1\)) and that, for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\) and for every torsor \(Q\) under \(G_1\), \((Q/F_1, f_1)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 0\)). Then \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 1\)).

  3. 3)

    Let \(F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) be a monomorphism of sheaves of groups on \(X\). Suppose that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\) and that \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). Then, for every torsor \(Q\) under \(G\), \((Q/F, f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

Proof

1) Let \(\Phi\) (resp. \(\Phi_1\), resp. \(\Phi_2\)) be the stack in discrete categories associated to \(F\) (resp. \(G\), resp. \(H\)) and let \(\Phi_3\) be the final stack on \(X\). We then have an exact diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]&{\Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\Phi_2}\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]&{\Phi_3}} \text{.} \]

By hypothesis \((\Phi_1, f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\) and \((\Phi_2, f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (2). Since \((\Phi_3, f)\) is obviously cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), it follows from 12 that \((\Phi, f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), i.e. that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

2) Let us first show that, if \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and if the \((Q/F_1, f_1)\) are cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), then \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). By 3.1 it suffices to prove that, for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\) and for every torsor \(P\) on \(X_1\) with group \(F_1\), \(({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1, f_1)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) when one considers \({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1\) as a sheaf of sets, and that the canonical morphism

\[ d \colon g^*(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^1 f'_* F' \]

is injective. The first assertion follows at once from 1), for, if \(Q\) denotes the torsor deduced from \(P\) by the extension \(F_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_1\) of the structural group, one has an isomorphism \({\vphantom{G}}^{Q}{G}_1 / {\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1 \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} Q/F_1\).

Let us show that \(d\) is injective. It suffices to prove that, if \(Y_1\) is an étale scheme over \(Y\), if \(P\) and \(\widetilde{P}\) are two torsors under \(F_1\) whose inverse images \(P'\) and \(\widetilde{P}'\) on \(X'_1\) are isomorphic, then, after making a surjective étale extension of \(Y_1\), \(P\) and \(\widetilde{P}\) become isomorphic. Let us choose an isomorphism \(p' \colon P' \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \widetilde{P}'\). Let \(Q\) (resp. \(\widetilde{Q}\)) be the torsor deduced from \(P\) (resp. \(\widetilde{P}\)) by the extension of the structural group \(F_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_1\). The inverse images \(Q'\) (resp. \(\widetilde{Q}'\)) of \(Q\) (resp. \(\widetilde{Q}\)) on \(X'_1\) are deduced from \(P'\) (resp. \(\widetilde{P}'\)) by the extension of the structural group \(F'_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G'_1\); let \(q' \colon Q' \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \widetilde{Q}'\) be the isomorphism obtained in the same way from \(p'\). Since \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), one may suppose, after making a surjective étale extension of \(Y_1\), that \(q'\) is the image of an isomorphism \(q \colon Q \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \widetilde{Q}\). To the torsor \(P\) (resp. \(\widetilde{P}\)) is associated a section \(x\) of \(Q/F_1\) (resp. a section \(\widetilde{x}\) of \(\widetilde{Q}/F_1\)), and, in order that \(P\) and \(\widetilde{P}\) be isomorphic, it is necessary and sufficient that one have an isomorphism \(Q \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \widetilde{Q}\) such that the isomorphism

\[ e \colon \mathrm{H}^0(X_1, Q/F_1) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(X_1,\widetilde{Q}/F_1) \]

which it induces transforms \(x\) into \(\widetilde{x}\). One takes the isomorphism \(q\). The sections \(e(x)\) and \(\widetilde{x}\) of \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_1,\widetilde{Q}/F_1)\) have the same image in \(\mathrm{H}^0(X'_1,\widetilde{Q}'/F'_1)\). Since \((\widetilde{Q}/F_1,f_1)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), after making a surjective étale extension of \(Y_1\), one indeed has \(e(x) = \widetilde{x}\), which proves the injectivity of \(d\).

To complete the proof, it remains to prove that, if \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), and if, for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\) and every torsor \(Q\) on \(X_1\) of group \(F_1\), \((Q/F_1, f_1)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), then the morphism \(d\) is surjective. Let \(P'\) be a torsor on \(X'_1\) of group \(F'_1\), \(Q'\) the torsor under \(G'_1\) obtained from \(P'\) by extension of the structural group. The datum of \(P'\) is equivalent to that of \(Q'\) and of a section \(x'\) of \(\mathrm{H}^0(X'_1,Q'/F'_1)\). It then follows from the surjectivity of the morphism

\[ g^*(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^1 f'_* G' \]

that, after making a surjective étale extension of \(Y_1\), there exists a torsor \(Q\) under \(G_1\), whose inverse image on \(X'_1\) is isomorphic to \(Q'\). Using the fact that \((Q/F_1, f_1)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), one may likewise suppose that there exists an element \(x\) of \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_1, Q/F_1)\) whose image in \(\mathrm{H}^0(X'_1, Q'/F'_1)\) is \(x'\). The datum of \(Q\) and of \(x\) determines a torsor \(P\) under \(F_1\), whose inverse image on \(X_1\) is isomorphic to \(P'\), which proves the surjectivity of \(d\).

3) Let us show that \((Q/F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), i.e. that, for every \(S\)-scheme \(S'\), for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\), if \(x\), \(\widetilde{x}\) are two elements of \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_1,Q_1/F_1)\) whose images \(x'\), \(\widetilde{x}'\) in \(\mathrm{H}^0(X'_1, Q'_1/F'_1)\) are equal, then, after surjective extension of \(Y_1\), one has \(x = \widetilde{x}\). To \(x\) (resp. \(\widetilde{x}\)) is associated a torsor \(P\) (resp. \(\widetilde{P}\)) under \(F_1\), such that \(Q_1\) is deduced from \(P\) (resp. \(\widetilde{P}\)) by the extension \(F_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_1\) of the structural group. From the relation \(x' = \widetilde{x}'\) it follows that one has an isomorphism \(u' \colon P' \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \widetilde{P}'\) such that the isomorphism induced on \(Q'_1\) by \(u'\) is the identity. Since \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), one deduces that, after surjective étale extension of \(Y_1\), one has an isomorphism \(u \colon P \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widetilde{P}\) lifting \(u'\); the fact that \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) then entails that one has \(x = \widetilde{x}\).

Let us show that \((Q/F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). Let \(Y''\) be a scheme étale over \(Y'\) and \(x''\) an element of \(\mathrm{H}^0(X'',Q''/F'')\). To \(x''\) is associated a torsor \(P''\) on \(X''\) of group \(F''\). Since \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), one can find surjective étale morphisms \(Y''_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y''\) and \(Y_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), such that one has a morphism \(Y''_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'_1\), and a torsor \(P\) on \(X_1\) of group \(F_1\) whose inverse image on \(X''_1\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of \(P''\). It then follows from the fact that \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) that one may even choose \(Y''_1\) and \(Y_1\) such that the torsor deduced from \(P\) by extension of the structural group \(F_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_1\) is isomorphic to \(Q_1\); to \(P\) there corresponds an element \(x\) of \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_1,Q_1/F_1)\), whose image in \(\mathrm{H}^0(X''_1,Q''_1/F''_1)\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of \(x''\), which completes the proof.

Proposition 14

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(F\) a sheaf of sets or of groups on \(X\) (resp. a sheaf of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups, where \(\mathbb{L}\) is a set of prime numbers). Suppose \(F\) locally constant, \((F,f)\) cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 1\)) and \(f\) locally \(0\)-acyclic (resp. locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\)) (SGA 4 XV 1.11). Then, for every specialization \(\overline{s}_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{s}_2\) of geometric points of \(S\), the specialization morphism (SGA 4 VIII 7.1)

\[ a_0 \colon (f_* F)_{\overline{s}_2} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (f_* F)_{\overline{s}_1} \]

is an isomorphism, and, if \(F\) is a sheaf of groups, the morphism

\[ a_1 \colon (\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F)_{\overline{s}_2} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F)_{\overline{s}_1} \]

is injective (resp. the morphisms \(a_0\) and \(a_1\) are isomorphisms).

The proof is obtained by copying word for word that of SGA 4 XVI 2.3, but replacing therein the expression “proper” by the expression “cohomologically proper”.

.5cm

Corollary 15

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism, \(\Phi\) a stack on \(X\), \(\mathbb{L}\) a set of prime numbers. Suppose that, for every scheme \(X_1\) étale over \(X\) and for every pair of objects \(x\), \(y\) of \(\Phi_{X_1}\), the sheaf \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{X_1}(x,y)\) is locally constant, that the sheaf \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{X_1}(x)\) is a locally constant ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-group, and that the sheaf of maximal subgerbes \(S\Phi\) of \(\Phi\) [1] is locally constant. Suppose that \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 1\) and that \(f\) is locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\). Then, for every specialization \(\overline{s}_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{s}_2\) of geometric points of \(S\), the specialization morphism

\[ a \colon (f_* \Phi)_{\overline{s}_2} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (f_* \Phi)_{\overline{s}_1} \]

is an equivalence of categories.

Let \(\overline{S}_1\) (resp. \(\overline{S}_2\)) be the strict localization of \(S\) at \(\overline{s}_1\) (resp. the strict localization of \(S\) at \(\overline{s}_2\)), \(\overline{X}_2\), \(\overline{\Phi}_2\) (resp. \(\overline{X}_1\), \(\overline{\Phi}_1\)) the inverse images of \(X_2\), \(\Phi_2\) on \(\overline{S}_2\) (resp. of \(X_1\), \(\Phi_1\) on \(\overline{S}_1\)) and let us consider the cartesian square

\[ \xymatrix{{\overline{X}_1}\ar[r]^h\ar[d]_{\overline{f}_1}&{\overline{X}_2}\ar[d]^{\overline{f}_2}\\{\overline{S}_1}\ar[r]^g&{\overline{S}_2}} \text{.} \]

We must show that the functor

\[ \varphi \colon \overline{\Phi}_2(\overline{X}_2) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{\Phi}_1 (\overline{X}_1) \]

is an equivalence. The functor \(\varphi\) is fully faithful; let

indeed \(x\), \(y\) be two objects of \((\overline{\Phi}_2)_{\overline{X}_2}\); the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\overline{X}_2}(x,y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\overline{X}_1}(\varphi(x),\varphi(y)) \]

is identified with the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X}_2, \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\overline{X}_2}(x,y)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X}_1,h^*(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\overline{X}_2}(x,y)) \text{.} \]

This morphism is an isomorphism by 14.

Let us show that \(\varphi\) is an equivalence. Let \(x_1\) be an object of \(\overline{\Phi}_1(\overline{X}_1)\) and \(G_1\) the maximal subgerbe of \(\overline{\Phi}_1\) generated by \(x_1\). The morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X}_2, S\overline{\Phi}_2) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X}_1, h^*(S \overline{\Phi}_2)) = \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X}_1,S\overline{\Phi}_1) \]

is bijective, and there exists therefore a maximal subgerbe \(G_2\) of \(\overline{\Phi}_2\) such that \(h^* G_2\) is isomorphic to \(G_1\). It then suffices to prove that the functor

\[ G_2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} h_* h^* G_2 \]

is an equivalence. But, under this form, the question is local for the étale topology on \(\overline{X}_2\). One may therefore suppose that \(G_2\) is a gerbe of torsors under the group of automorphisms of an object of \(G_2\), a case in which the assertion follows from 14.

Corollary 16

The hypotheses are those of 14. If one supposes moreover that \(F\) is a sheaf of sets (resp. of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups) and that \(f_* F\) (resp. \(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F\)) is constructible, then \(f_* F\) (resp. \(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F\)) is locally constant.

The corollary follows from 14 thanks to SGA 4 IX 2.11.

Remark 17

Let us recall that the condition \(f\) locally \(0\)-acyclic is satisfied if \(f\) is flat with separable fibres, \(X\) and \(Y\) locally Noetherian (SGA 4 XV 4.1), and that the condition \(f\) locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\) is satisfied if \(f\) is smooth, \(\mathbb{L}\) being the set of prime numbers distinct from the residue characteristics of \(S\) (SGA 4 XV 2.1).

blocks.html: 1 Cohomological properness

Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a morphism of \(S\)-schemes. If \(S'\) is an \(S\)-scheme, one considers the following diagram, in which all the squares are cartesian:
\[ \tag{0.1} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{{X}\ar[d]_f&{X'}\ar[l]_h\ar[d]^{f'}\\{Y}\ar[d]&{Y'}\ar[l]_g\ar[d]\\{S}&{\,S'.}\ar[l]} \end{array} \]
If \(Y_1\) is a scheme étale over \(Y\), one sets \(X_1 = X \times_Y Y_1\), \(Y_1' = Y' \times_Y Y_1\), and one considers the cartesian square
\[ \tag{0.2} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{{X_1}\ar[d]_{f_1}&{X_1'}\ar[l]_{h_1}\ar[d]^{f_1'}\\{Y_1}&{\,Y_1'.}\ar[l]^{g_1}} \end{array} \]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1

Let \(F\) be a stack over \(X\). One says that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 1\)) if, for every \(S\)-scheme \(S'\), the canonical functor (defined in the obvious way by the universal property of the inverse image of stacks):

\[ g^* f_* F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_* h^* F \qquad\text{ (cf. 1.0.1)} \]

is faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence of categories).
If there is no possible confusion regarding \(S\), in particular if \(S=Y\), one says cohomologically proper instead of cohomologically proper relative to \(S\).

Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets on \(X\); let \(\Phi\) be the stack in discrete categories associated with \(F\), i.e. the stack whose fibre over any scheme \(X_1\) étale over \(X\) is the discrete category having \(F(X_1)\) as its set of objects. One says that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 0\)) if \((\Phi, f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 1\)).
The canonical morphism
\[ \tag{2.1} { g^* f_* F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_* h^* F } \]
gives, by passage to the associated stacks in discrete categories, the canonical morphism
\[ g^* f_* \Phi \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_* h^* \Phi \text{.} \]

Consequently, to say that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 0\)) amounts to saying that, for every \(S\)-scheme \(S'\), the morphism (2.1) is injective (resp. bijective).

Let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups on \(X\) and \(\Phi\) the stack of torsors on \(X\) with group \(F\) [1]. One says that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)) if \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)). The condition of cohomological properness can be made explicit as follows.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.1

The notations are those of (0.1) and (0.2). Let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups on \(X\). One denotes by \(F'\) (resp. \(F_1\), resp. \(F_1'\), etc.) the inverse image of \(F\) on \(X'\) (resp. on \(X_1\), resp. on \(X_1'\), etc.). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)).

  2. (ii)

    For every morphism \(S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\), and for every torsor \(P\) on \(X_1\) with group \(F_1\), if \({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1\) denotes the group twisted from \(F_1\) by \(P\) [1], the canonical morphism

    \[ a_0 \colon g_1^*(f_{1 *}({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_{1 *}({\vphantom{F}}^{P'\mkern-6mu}{F}'_1) \]

    is injective (resp. \(a_0\) is bijective and the canonical morphism

    \[ a_1 \colon g^*(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^1 f'_* F' \]

    is injective, resp. \(a_0\) and \(a_1\) are bijective).

  3. (ii bis)

    For every morphism \(S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\), for every torsor \(P\) on \(X_1\) with group \(F_1\), and for every torsor \(R\) under \({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1\), the canonical morphism

    \[ \alpha_0 \colon g_1^*(f_{1 *}R) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_{1 *} R' \]

    is injective (resp. \(\alpha_0\) is bijective, resp. the morphisms \(\alpha_0\) and

    \[ \alpha_1 \colon g_1^*(\mathrm{R}^1 f_{1 *}({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^1 f'_{1 *}({\vphantom{F}}^{P'\mkern-6mu}{F}'_1) \]

    are bijective).
Proof

(i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii bis). According to [1] every torsor \(R\) with group \({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1\) is of the form \(R = Q \overset{F_1}{\wedge} P^\circ\), where \(Q\) is a torsor with group \(F_1\) and \(P^\circ\) the opposite of \(P\). One then has \(R' \simeq Q' \overset{F'_1}{\wedge} {P'}^\circ\). Let \(\Phi\) be the stack of torsors under \(F\) and let \(x\), \(y\) (resp. \(x'\), \(y'\)) be the objects of the fibre category \((g^* f_* \Phi)_{Y_1'}\) (resp. \((f'_* \Phi')_{Y_1'}\)) associated with \(P\), \(Q\) (resp. \(P'\), \(Q'\)). One has the relation
\[ Q\overset{F_1}{\wedge} P^\circ \simeq \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{F_1}(P,Q) \text{,} \]
and it follows that one has canonical isomorphisms

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{Y'_1}(x,y) \simeq g_1^* f_{1 *}(Q \overset{F_1}{\wedge} P^\circ) \text{,}\quad \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{Y_1'}(x',y') \simeq f'_{1 *}(Q' \overset{F'_1}{\wedge}{P'}^\circ)\text{.} \]
Consequently the morphism \(\alpha_0\) is identified with the morphism
\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{Y'_1}(x,y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{Y'_1}(x',y') \text{,} \]

whence it follows that, if \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), \(\alpha_0\) is injective and that, if \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), \(\alpha_0\) is bijective.
Suppose now that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), i.e. that the canonical morphism
\[ \varphi \colon g^* f_* \Phi \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_* \Phi' \]

is an equivalence. Let \(G\) be the sheaf of maximal sub-gerbes of the stack \(f_* \Phi\) [1]; one then has an isomorphism \(G \simeq \mathrm{R}^1 f_* F\). Since \(g^* G\) is the sheaf of maximal sub-gerbes of \(g^* f_* \Phi\) [2], the morphism \(\alpha_1\) is obtained from \(\varphi|Y'_1\) by taking the sheaves of maximal sub-gerbes, and hence is an isomorphism.

(ii bis)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii). It suffices to show that, if the morphisms \(\alpha_0\) are bijective, then the morphisms \(a_1\) are injective. Let \(Y'_1\) be a scheme étale over \(Y'\), \(s\) and \(t\) two elements of \(g^*(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F)(Y'_1)\) having the same image in \(\mathrm{R}^1 f'_* F'(Y'_1)\), and let us show that one has \(s=t\). The assertion is local for the étale topology of \(Y'_1\) and, taking into account the definition of the inverse image \(g^*(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F)\), one may suppose that \(Y'_1\) is the inverse image of a scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\) and that \(s\) and \(t\) come from torsors \(P\) and \(Q\) on \(X_1\). The hypothesis made on \(s\) and \(t\) then means that the inverse images \(P'\) and \(Q'\) of \(P\) and \(Q\) on \(X'_1\) are isomorphic locally for the étale topology of \(Y'_1\). If one sets \(R = \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{F_1}(P,Q)\), the fact that the morphism
\[ g_1^* f_{1 *} R \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_{1 *} R' \]
is bijective proves that \(P\) and \(Q\) are isomorphic locally for the étale topology of \(Y_1\), and hence that one has \(s=t\).

(ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i). To prove that \(\varphi\) is faithful (resp. fully faithful), it suffices to show that, if \(Y_1\) is a scheme étale over \(Y\), if \(P\), \(Q\) are two torsors on \(X_1\) with group \(F_1\), if \(x\), \(y\) (resp. \(x'\), \(y'\)) are the objects of \((g^* f_* \Phi)_{Y'_1}\) (resp. \((f'_* \Phi')_{Y'_1}\)) associated with \(P\), \(Q\) (resp. \(P'\), \(Q'\)), the morphism
\[ a \colon \operatorname{Hom}(x,y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(x',y') \]
is injective (resp. bijective). Now \(a\) is identified with the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(Y'_1,g^*_1 f_{1 *} (Q \overset{F_1}{\wedge} P^\circ)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(Y'_1, f'_{1 *}(Q' \overset{F'_1}{\wedge} {P'}^\circ))\text{.} \]

If one has \(\operatorname{Hom}(x,y) \neq \emptyset\), then \(Q \overset{F_1}{\wedge} P^\circ\) is a torsor under \({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1\) locally trivial on \(Y_1\); it follows that \(f_{1 *}(Q \overset{F_1}{\wedge} P^\circ)\) is a torsor under \(f_{1*}({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1)\), and that \(g^*_1 f_{1 *} (Q \overset{F_1}{\wedge} P^\circ)\) is a trivial torsor. The morphism \(a\) is then identified with the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(Y_1, g^*_1 f_{1 *}({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0 (Y'_1, f'_{1 *}({\vphantom{F}}^{P'\mkern-6mu}{F}'_1)) \text{.} \]

The same holds if one has \(\operatorname{Hom}(x',y') \neq \emptyset\) and if \(a_1\) is injective, for then \(Q' \overset{F'_1}{\wedge} {P'}^\circ\) is trivial, and it follows from the injectivity of \(a_1\) that \(P\) and \(Q\) are locally isomorphic on \(Y_1\). One concludes that, if \(a_0\) is injective (resp. if \(a_0\) is bijective and \(a_1\) injective), \(\varphi\) is faithful (resp. fully faithful).

It remains to show that, if \(a_0\) and \(a_1\) are bijective, the functor \(\varphi\) is essentially surjective. Let \(Y''\) be a scheme étale over \(Y'\), \(X'' = X' \times_{Y'} Y''\), and let \(P''\) be a torsor on \(X''\) of group \(F'' = F'|X''\). We shall show that there exists an element \(x\) of \((g^* f_* \Phi)_{Y''}\) whose image in \((f'_* \Phi')_{Y''}\) is isomorphic to \(P''\). Let \(p''\) be the class of \(P''\). From the fact that \(a_1\) is surjective it follows that one can find a surjective étale morphism \(Y''_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y''\), an étale morphism \(Y_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) such that one has a morphism \(Y''_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'_1\) and a torsor \(P_1\) on \(X_1\) of group \(F_1\) whose inverse image \(P''_1\) on \(X''_1\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of \(P''\). Using the fact that \(\varphi\) is fully faithful, one sees that the object \(x_1\) of \((g^* f_* \Phi)_{Y''_1}\) which corresponds to \(P''_1\) is endowed with a descent datum relative to \(Y''_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y''\), and hence comes from an element \(x\) of \((g^* f_* \Phi)_{Y''}\). Since the image of \(x\) in \((f'_* \Phi')_{Y''}\) is \(P''\), this proves that \(\varphi\) is essentially surjective and completes the proof.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 4

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a proper morphism. It follows from [2] that, for every ind-finite stack \(F\) on \(X\), the pair \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper (relative to \(Y\)) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\). In particular, for every sheaf of sets (resp. every sheaf of groups, resp. every ind-finite sheaf of groups) \(F\) on \(X\), \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 1\)).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 5

a) Let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups on \(X\) such that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\)). If one considers \(F\) as a sheaf of sets, \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\)), but the converse is false.

For example, let \(Y\) be the spectrum of a strictly local discrete valuation ring with closed point \(t\), with generic point \(s\), \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a non-empty scheme over \(Y\) whose closed fibre is empty, \(F\) a non-trivial constant sheaf of groups on \(X\) and \(P\) a torsor under \(F\) such that one has \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_s, {\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}|X_s)=1\). Then \(({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F},f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(Y\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) when one considers \({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}\) as a sheaf of sets. If one considers \({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}\) as a sheaf of groups, one has an isomorphism \({\vphantom{F}}^{P^\circ\mkern-7mu}({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}) \simeq F\); since the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(X,F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(X_t,F|X_t)=1 \]

is not injective, this proves that \(({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F},f)\) is not cohomologically proper relative to \(Y\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\).

b) Suppose \(f\) coherent (i.e. quasi-compact and quasi-separated). Let \(F\) be a stack on \(X\). For every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y'\), one denotes by \(\overline{Y}\) (resp. \(\overline{Y'}\)) the strict localization of \(Y\) (resp. \(Y'\)) at \(\overline{y}\), and one sets \(\overline{X} = X \times_Y \overline{Y}\), \(\overline{X'} = X' \times_{Y'} \overline{Y'}\), etc. In order that \((F,f)\) be cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)), it is necessary and sufficient that, for every \(S\)-scheme \(S'\) and for every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y'\), the canonical functor

\[ \overline{F}(\overline{X})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{F'}(\overline{X'}) \]

be faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence).

Indeed, if \(S'\) is an \(S\)-scheme, in order that the functor

\[ g^* f_* F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f'_* F' \]

be faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for the functor induced on the fibres at the various geometric points \(\overline{y}'\) of \(\overline{Y'}\) [2]. The assertion thus follows from the computation of the geometric fibres of the direct image of a stack by a coherent morphism [2].

c) Let \(F\) be a stack on \(X\). The fact that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)) is local on \(Y\) for the étale topology.

Let \(S'\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(F'\) the inverse image of \(F\) on \(X'\) (cf. (0.1)). If \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), the same holds for \((F', f')\). But, if \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\)), it does not necessarily hold for \((F', f')\).

For example, let \(S'\) be a discrete valuation ring, \(f' \colon E_{S'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) the affine space over \(S'\), \(x\) a closed point of \(E_{S'}\) above the generic point of \(S'\) and \(F'\) the sheaf of sets on \(E_{S'}\) whose restriction to \(E_{S'}-\{x\}\) is the constant sheaf with one element and whose fibre at a geometric point above \(x\) has two elements. Then \((F',f')\) is not cohomologically proper relative to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\). Let \(S = S'[Z]\), \(f \colon E_S \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) the affine space over \(S\) and \(T\) a closed part of \(X = E_S\) which does not meet the closed set \(Z=0\) and such that \(f(T)\) contains the generic point of \(S\). Let \(G\) be the inverse image of \(F'\) on \(X\) and \(F\) the sheaf on \(X\) obtained by extending \(G|X-T\) by the empty set. Then \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), but this no longer holds after the base change \(S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) defined by \(Z=0\).

d) Let \(F\) be a stack on \(X\) such that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(Y\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)). Then, for every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\), the canonical functor

\[ (f_* F)_{\overline{y}} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(X_{\overline{y}}) \]

is faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence of categories).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) and \(g \colon Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) be two \(S\)-morphisms, \(\Phi\) a stack over \(X\).

  1. 1)

    Suppose that \((\Phi,f)\) and \((f_* \Phi, g)\) are cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)). Then the same holds for \((\Phi, gf)\).

  2. 2)

    Suppose that \((\Phi, gf)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. that \((\Phi, gf)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and \((\Phi,f)\) cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), resp. that \((\Phi, gf)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\) and \((\Phi, f)\) cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\)). Then \((f_* \Phi, g)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 1\)).
For every \(S\)-scheme \(S'\), we consider the following diagram, all of whose squares are cartesian:
\[ \tag{6.1} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{{X'}\ar[r]^{f'}\ar[d]_h&{Y'}\ar[r]^{g'}\ar[d]_k&{Z'}\ar[r]\ar[d]_{m}&{S'}\ar[d]\\{X}\ar[r]^f&{Y}\ar[r]^g&{Z}\ar[r]&{\,S.}} \end{array} \]
The canonical morphism
\[ m^* (g_* f_* \Phi) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g'_* f'_* (h^* \Phi) \]
is identified with the composite of the canonical morphisms
\[ m^*(g_* f_* \Phi) \xrightarrow{i} g'_*(k^*f_* \Phi) \xrightarrow{j} g'_* f'_*(h^* \Phi). \]

1. 1)

    The hypothesis implies that \(i\) and \(j\) are faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. equivalences); hence the same holds for \(ji\).

  2. 2)

    The hypothesis implies that \(ji\) is faithful (resp. that \(ji\) is fully faithful and \(j\) faithful, resp. that \(ji\) is an equivalence and \(j\) fully faithful); it follows that \(i\) is faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) and \(g \colon Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) be two \(S\)-morphisms, and let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups over \(X\). Suppose that \((F,gf)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. that \((F,gf)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\)). Then \((f_* F, g)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 0\)).

Let us resume the notation of (6.1) and, for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\), let us denote by \(f_1\), \(F_1\) the respective inverse images of \(f\), \(F\) by the morphism \(Y_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\). Let \(\Phi\) be the stack of torsors under \(F\) and \(\Psi\) the stack of torsors under \(f_* F\). We have a canonical functor
\[ \varphi \colon \Psi \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_* \Phi \text{,} \]

obtained by associating to every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\) and to every torsor \(P\) over \(Y_1\) with group \(f_{1 *}F_1\) the torsor \(\widetilde{P}\) over \(X_1\) deduced from \(f_1^* P\) by the extension of the structural group \(f_1^* f_{1 *}F_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F_1\). The functor \(\varphi\) is fully faithful. Indeed, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are two torsors over \(Y_1\) with group \(f_{1 *} F_1\), we have a canonical morphism
\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Isom}}_{f_{1 *}F_1}(P,Q) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_{1*}(\operatorname{\mathbf{Isom}}_{F_1}(\widetilde{P},\widetilde{Q})) \]
which is an isomorphism since this is so locally. From this we deduce that the canonical morphism
\[ \operatorname{Isom}_{f_{1 *}F_1}(P,Q) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Isom}_{F_1}(\widetilde{P},\widetilde{Q}) \]
is an isomorphism, hence that \(\varphi\) is fully faithful.
We have a commutative diagram
\[ \xymatrix{{g'_* k^* \Psi}\ar[r]^i\ar[d]_{g'_* k^*(\varphi)}&{m^*(g_* \Psi)}\ar[d]^{m^* g_*(\varphi)}\\{g'_* k^*(f_* \Phi)}\ar[r]^j&{m^*(g_* f_* \Phi)\text{,}}} \]

where \(i\) and \(j\) are the base-change morphisms. It follows from 6 2) that \(j\) is faithful (resp. fully faithful). Since \(g'_* k^* (\varphi)\) and \(m^* g_* (\varphi)\) are fully faithful, we deduce from the diagram above that \(i\) is faithful (resp. fully faithful).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a coherent \(S\)-morphism, \(g \colon Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) a proper \(S\)-morphism, \(\Phi\) an ind-finite stack over \(X\) [2]. Suppose that \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)), then the same holds for \((\Phi, gf)\).
Since \(f\) is coherent, \(f_* \Phi\) is an ind-finite stack (SGA 4 IX 1.6 (ii)). The corollary therefore follows from 6 1) and 4.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be an integral \(S\)-morphism, \(g \colon Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) an \(S\)-morphism.
