If \(F\) is a sheaf of sets over \(X\), then in order that \((f_* F, g)\) be cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\)), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for \((F, gf)\). If \(F\) is a sheaf of groups over \(X\), then in order that \((f_* F, g)\) be cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. \(\leqslant 0\), resp. \(\leqslant 1\)), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for \((F, gf)\).

The assertion concerning the case of a sheaf of sets follows from 6 and from the fact that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). Let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups over \(X\) and \(\Phi\) the stack of torsors under \(F\). By SGA 4 VIII 5.8, every torsor under \(F\) is locally trivial over \(Y\). It follows that the stack \(f_* \Phi\) is equivalent to the stack of torsors under \(f_* F\), the equivalence being obtained by associating to every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\) and to every torsor \(P\) over \(X_1 = X \times_Y Y_1\) with group \(F|X_1\) the torsor \(f_* P\) with group \(f_* F | Y_1\). Since \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), the corollary therefore follows from 6.

blocks.title: Definitions
blocks.html:
Definitions 10

Let \(E\) be a category and consider a diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=.6cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]^p&{\Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[r]^{p_1}\ar@<-2pt>[r]_{p_2}&{\Phi_2}} \text{,} \]

where \(\Phi\), \(\Phi_1\), \(\Phi_2\) are fibred categories over \(E\) and the arrows are morphisms of fibred categories, and let

\[ a \colon p_1 p \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_2 p \]

be an isomorphism of functors.

One says that the above diagram is exact if the following condition is satisfied

a) For every pair of objects \(x\), \(y\) of \(\Phi\) and every morphism \(f \colon p(x) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} p(y)\) such that \(p_1(f) = p_2(f)\) (\(p_1p\) and \(p_2p\) being identified by means of \(a\)), there exists a unique morphism \(g \colon x \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} y\) such that \(p(g)=f\).

Consider the diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]^p&{\Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[rr]^{p_1,p_2}\ar@<-2pt>[rr]&&{\Phi_2}\ar@<4pt>[rrr]^{p_{12},p_{23},p_{31}}\ar[rrr]\ar@<-4pt>[rrr]&&&{\Phi_3}} \text{,} \]

where \(\Phi\), \(\Phi_i\), \(1 \leqslant i \leqslant 3\), are fibred categories over \(E\) and the arrows are morphisms of fibred categories. Suppose given isomorphisms of functors

\[ a \colon p_1 p \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_2 p \]
\[ a_1 \colon p_{31} p_2 \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_{12} p_1 \text{,}\quad a_2 \colon p_{12} p_2 \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_{23} p_1 \text{,}\quad a_3 \colon p_{23} p_2 \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_{31}p_1 \]

such that the following diagram is commutative:

\[ \xymatrix@C=1.2cm{{p_{23} p_1 p}\ar[r]^{\mathrm{id}.a}&{p_{23} p_2 p}\ar[r]^{a_3.\mathrm{id}}&{p_{31} p_1 p}\ar[d]^{\mathrm{id}.a}\\{p_{12} p_2 p}\ar[u]^{a_2.\mathrm{id}}&{p_{12} p_1 p}\ar[l]^{\mathrm{id}.a}&{p_{31} p_2 p}\ar[l]^{a_1.\mathrm{id}}} \text{.} \]

One identifies \(p_1 p\) and \(p_2 p\), \(p_{31} p_2\) and \(p_{12} p_1\), etc.

One says that the above diagram is exact if the following conditions are satisfied:

  1. a)

    Analogous to condition a) of 10.1.

  2. b)

    For every object \(x_1\) of \(\Phi_1\) and for every isomorphism \(u \colon p_1(x_1) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_2(x_1)\) such that

    \[ \tag{10.2.1} { p_{23}(u) p_{31}(u) = p_{12}(u)^{-1} \text{,} } \]

    there exists an object \(x\) of \(\Phi\) such that one has an isomorphism \(i \colon p(x) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} x_1\) making commutative the diagram

    \[ \tag{10.2.2} \begin{array}{c} { \xymatrix{{p_1p(x)}\ar@{=}[r]\ar[d]_{p_1(i)}&{p_2p(x)}\ar[d]^{p_2(i)}\\{p_1(x_1)}\ar[r]^{\sim}_u&{p_2(x_1)}} } \end{array} \]

One defines in the obvious way the notion of morphism of exact diagrams of fibred categories over a category \(E\).
We shall use in particular the notion of exact diagram in the case where \(E\) is a site and \(\Phi\), \(\Phi_i\), \(1 \leqslant i \leqslant 3\), are stacks over \(E\).
Let \(f \colon E \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E'\) be a morphism of sites and let
\[ \tag{10.4.1} { \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]^p&{\Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[rr]^{p_1,p_2}\ar@<-2pt>[rr]&&{\Phi_2}\ar@<4pt>[rrr]^{p_{12},p_{23},p_{31}}\ar[rrr]\ar@<-4pt>[rrr]&&&{\Phi_3}} } \]
be an exact diagram of stacks over \(E\). One obtains by direct image a diagram
\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{f_* \Phi}\ar[r]&{f_* \Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{f_* \Phi_2}\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]&{f_* \Phi_3}} \]
which is obviously exact.
If \(h \colon E'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E\) is a morphism of sites, one has likewise an exact diagram
\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{h^* \Phi}\ar[r]^{p''}&{h^* \Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[rr]^{p''_1,p''_2}\ar@<-2pt>[rr]&&{h^* \Phi_2}\ar@<4pt>[rrr]^{p''_{12},p''_{23},p''_{31}}\ar[rrr]\ar@<-4pt>[rrr]&&&{h^* \Phi_3}} \]

Let us first verify condition a) of 10.2. Let \(F''\) be an object of \(E''\), \(x''\) and \(y''\) two objects of \((h^* \Phi)_{F''}\), \(x''_1\) and \(y''_1\) their respective images in \(h^* \Phi_1\) and \(x''_2\), \(y''_2\) their images in \(h^* \Phi_2\). Let \(u''_1 \colon x''_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} y''_1\) be a morphism such that \(p''_1(u''_1) = p''_2(u''_1)\), and let us prove that \(u''_1\) comes from a unique morphism \(u'' \colon x'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} y''\). The question being local on \(F''\), one may suppose that one has an object \(F_1\) of \(E\), a morphism from \(F''\) to the inverse image \(F''_1\) of \(F_1\) by \(h\), objects \(x\), \(y\) of \(\Phi_{F_1}\) whose inverse images on \(F''\) are \(x''\) and \(y''\). Let \(x_1\), \(y_1\) (resp. \(x_2\), \(y_2\)) be the images of \(x\), \(y\) in \(\Phi_1\) (resp. \(\Phi_2\)). One may suppose that \(u''_1\) comes from a morphism \(u_1 \colon x_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} y_1\), such that \(p_1(u_1) = p_2(u_1)\). In view of the exactness of (10.4.1), one obtains a unique morphism \(u \colon x \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} y\) whose inverse image by \(h\) is the sought morphism \(u''\).

Condition b) of 10.2 is verified in an analogous manner. Let \(x''_1\) be an object of \((h^* \Phi_1)_{F''}\), \(u'' \colon p''_1(x''_1) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} p''_2(x''_1)\) a morphism satisfying the relation

and let us prove that there exists an object \(x''\) of \((h^* \Phi)_{F''}\) and an isomorphism \(i'' \colon p''(x'') \simeq x''_1\) making commutative a diagram analogous to (10.2.2). Since the question is local on \(F''\), one may suppose that one has an object \(F_1\), a morphism \(F'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F''_1\) as above, and an object \(x_1\) of \((\Phi_1)_{F_1}\) whose inverse image in \((h^* \Phi_1)_{F''}\) is \(x''_1\). Likewise one may suppose that \(u''\) comes from a morphism \(u \colon p_1(x_1) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} p_2(x_1)\) satisfying (10.2.2). The existence of an object \(x\) of \(\Phi_{F_1}\), whose inverse image by \(h\) is an element \(x''\) answering the question, then results from the exactness of (10.4.1).

blocks.title: Examples
blocks.html:
Examples 11

  1. 1)

    Let \(f \colon X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be a descent morphism for the category of étale sheaves on variable schemes (for example a universally submersive morphism (SGA 4 VIII 9.3)). Let \(X_2 = X_1 \times_X X_1\), let \(g \colon X_2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be the canonical projection and \(F\) a sheaf of sets on \(X\). It then follows from loc. cit. that we have an exact sequence of sheaves of sets

    \[ \tag{11.1} { \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{F}\ar[r]&{f_* f^* F}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{g_* g^* F \text{.}}} } \]

    If \(\Phi\) is the stack in discrete categories associated to \(F\) and \(\Phi_3\) the final stack on \(X\), i.e. the stack all of whose fibres are reduced to a single element having as its only morphism the identity morphism, to say that the sequence (11.1) is exact amounts to saying that this is so for the diagram of stacks

    \[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]&{f_* f^* \Phi}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{g_* g^* \Phi}\ar[r]\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]&{\Phi_3}} \text{.} \]
  2. 2)

    Let \(f \colon X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be a morphism of effective descent for the category of étale sheaves on variable schemes (for example a proper surjective morphism, or a surjective integral morphism, or a faithfully flat morphism locally of finite presentation (SGA 4 VIII 9.4)). Let \(X_2 = X_1 \times_X X_1\), let \(g \colon X_2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be the canonical projection, \(X_3 = X_1 \times_X X_1 \times_X X_1\), \(h \colon X_3 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism. Let \(\Phi\) be a stack on \(X\), \(\Phi_1 = f_* f^* \Phi\), \(\Phi_2 = g_* g^* \Phi\), \(\Phi_3 = h_* h^* \Phi\). We then have an exact diagram

    \[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]&{\Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\Phi_2}\ar[r]\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]&{\Phi_3 \text{,}}} \]

    where the arrows are the canonical morphisms associated to the projections.

    Indeed, consider \(\Phi\) as a stack on the category \(\mathbf{Sch}_X\) of schemes over \(X\), equipped with the étale topology. Then, by [2], \(\Phi\) is also a stack for the finest topology on \(\mathbf{Sch}_X\) for which the covering morphisms are the morphisms of effective descent for the category of étale sheaves. The exactness of the diagram above follows at once from this.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 12

Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(f\colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) an \(S\)-morphism.

  1. 1)

    Let

    \[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]&{\Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\Phi_2}} \]

    be an exact diagram of stacks on \(X\). Suppose that \((\Phi_1, f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and that \((\Phi_2, f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\). Then \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

  2. 2)

    Let

    \[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]&{\Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\Phi_2}\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]&{\Phi_3}} \]

    be an exact diagram of stacks on \(X\). Suppose that \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), that \((\Phi_2, f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and that \((\Phi_3, f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\). Then \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\).
For every \(S\)-scheme \(S'\), we consider the following commutative diagram, all of whose squares are cartesian:
\[ \xymatrix{{X'}\ar[r]^{f'}\ar[d]_h&{Y'}\ar[r]\ar[d]_g&{S'}\ar[d]\\{X}\ar[r]^f&{Y}\ar[r]&{S}} \]

Let us prove 2), the proof of 1) being analogous. Since the direct image and inverse image functors transform an exact diagram of stacks into an exact diagram (10.4), we have the following morphism of exact diagrams of stacks:

\[ \xymatrix{{g^* f_* \Phi}\ar[r]^\pi\ar[d]_{\varphi}&{g^* f_* \Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[r]^{\pi_1}\ar@<-2pt>[r]_{\pi_2}\ar[d]_{\varphi_1}&{g^* f_* \Phi_2}\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]\ar[d]_{\varphi_2}&{g^* f_* \Phi_3}\ar[d]_{\varphi_3}\\{f'_* h^* \Phi}\ar[r]&{f'_* h^* \Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{f'_* h^* \Phi_2}\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]&{f'_* h^* \Phi_3}} \text{.} \]

By hypothesis \(\varphi_1\) is an equivalence of categories, \(\varphi_2\) is fully faithful and \(\varphi_3\) faithful. It therefore follows from the preceding diagram that \(\varphi\) is an equivalence.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 13

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be an \(S\)-morphism.

  1. 1)

    Let

    \[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{F}\ar[r]&{G}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{H}} \]

    be an exact diagram of sheaves of sets on \(X\). Suppose that \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and that \((H,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\). Then \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

  2. 2)

    Let \(F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) be a monomorphism of sheaves of groups on \(X\). If \(Y_1\) is a scheme étale over \(Y\), we set \(X_1 = Y_1 \times_Y X\), and we denote by \(f_1\) (resp. \(F_1\), resp. \(G_1\)) the inverse image of \(f\) (resp. \(F\), resp. \(G\)) on \(Y_1\) (cf. 0.2). Suppose that \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 1\)) and that, for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\) and for every torsor \(Q\) under \(G_1\), \((Q/F_1, f_1)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 0\)). Then \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 1\)).

  3. 3)

    Let \(F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) be a monomorphism of sheaves of groups on \(X\). Suppose that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\) and that \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). Then, for every torsor \(Q\) under \(G\), \((Q/F, f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).
Proof

1) Let \(\Phi\) (resp. \(\Phi_1\), resp. \(\Phi_2\)) be the stack in discrete categories associated to \(F\) (resp. \(G\), resp. \(H\)) and let \(\Phi_3\) be the final stack on \(X\). We then have an exact diagram
\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]&{\Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{\Phi_2}\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]&{\Phi_3}} \text{.} \]

By hypothesis \((\Phi_1, f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\) and \((\Phi_2, f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (2). Since \((\Phi_3, f)\) is obviously cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), it follows from 12 that \((\Phi, f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), i.e. that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

2) Let us first show that, if \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and if the \((Q/F_1, f_1)\) are cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), then \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). By 3.1 it suffices to prove that, for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\) and for every torsor \(P\) on \(X_1\) with group \(F_1\), \(({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1, f_1)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) when one considers \({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1\) as a sheaf of sets, and that the canonical morphism
\[ d \colon g^*(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^1 f'_* F' \]

is injective. The first assertion follows at once from 1), for, if \(Q\) denotes the torsor deduced from \(P\) by the extension \(F_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_1\) of the structural group, one has an isomorphism \({\vphantom{G}}^{Q}{G}_1 / {\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}_1 \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} Q/F_1\).

Let us show that \(d\) is injective. It suffices to prove that, if \(Y_1\) is an étale scheme over \(Y\), if \(P\) and \(\widetilde{P}\) are two torsors under \(F_1\) whose inverse images \(P'\) and \(\widetilde{P}'\) on \(X'_1\) are isomorphic, then, after making a surjective étale extension of \(Y_1\), \(P\) and \(\widetilde{P}\) become isomorphic. Let us choose an isomorphism \(p' \colon P' \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \widetilde{P}'\). Let \(Q\) (resp. \(\widetilde{Q}\)) be the torsor deduced from \(P\) (resp. \(\widetilde{P}\)) by the extension of the structural group \(F_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_1\). The inverse images \(Q'\) (resp. \(\widetilde{Q}'\)) of \(Q\) (resp. \(\widetilde{Q}\)) on \(X'_1\) are deduced from \(P'\) (resp. \(\widetilde{P}'\)) by the extension of the structural group \(F'_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G'_1\); let \(q' \colon Q' \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \widetilde{Q}'\) be the isomorphism obtained in the same way from \(p'\). Since \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), one may suppose, after making a surjective étale extension of \(Y_1\), that \(q'\) is the image of an isomorphism \(q \colon Q \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \widetilde{Q}\). To the torsor \(P\) (resp. \(\widetilde{P}\)) is associated a section \(x\) of \(Q/F_1\) (resp. a section \(\widetilde{x}\) of \(\widetilde{Q}/F_1\)), and, in order that \(P\) and \(\widetilde{P}\) be isomorphic, it is necessary and sufficient that one have an isomorphism \(Q \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \widetilde{Q}\) such that the isomorphism
\[ e \colon \mathrm{H}^0(X_1, Q/F_1) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(X_1,\widetilde{Q}/F_1) \]

which it induces transforms \(x\) into \(\widetilde{x}\). One takes the isomorphism \(q\). The sections \(e(x)\) and \(\widetilde{x}\) of \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_1,\widetilde{Q}/F_1)\) have the same image in \(\mathrm{H}^0(X'_1,\widetilde{Q}'/F'_1)\). Since \((\widetilde{Q}/F_1,f_1)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), after making a surjective étale extension of \(Y_1\), one indeed has \(e(x) = \widetilde{x}\), which proves the injectivity of \(d\).

To complete the proof, it remains to prove that, if \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), and if, for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\) and every torsor \(Q\) on \(X_1\) of group \(F_1\), \((Q/F_1, f_1)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), then the morphism \(d\) is surjective. Let \(P'\) be a torsor on \(X'_1\) of group \(F'_1\), \(Q'\) the torsor under \(G'_1\) obtained from \(P'\) by extension of the structural group. The datum of \(P'\) is equivalent to that of \(Q'\) and of a section \(x'\) of \(\mathrm{H}^0(X'_1,Q'/F'_1)\). It then follows from the surjectivity of the morphism
\[ g^*(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^1 f'_* G' \]

that, after making a surjective étale extension of \(Y_1\), there exists a torsor \(Q\) under \(G_1\), whose inverse image on \(X'_1\) is isomorphic to \(Q'\). Using the fact that \((Q/F_1, f_1)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), one may likewise suppose that there exists an element \(x\) of \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_1, Q/F_1)\) whose image in \(\mathrm{H}^0(X'_1, Q'/F'_1)\) is \(x'\). The datum of \(Q\) and of \(x\) determines a torsor \(P\) under \(F_1\), whose inverse image on \(X_1\) is isomorphic to \(P'\), which proves the surjectivity of \(d\).

3) Let us show that \((Q/F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), i.e. that, for every \(S\)-scheme \(S'\), for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\), if \(x\), \(\widetilde{x}\) are two elements of \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_1,Q_1/F_1)\) whose images \(x'\), \(\widetilde{x}'\) in \(\mathrm{H}^0(X'_1, Q'_1/F'_1)\) are equal, then, after surjective extension of \(Y_1\), one has \(x = \widetilde{x}\). To \(x\) (resp. \(\widetilde{x}\)) is associated a torsor \(P\) (resp. \(\widetilde{P}\)) under \(F_1\), such that \(Q_1\) is deduced from \(P\) (resp. \(\widetilde{P}\)) by the extension \(F_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_1\) of the structural group. From the relation \(x' = \widetilde{x}'\) it follows that one has an isomorphism \(u' \colon P' \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \widetilde{P}'\) such that the isomorphism induced on \(Q'_1\) by \(u'\) is the identity. Since \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), one deduces that, after surjective étale extension of \(Y_1\), one has an isomorphism \(u \colon P \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widetilde{P}\) lifting \(u'\); the fact that \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) then entails that one has \(x = \widetilde{x}\).

Let us show that \((Q/F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). Let \(Y''\) be a scheme étale over \(Y'\) and \(x''\) an element of \(\mathrm{H}^0(X'',Q''/F'')\). To \(x''\) is associated a torsor \(P''\) on \(X''\) of group \(F''\). Since \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), one can find surjective étale morphisms \(Y''_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y''\) and \(Y_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), such that one has a morphism \(Y''_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'_1\), and a torsor \(P\) on \(X_1\) of group \(F_1\) whose inverse image on \(X''_1\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of \(P''\). It then follows from the fact that \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) that one may even choose \(Y''_1\) and \(Y_1\) such that the torsor deduced from \(P\) by extension of the structural group \(F_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G_1\) is isomorphic to \(Q_1\); to \(P\) there corresponds an element \(x\) of \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_1,Q_1/F_1)\), whose image in \(\mathrm{H}^0(X''_1,Q''_1/F''_1)\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of \(x''\), which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 14

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(F\) a sheaf of sets or of groups on \(X\) (resp. a sheaf of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups, where \(\mathbb{L}\) is a set of prime numbers). Suppose \(F\) locally constant, \((F,f)\) cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. in dimension \(\leqslant 1\)) and \(f\) locally \(0\)-acyclic (resp. locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\)) (SGA 4 XV 1.11). Then, for every specialization \(\overline{s}_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{s}_2\) of geometric points of \(S\), the specialization morphism (SGA 4 VIII 7.1)

\[ a_0 \colon (f_* F)_{\overline{s}_2} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (f_* F)_{\overline{s}_1} \]

is an isomorphism, and, if \(F\) is a sheaf of groups, the morphism

\[ a_1 \colon (\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F)_{\overline{s}_2} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F)_{\overline{s}_1} \]

is injective (resp. the morphisms \(a_0\) and \(a_1\) are isomorphisms).
The proof is obtained by copying word for word that of SGA 4 XVI 2.3, but replacing therein the expression “proper” by the expression “cohomologically proper”.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 15

Let \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism, \(\Phi\) a stack on \(X\), \(\mathbb{L}\) a set of prime numbers. Suppose that, for every scheme \(X_1\) étale over \(X\) and for every pair of objects \(x\), \(y\) of \(\Phi_{X_1}\), the sheaf \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{X_1}(x,y)\) is locally constant, that the sheaf \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{X_1}(x)\) is a locally constant ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-group, and that the sheaf of maximal subgerbes \(S\Phi\) of \(\Phi\) [1] is locally constant. Suppose that \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 1\) and that \(f\) is locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\). Then, for every specialization \(\overline{s}_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{s}_2\) of geometric points of \(S\), the specialization morphism

\[ a \colon (f_* \Phi)_{\overline{s}_2} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (f_* \Phi)_{\overline{s}_1} \]

is an equivalence of categories.

Let \(\overline{S}_1\) (resp. \(\overline{S}_2\)) be the strict localization of \(S\) at \(\overline{s}_1\) (resp. the strict localization of \(S\) at \(\overline{s}_2\)), \(\overline{X}_2\), \(\overline{\Phi}_2\) (resp. \(\overline{X}_1\), \(\overline{\Phi}_1\)) the inverse images of \(X_2\), \(\Phi_2\) on \(\overline{S}_2\) (resp. of \(X_1\), \(\Phi_1\) on \(\overline{S}_1\)) and let us consider the cartesian square
\[ \xymatrix{{\overline{X}_1}\ar[r]^h\ar[d]_{\overline{f}_1}&{\overline{X}_2}\ar[d]^{\overline{f}_2}\\{\overline{S}_1}\ar[r]^g&{\overline{S}_2}} \text{.} \]
We must show that the functor
\[ \varphi \colon \overline{\Phi}_2(\overline{X}_2) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{\Phi}_1 (\overline{X}_1) \]
is an equivalence. The functor \(\varphi\) is fully faithful; let
indeed \(x\), \(y\) be two objects of \((\overline{\Phi}_2)_{\overline{X}_2}\); the canonical morphism
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\overline{X}_2}(x,y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\overline{X}_1}(\varphi(x),\varphi(y)) \]
is identified with the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X}_2, \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\overline{X}_2}(x,y)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X}_1,h^*(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\overline{X}_2}(x,y)) \text{.} \]
This morphism is an isomorphism by 14.

Let us show that \(\varphi\) is an equivalence. Let \(x_1\) be an object of \(\overline{\Phi}_1(\overline{X}_1)\) and \(G_1\) the maximal subgerbe of \(\overline{\Phi}_1\) generated by \(x_1\). The morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X}_2, S\overline{\Phi}_2) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X}_1, h^*(S \overline{\Phi}_2)) = \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X}_1,S\overline{\Phi}_1) \]
is bijective, and there exists therefore a maximal subgerbe \(G_2\) of \(\overline{\Phi}_2\) such that \(h^* G_2\) is isomorphic to \(G_1\). It then suffices to prove that the functor
\[ G_2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} h_* h^* G_2 \]

is an equivalence. But, under this form, the question is local for the étale topology on \(\overline{X}_2\). One may therefore suppose that \(G_2\) is a gerbe of torsors under the group of automorphisms of an object of \(G_2\), a case in which the assertion follows from 14.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 16

The hypotheses are those of 14. If one supposes moreover that \(F\) is a sheaf of sets (resp. of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups) and that \(f_* F\) (resp. \(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F\)) is constructible, then \(f_* F\) (resp. \(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F\)) is locally constant.
The corollary follows from 14 thanks to SGA 4 IX 2.11.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 17

Let us recall that the condition \(f\) locally \(0\)-acyclic is satisfied if \(f\) is flat with separable fibres, \(X\) and \(Y\) locally Noetherian (SGA 4 XV 4.1), and that the condition \(f\) locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\) is satisfied if \(f\) is smooth, \(\mathbb{L}\) being the set of prime numbers distinct from the residue characteristics of \(S\) (SGA 4 XV 2.1).

pages.title:
A particular case of cohomological properness: divisors
with relative normal crossings
pages.html:
2 A particular case of cohomological properness: divisors with relative normal crossings

0

Let \(R\) be a discrete valuation ring with field of fractions \(K\) and \(L\) an étale \(K\)-algebra; \(L\) is then a direct product of a finite number of fields \(L_i\), where \(L_i\) is an étale extension of \(K\). If \(L'_i\) denotes the Galois extension generated by \(L_i\) in an algebraic closure of \(L_i\), one says that \(L\) is tamely ramified over \(R\) if the \(L'_i\) are tamely ramified extensions in the sense of X 3, i.e. if an inertia group \(I_i\) of \(L'_i|K\) is of order prime to the residue characteristic \(p\) of \(R\).

It is known that \(I_i\) is in any case an extension of a cyclic group of order prime to \(p\) by a \(p\)-group. (This follows from [5] when the residue extension of \(R\) is separable. The proof given in loc. cit. extends to the general case in the following way. Let us take up again the hypotheses and notation of loc. cit. but without assuming the residue extension separable. Let \(H_i\) be the subgroup of the inertia group \(G_0\), the set of elements \(s\) of \(G_0\) such that one has \(s \pi / \pi \in U^i\) for every uniformizing element \(\pi\) of \(A_L\). One then verifies that \(G_0/H_1\) is a group of order prime to \(p\) and that, for \(i \geqslant 1\), the \(H^i/H^{i+1}\) are \(p\)-groups, whence one deduces the announced result.)

0.1

In the case where \(R\) is strictly local, one has the following simple characterization: the \(K\)-algebra \(L\) is tamely ramified over \(R\) if and only if the \([L_i:K]\) are prime to \(p\). Moreover, if \(L\) is tamely ramified over \(R\), the \(L_i\) are cyclic extensions of \(K\). Indeed, when \(R\) is strictly local, \(I_i\) is equal to the Galois group of \(L'_i\) over \(K\). As we have just recalled, \(I_i\) is an extension of a cyclic group of order prime to \(p\) by a \(p\)-group. If one assumes \(L'_i\) tamely ramified over \(R\), \(I_i\) is then a cyclic group of order prime to \(p\). It follows that \([L_i:K]\) is prime to \(p\) and that one has \(L_i = L'_i\). Conversely, if \([L_i:K]\) is prime to \(p\), \(I_i\) cannot contain a nontrivial normal \(p\)-subgroup; \(I_i\) is therefore a cyclic group of order prime to \(p\), which proves that \(L_i\) is tamely ramified over \(R\).

0.2

Let \(R\) be a discrete valuation ring with field of fractions \(K\), \(L\) an étale \(K\)-algebra, and let \(\overline{R}\) be a strict localization of \(R\), \(\overline{K}\) its field of fractions, \(\overline{L} = L \otimes_K \overline{K}\). Then, for \(L\) to be tamely ramified over \(R\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\overline{L}\) be tamely ramified over \(\overline{R}\). Indeed, one reduces to the case where \(L\) is a field. Let then \(\overline{L} = \prod_i \overline{L}_i\), where the \(\overline{L}_i\) are fields that are extensions of \(\overline{K}\); if \(L'\) is the Galois extension generated by \(L\), and, if \(\overline{L'} = L' \otimes_K \overline{K}\), one likewise has a decomposition of \(\overline{L'}\) into a product of fields, \(\overline{L'} = \prod_j \overline{L'_j}\) and each \(\overline{L}_i\) is a subextension of at least one of the \(\overline{L'_j}\). Since \(L'\) is a Galois extension of \(K\), the \(\overline{L'_j}\) are Galois extensions of \(\overline{K}\). Suppose \(L\) tamely ramified over \(R\); since the Galois group of \(\overline{L'_j}|K\) is isomorphic to the inertia group of \(L'|K\), the \(L'_j\) are also tamely ramified over \(\overline{R}\), and the same is therefore true of the \(\overline{L}_i\). Conversely, suppose \(\overline{L}\) tamely ramified over \(\overline{R}\). For each \(j\), let \(v_j\) be the discrete valuation of \(\overline{L'_j}\) which extends the valuation of \(\overline{K}\) and let us again denote by \(v_j\) the valuation induced on \(L'\). As \(j\) varies, \(v_j\) runs through the set of valuations of \(L\) which extend the valuation of \(K\). Let \(G = \operatorname{Gal}(L'|K)\), \(H=\operatorname{Gal}(L'|L)\), \(I_j\) the inertia group of \(L'|K\) at \(v_j\), \(J_j\) the inertia group of \(L'|L\) at \(v_j\). The group \(I_i\) is an extension of a cyclic group of order prime to \(p\) by a \(p\)-group \(P_j\). Since the \(\overline{L}_i\) are tamely ramified over \(\overline{R}\), \(I_j/J_j\) is of order prime to \(p\), so one has \(P_j \subset J_j\). Consequently the group \(H\) contains all the \(P_j\), hence also the group \(P\) generated by the \(P_j\) for varying \(j\). But the group \(P\) is invariant in \(G\) because an inner automorphism of \(G\) transforms the \(I_j\) among themselves, hence also the \(P_j\) among themselves. It follows that \(P\) is a subgroup of \(H\) normal in \(G\), hence, since \(L'\) is the Galois extension generated by \(L\), that one has \(P=1\), which proves that \(L\) is tamely ramified over \(R\).

Let more generally \(R \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R'\) be a morphism of discrete valuation rings such that the image of a uniformizing element \(\pi\) of \(R\) is a uniformizing element \(\pi'\) of \(R'\) and such that the residue extension \(\mathit{k}(R')\) is a separable extension of \(\mathit{k}(R)\). Let \(K\) be the field of fractions of \(R\), \(K'\) the field of fractions of \(R'\), \(L\) an étale \(K\)-algebra, \(L' = L \otimes_K K'\). Then, for \(L\) to be tamely ramified over \(R\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(L'\) be tamely ramified over \(R'\). Indeed, one may assume \(R\) and \(R'\) strictly local. By 0.1 it suffices to prove that, when \(L\) is a field, so is \(L'\). Let \(\widetilde{R}\) be the normalization of \(R\) in \(L\), \(\widetilde{\pi}\) a uniformizing element of \(\widetilde{R}\), \(\widetilde{R}' = \widetilde{R} \otimes_R R'\). The extension \(\mathit{k}(\widetilde{R})|\mathit{k}(R)\) being radicial and the extension \(\mathit{k}(R')|\mathit{k}(R)\) étale, \(\mathit{k}(\widetilde{R})\otimes_{\mathit{k}(R)}\mathit{k}(R')\) is a field [EGA IV 4.3.2 and 4.3.5]. This proves that \(R'\) is a local ring, and, since \(\pi\) has image \(\pi'\) in \(R'\), one has \(\mathit{k}(\widetilde{R}')= R'/(\widetilde{\pi})\); consequently \(R'\) is a discrete valuation ring [5] hence \(L'\) is a field.

0.3

By reduction to the strictly local case, one sees that a subalgebra of a tamely ramified algebra is tamely ramified, that the tensor product of two tamely ramified algebras is tamely ramified, that a tamely ramified algebra remains so after extension of the discrete valuation ring, that an algebra which becomes tamely ramified after a tamely ramified extension is tamely ramified.

1

Let \(X\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(D\) a divisor \(\geqslant 0\) on \(X\). Recall (SGA 5 II 4.2) that one says that \(D\) has strict normal crossings relatively to \(S\) if there exists a finite family \((f_i)_{i\in I}\) of elements of \(\Gamma(X,\mathscr{O}_X)\), such that one has \(D=\sum_{i\in I}\operatorname{div}(f_i)\) and such that the following condition is fulfilled:

1.0

For every point \(x\) of \(\operatorname{Supp} D\), \(X\) is smooth over \(S\) at \(x\), and, if one denotes by \(I(x)\) the set of \(i\in I\) such that \(f_i(x)=0\), the subscheme \(V((f_i)_{i\in I(x)})\) is smooth over \(S\) of codimension \(\operatorname{card} I(x)\) in \(X\).

The divisor \(D\) is said to have normal crossings relatively to \(S\) if, locally on \(X\) for the étale topology, it has strict normal crossings.

Let \(D\) be a divisor with normal crossings relatively to \(S\). One sets \(Y=\operatorname{Supp} D\), \(U=X-Y\), and one denotes by \(i\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion. For every geometric point \(\overline{s}\) of \(S\) and for every maximal point \(y\) of the geometric fibre \(Y_{\overline{s}}\), the ring \(R=\mathscr{O}_{X_{\overline{s}},y}\) is a discrete valuation ring.

In the remainder of this article, we shall use the following technical definition:

Definition 1.1

Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets on \(U\). One says that \(F\) is tamely ramified over \(X\) (along \(D\)) relatively to \(S\) if, for every geometric point \(\overline{s}\) of \(S\), the following condition is satisfied:

For every maximal point \(y\) of \(Y_{\overline{s}}\), the restriction of \(F\) to the field of fractions \(K\) of \(\mathscr{O}_{X_{\overline{s}}}\) is representable by the spectrum of an étale \(K\)-algebra \(L\), tamely ramified over \(\mathscr{O}_{X_{\overline{s},y}}\).

Most often, when no confusion can result from it, we shall omit the mention of \(D\) in the terminology.

Definition 1.2

If \(F\) is a sheaf of groups on \(U\), tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\), one denotes by

\[ \mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(U,F) \]

the subset of \(\mathrm{H}^1(U,F)\) formed of the classes of torsors under \(F\) which are tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\).

Let

\[ \xymatrix{{U}\ar[r]^i\ar[d]_g&{X}\ar[dl]^f\\{T}} \]

be a commutative diagram of \(S\)-schemes, with \(i\) as in 1; one denotes by

\[ \mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}} g_* F \]

the sheaf on \(T\) associated to the presheaf \(T'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}\mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(U',F)\), where \(T'\) runs through the schemes étale over \(T\) and where \(U'=U\times_T T'\); \(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}} g_*F\) is a subsheaf of \(\mathrm{R}^1g_*F\).

Let us note that, if \(g\) is coherent, if \(\overline{t}\) is a geometric point of \(T\), \(\overline{T}\) the strict localization of \(T\) at \(\overline{t}\), \(\overline{U}=U\times_T\overline{T}\), one has an isomorphism

\[ \tag{1.2.1} (\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}} g_*F)_{\overline{t}}\simeq \mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U},\overline{F}) \]

1.3

Let \(C_t((U,X)/S)\) or simply \(C_t\) be the category of étale coverings of \(U\) that are tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\). Suppose \(U\) connected and let \(a\) be a geometric point of \(U\). Let \(\Gamma_t\) be the functor which, to an étale covering \(U'\) of \(U\) tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\), assigns the set of geometric points of \(U'\) lying over \(a\). From 0 it follows that the pair \((C_t,\Gamma_t)\) satisfies the axioms (\(G_1\)) to (\(G_6\)) of V 4. Consequently \(\Gamma_t\) is representable by a pro-object which one calls the universal tamely ramified covering of \((U,X)\) relatively to \(S\) pointed at \(a\). The group opposite to the group of \(U\)-automorphisms of the universal tamely ramified covering is called the tamely ramified fundamental group and denoted

\[ \pi_1^{\mathrm{t}}((U,X)/S,a)\text{ or simply }\pi_1^{\mathrm{t}}(U,a)\text{ or even }\pi_1^{\mathrm{t}}(U). \]

It is obviously a quotient of the fundamental group \(\pi_1(U,a)\) (V 9).

1.4

Let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups on \(U\), \(P\) a right torsor with group \(F\), \(Q\) a left torsor with group \(F\), and suppose \(P\) and \(Q\) tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\). Then the same holds for \(P\stackrel{F}{\wedge}Q\). Indeed, one reduces to showing that, if \(R\) is a discrete valuation ring with field of fractions \(K\) and if \(F\) is a finite étale group scheme over \(K\), and if \(P\) and \(Q\) are two torsors under \(F\) tamely ramified over \(R\), then the same holds for \(P\stackrel{F}{\wedge} Q\). Now \(T=P\stackrel{F}{\wedge} Q\) is a quotient of \(P\times_K Q\). If \(L\), \(M\), \(N\) denote the \(K\)-algebras representing respectively \(T\), \(P\), \(Q\), then \(L\) is a subalgebra of \(M\otimes_K N\), and it follows from 0.3 that \(L\) is tamely ramified over \(R\).

From the foregoing one deduces that, if \(F\) is a sheaf of groups on \(U\) and if there exists a torsor with group \(F\), tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\), then \(F\) is tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\). Indeed the torsor \(P^{\circ}\) opposite to \(P\) is tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\), since it is isomorphic to \(P\) as a sheaf of sets. If \({\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}\) is the group obtained from \(F\) by twisting by \(P\), one has an isomorphism

\[ F\simeq P^{\circ}\stackrel{{\vphantom{F}}^{P\mkern-4mu}{F}}{\wedge}P \]

and consequently \(F\) is tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\).

One sees as before that, if \(F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F'\) is a morphism of sheaves of groups on \(U\), tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\), and if \(P\) is a torsor under \(F\) tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\), then the torsor \(P'\) deduced from \(P\) by the extension of the structural group \(F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F'\) is tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\).

In particular the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(U,F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(U,F') \]

gives, by restriction to \(\mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(U,F)\), a canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(U,F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(U,F') \]

1.5

Let \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism and denote by \(U'\) (resp. \(X'\), etc.) the inverse image of \(U\) (resp. \(X\), etc.) over \(S'\). If \(F\) is a sheaf of sets on \(U\) tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\), it follows from definition 1.1 and from 0.3 that \(F'\) is tamely ramified over \(X'\) relatively to \(S'\).

If now \(F\) is a sheaf of groups on \(U\), the inverse image over \(S'\) of a torsor under \(F\) tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\) is a torsor under \(F'\) tamely ramified over \(X'\) relatively to \(S'\). In particular one has a canonical functor

\[ \tag{1.5.1} C_t((U,X)/S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} C_t((U',X')/S'). \]

Suppose \(U\) and \(U'\) connected and let \(a\) be a geometric point of \(U\), \(a'\) a geometric point of \(U'\) lying over \(a\); from the foregoing one deduces a canonical morphism

\[ \tag{1.5.2} \pi_1^{\mathrm{t}}(U',a')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{\mathrm{t}}(U,a). \]

If \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a morphism and \(h\colon T'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) the canonical projection, the morphism

\[ h^*(\mathrm{R}^1g_*F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{R}^1g_*'F' \]

gives by restriction a canonical morphism

\[ \tag{1.5.3} h^*(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}g_*F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}g_*'F' \]

1.6

Let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups on \(U\), tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\). The notations being those of 1.2, one has canonical exact sequences:

\[ \tag{1.6.1} \begin{array}{ccccccc} 1 & \longrightarrow & \mathrm{H}^1(X,i_*F)& \longrightarrow & \mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(U,F) & \longrightarrow & \mathrm{H}^0(X,\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}} i_*F)\\[10pt] 1 & \longrightarrow & \mathrm{R}^1f_*(i_*F) & \longrightarrow & \mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}g_*F & \longrightarrow & f_*(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}i_*F).\end{array} \]

The first is obtained from the exact sequence (SGA 4 III 3.2):

\[ 1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(X,i_*F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(U,F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(X,\mathrm{R}^1i_*F). \]

It suffices indeed to show that the image of \(\mathrm{H}^1(X,i_*F)\) in \(\mathrm{H}^1(U,F)\) is in fact contained in \(\mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(U,F)\) and that the image of \(\mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(U,F)\) in \(\mathrm{H}^0(X,\mathrm{R}^1i_*F)\) is in fact contained in \(\mathrm{H}^0(X,\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}i_*F)\). Now the inverse image over \(U\) of a torsor under \(i_*F\) is a torsor under \(i^*i_*F\) which is obviously tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\), hence the same holds after the extension of the structural group \(i^*i_*F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F\), which proves the existence of the arrow \(\mathrm{H}^1(X,i_*F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(U,F)\). The fact that the image of \(\mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(U,F)\) in \(\mathrm{H}^0(X,\mathrm{R}^1i_*F)\) is contained in \(\mathrm{H}^0(X,\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}i_*F)\) follows at once from the definition of \(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}i_*F\). This proves the existence of the first exact sequence, and the second is deduced from it by localization.

2

We keep the notations of 1. We are going to define a notion of tamely ramified object of a stack \(\Phi\) on \(U\) when the latter is given, locally over \(X\) and over \(S\) for the étale topology, as inverse image of a stack \(\Psi\) over \(S\).

Let first \(G\) be a gerbe on \(U\) and suppose given an étale surjective morphism \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), an étale surjective morphism \(X_2\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S S_1\), a trivial gerbe \(H\) on \(S_1\) and an isomorphism

\[ G|{U_2}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H|{U_2}, \]

where \(U_1=U\times_X X_1\), \(U_2=U\times_X X_2\). When one chooses a trivialization of \(H|{X_2}\), the isomorphism above identifies \(G|{U_2}\) with the stack of torsors under a sheaf of groups \(F\). One says that an element \(x\) of \(G_U\) is tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\) if the restriction of \(x\) to \(U_2\) is a torsor tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\). By 1.4 this notion does not depend on the way in which one has trivialized \(H|X_2\).

Let now \(\Phi\) be a stack on \(U\) and suppose given an étale surjective morphism \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), an étale surjective morphism \(X_2\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_S S_1\), a stack \(\Psi\) on \(S_1\) and an isomorphism

\[ i\colon \Phi|U_2\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Psi|U_2. \]

Let \(x\) be an element of \(\Phi_U\), \(G_x\) the maximal subgerbe of \(\Phi\) generated by \(x\) [1], \(S\Phi\) the sheaf of maximal subgerbes of \(\Phi\). The isomorphism \(i\) induces an isomorphism

\[ S\Phi|U_2\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\Psi|U_2. \]

It follows from 7 that, up to replacing \(S_1\) by a surjective étale extension, one has a unique maximal sub-gerbe \(H\) of \(\Psi\), which may be assumed trivial, such that \(i\) defines an isomorphism

\[ G_x|U_2\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H|U_2. \]

One says that the element \(x\) is tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\) if it is so as an element of \(G_x\) endowed with the isomorphism above.

2.1

Let \(\Phi\) be a stack over \(U\) given, locally on \(X\) and \(S\), as the inverse image of a stack over \(S\), and let

\[ \xymatrix{{U}\ar[r]^i\ar[d]_g&{X}\ar[dl]^f\\{T}} \]

a diagram as in 1.2. For every scheme \(T'\) étale over \(T\), if \(U'=U\times_T T'\), one considers the subset \((g_*^{\mathrm{t}}\Phi)_{T'}\) of \((g_*\Phi)_{T'}=\Phi_{U'}\) formed of the elements of \(\Phi_{U'}\) which are tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\). One calls tamely ramified direct image of \(\Phi\) by \(g\), and one denotes

\[ g_*^{\mathrm{t}}\Phi \]

the full subcategory of \(g_*\Phi\) whose objects over a scheme \(T'\) étale over \(T\) are the elements of \((g_*^{\mathrm{t}}\Phi)_{T'}\). It is clear that \(g_*^{\mathrm{t}}\Phi\) is a substack of \(g_*\Phi\).

2.2

By reduction to the case of a stack of torsors, one sees that, if \(\Phi\) is a stack over \(U\) which is locally for the étale topology of \(S\) and \(X\) the inverse image of a stack over \(S\), the canonical morphism

\[ h^*(g_*\Phi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g_*'\Phi' \]

gives by restriction a canonical morphism

\[ h^*(g_*^{\mathrm{t}}\Phi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} g_*^{\prime\mathrm{t}}\Phi' \]
Remarks 3

a) If \(F\) is a locally constant constructible sheaf of sets on \(U\), for \(F\) to be tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\), it suffices that the condition of 1.1 be satisfied for the geometric points of \(S\) above the maximal points of \(S\). To see this one may assume the divisor \(D\) has strictly normal crossings. The sheaf \(F\) is representable by an étale covering \(V\) of \(U\). If \(\overline{s}\) is a geometric point of \(S\), \(y\) a maximal point of \(Y_{\overline{s}}\), one denotes by \(\overline{S}\) the strict localization of \(S\) at \(\overline{s}\), \(\overline{X}\) the strict localization of \(X\) at \(\overline{y}\), \(\overline{U}=U\times_X \overline{X}\), \(\overline{V}=V\times_X\overline{X}\). If the condition of 1.1 is satisfied at the geometric points above the maximal points of \(S\), it follows from 5 below that \(\overline{V}\) is a covering of \(\overline{U}\) tamely ramified over \(\overline{X}\) relatively to \(\overline{S}\); consequently \(V\) is an étale covering of \(U\) tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\).

b) Let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups on \(U\) tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\). If \(\overline{s}\) is a geometric point of \(S\), \(y\) a maximal point of \(Y_{\overline{s}}\), one denotes by \(K\) the field of fractions of \(\mathscr{O}_{X_{\overline{s}},y}\). Suppose that, for every point \(\overline{s}\) and for every point \(y\), the \(K\)-algebra \(L\) whose spectrum represents \(F|K\) is of rank prime to the residue characteristic \(p\) of \(\mathscr{O}_{X_{\overline{s}}}\). One will sometimes say, by abuse of language, that \(F\) is prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\). When this is so, every torsor \(P\) under \(F\) is tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\).

Indeed, let \(\overline{R}\) be the strict localization of \(\mathscr{O}_{X_{\overline{s}},y}\) at \(\overline{y}\), \(\overline{K}\) its field of fractions, \(\overline{F}\) the inverse image of \(F\) on \(\overline{K}\). Let us show that one may assume \(F\) constant. Since \(F\) is tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\), \(\overline{F}\) is representable by the spectrum of a \(\overline{K}\)-algebra \(L=\prod L_i\), where the \(L_i\) are extensions of \(\overline{K}\) of degree prime to \(p\). One can therefore find an extension \(K'\) of \(\overline{K}\) of degree prime to \(p\) such that \(\overline{F}|K'\) is a constant sheaf. By 0.3, to prove that \(P|\overline{K}\) is tamely ramified over \(\overline{R}\), it suffices to see that \(P|K'\) is tamely ramified over the integral closure of \(\overline{R}\) in \(K'\), whence the reduction to the case where \(\overline{F}\) is constant. Assume henceforth \(\overline{F}\) constant. The \(\overline{K}\)-algebra \(H\) which represents \(P|\overline{K}\) is then a product of extensions \(H_i\) of \(\overline{K}\) mutually isomorphic. Since the rank of \(H\) is prime to \(p\), so is that of \([H_1:\overline{K}]\), which proves that \(H\) is tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\).

c) Let \(X\) be a regular scheme, \(D\) a divisor with normal crossings on \(X\) (SGA 5 I 3.1.5), \(U=X-\operatorname{Supp} D\), \(F\) a sheaf of sets on \(U\). If \(y\) is a maximal point of \(\operatorname{Supp} D\), one designates by \(K\) the field of fractions of \(\mathscr{O}_{X,y}\). One says that \(F\) is tamely ramified relatively to \(D\), if, for every maximal point \(y\) of \(\operatorname{Supp} D\), \(F|K\) is representable by a \(K\)-algebra tamely ramified over \(\mathscr{O}_{X,y}\).

Theorem 4

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(D\) a divisor on \(X\) with normal crossings relatively to \(S\) (1), \(Y=\operatorname{Supp} D\), \(U=X-Y\), \(i\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion. Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets (resp. of groups) on \(U\), satisfying one of the following conditions:

  1. a)

    \(F\) is locally for the étale topology on \(X\) and on \(S\) the inverse image of a sheaf of sets (resp. of a constructible sheaf of groups) on \(S\).

  2. b)

    \(F\) is locally constant constructible on \(U\) and tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\).

Then one has the following conclusions:

  1. 1)

    \((F,i)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. for every morphism \(h\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), if \(i'\colon U'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) is the inverse image of \(i\) on \(S'\), if \(F'=F|U'\) and if \(k=h_{(X)}\), the canonical morphism

    \[ \Psi\colon k^*(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}i_*F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}i_*'F' \]

    is an isomorphism).

    If \(F\) is a sheaf of groups prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\) (3.b)) (tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\)), then \((F,i)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\).

  2. 2)

    If \(F\) is a constructible sheaf of sets (resp. of groups), \(i_*F\) (resp. \(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}i_*F\)) is constructible.

Proof

For every \(S\)-scheme \(S'\), one considers the following diagram in which all the squares are cartesian:

\[ \xymatrix{{U}\ar[dd]_-g\ar[dr]^-i& & &{U'}\ar[lll]\ar[dd]_{g'}\ar[dr]^-{i'}& \\ &{X}\ar[dl]_{f\mkern-8mu}& & &{X'}\ar[lll]_{k}\ar[dl]_{f'\mkern-10mu}\\{S}& & &{S'}\ar[lll]_-h& & } \]

Since the question is local on \(X\) for the étale topology, one may assume that \(D\) is a divisor with strictly normal crossings relatively to \(S\) (1); moreover, up to restricting \(X\) to a neighbourhood of \(Y\), one may assume \(X\) smooth over \(S\).

Proof of 4 1)

4.1

Case of a sheaf of sets satisfying a). One may assume that one has \(F=g^*G\), where \(G\) is a sheaf on \(S\). It then follows from (SGA 4 XVI 3.2) that the canonical morphism

\[ \tag{4.1.1} f^*G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*F \]

is an isomorphism. For every \(S\)-scheme \(h\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), one has likewise an isomorphism \(f'^*G'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*'F'\); consequently the canonical morphism

\[ \varphi\colon k^*(i_*F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*'F' \]

is identified with the natural isomorphism

\[ k^*f^*G\simeq f'^*G' \]

4.2

Case of a sheaf of sets satisfying b). One must show that \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, and for this it suffices to see that this is so at each geometric point \(\overline{x}'\) of \(X'\). Let \(\overline{S}\) (resp. \(\overline{X}\), resp. \(\overline{S'}\), resp. \(\overline{X'}\)) be the strict localization of \(S\) (resp. \(X\), resp. \(S'\), resp. \(X'\)) at \(\overline{x}'\), and set \(\overline{U}=U_{(\overline{X})}\), \(\overline{U'}=U_{(\overline{X'})}\), etc. The morphism \(\varphi_{\overline{x}}\) is identified with the canonical morphism

\[ \overline{\varphi}\colon \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{U},\overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{U'}, \overline{F'}) \]

One can find a principal covering \(V\) of \(\overline{U}\), of the type appearing in 4, such that the inverse image of \(\overline{F}\) on \(V\) is a constant sheaf with value \(C\). If \(\Pi\) is the Galois group of \(V\) over \(\overline{U}\), \(\Pi\) operates on \(\overline{F}|V\), and one has

\[ \tag{4.2.1} \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{U},\overline{F}))\simeq \mathrm{H}^0(V,C_V)^{\Pi} \]

where the second member denotes the set of elements of \(\mathrm{H}^0(V,C_V)\) invariant under \(\Pi\). Since \(V'=V\times_{\overline{U}}\overline{U'}\) is a principal covering of \(\overline{U'}\) with Galois group \(\Pi'\simeq \Pi\), one sees that the morphism \(\overline{\varphi}\) is obtained, by taking the invariants under \(\Pi\), from the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(V,C_V)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(V',C_{V'}). \]

Since \(V\) and \(V'\) are connected (4), this morphism, and hence also \(\overline{\varphi}\), is an isomorphism.

Let us note that if moreover \(F\) is a sheaf of groups and if \(P\) is a torsor on \(\overline{U}\) with group \(\overline{F}\), tamely ramified relatively to \(D\), it follows from the preceding proof and from 2 that \(({\vphantom{\overline{F}}}^{P}{\overline{F}},\overline{i})\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

4.3

Case of a sheaf of groups. To show that \(\Psi\) is an isomorphism, it suffices to prove that, for every geometric point \(\overline{y}'\) of \(Y'\), the morphism

\[ \Psi_{\overline{y}'}\colon (k^*(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}i_*F))_{\overline{y}'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}} i_*'F')_{\overline{y}'} \]

is an isomorphism. Now, according to 1.2, \(\Psi_{\overline{y}'}\) is identified with the canonical morphism

\[ \overline{\Psi}\colon \mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U},\overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U'},\overline{F'}). \]

Let \(\widetilde{U}\) be the universal tamely ramified covering of \(\overline{U}\) (1.3) and \(\widetilde{F}\) the inverse image of \(\overline{F}\) on \(\widetilde{U}\). It follows from 7 in case a) and from 5 in case b), that \(\mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U},\overline{F})\) is identified with the subset \(\mathrm{H}^1(\overline{U},\overline{F})\) formed of the classes of \(\overline{F}\)-torsors whose inverse image on \(\widetilde{U}\) is trivial. On the other hand a classical argument (cf. IX 5, p. ) shows that the set of elements of \(\mathrm{H}^1(\overline{U},\overline{F})\) whose inverse image on \(\widetilde{U}\) is trivial is identified with \(\mathrm{H}^1(\pi_1^{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U}),\mathrm{H}^0(\widetilde{U},\widetilde{F})).\) One thus obtains a canonical isomorphism

\[ \tag{4.3.1} {} \mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U},\overline{F})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(\pi_1^{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U}),\mathrm{H}^0(\widetilde{U},\widetilde{F})). \]

Consequently the morphism \(\overline{\Psi}\) is identified with the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(\pi_1^{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U}),\mathrm{H}^0(\widetilde{U},\widetilde{F})) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(\pi_1^{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U'}),\mathrm{H}^0(\widetilde{U}',\widetilde{F}')). \]

Let us show that this morphism is an isomorphism. The morphism \(\pi_1^{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U'})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U})\) is an isomorphism according to 6, and the same holds for the morphism \(\mathrm{H}^0(\widetilde{U},\widetilde{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(\widetilde{U}',\widetilde{F}')\). Indeed, this is evident in case b) since \(\widetilde{F}\) is constant and \(\widetilde{U}\) and \(\widetilde{U}'\) are connected. In case a), let \(\overline{G}\) be a constructible sheaf of groups on \(\overline{S}\) such that one has \(\overline{F}=\overline{g}^*\overline{G};\) since the morphisms \(\widetilde{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{S}\) and \(\widetilde{U}'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{S'}\) are \(0\)-acyclic (7), one has

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(\widetilde{U},\widetilde{F})\simeq \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{S},\overline{G}) \simeq \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{S'},\overline{G'})\simeq \mathrm{H}^0(\widetilde{U}',\widetilde{F}'), \]

which entails that \(\Psi\) is an isomorphism. The last assertion of 4 1) follows from what precedes, taking into account 3 b).

Proof of 4 2)

The case of a constructible sheaf of sets satisfying a) follows at once from (4.1.1). Let \(F=g^*G\) be a sheaf of groups satisfying a), where \(G\) is a constructible sheaf; one may assume \(S\) affine; let \((S_j)_{j\in J}\) be a finite family of reduced closed subschemes of \(S\) whose union covers \(S\), such that the inverse image of \(G\) on \(S_j\) is a locally constant sheaf. Taking into account 4 1), in order to establish that \(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}i_*F\) is constructible, it suffices to see that this is so after the change of base \(S_j\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), for each \(j\in J\). One is thus reduced to case b) where \(F\) is locally constant.

One assumes henceforth that \(F\) is a sheaf of sets or of groups satisfying b). Since the question is local for the étale topology on \(X\), one may assume \(X\) of finite presentation over \(S\), and, by passage to the limit, one may assume \(X\) and \(S\) Noetherian.

Let \(D=\sum_{1\leqslant i\leqslant r}\operatorname{div} f_i\), where, for each point \(x\) of \(\operatorname{Supp} D\), if \(I(x)\) is the set of the \(i\) such that \(f_i(x)=0\), the subscheme \(V((f_i)_{i\in I(x)})\) is smooth over \(S\) of codimension \(\operatorname{card} I(x)\) in \(X\). Let \(\mathscr{P}\) be the set of the parts of \([ 1,r ]\) and, for each \(I\in\mathscr{P}\), set

\[ X_I=\textstyle\Big(\bigcap_{i\in I}V(f_i)\Big)\cap\Big(\bigcap_{i\not\in I}X_{f_i}\Big). \]

Let \(z\) be a point of \(X_I\). Up to first restricting to an étale neighbourhood of \(z\), one can find an open \(W\) of \(X\) containing \(z\) and a principal covering \(V\) of \(U\cap W\), tamely ramified over \(W\) relatively to \(S\), of the type considered in 6.1, such that the inverse image of \(F\) on \(V\) is a constant sheaf with value \(C\). Let \(\pi\) be the Galois group of the covering \(V\). For every geometric point \(\overline{x}\) of \(X_I\), one then has according to (4.2.1)

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{U},\overline{F})\simeq \mathrm{H}^0(V,C_V)^{\pi}. \]

It follows that \(i_*F|X_I\cap W\) is locally constant (SGA 4 IX 2.13) and consequently \(i_*F\) is constructible.

Let us show finally that if \(F\) is a locally constant sheaf of groups, \(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}i_*F|_{X_I}\) is constructible. If \(\overline{x}\) is a geometric point of \(X\), one has obtained in (4.3.1) the expression

\[ \mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U},\overline{F})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(\pi^{\mathrm{t}}_1(\overline{U}),\mathrm{H}^0(\widetilde{U},\widetilde{F})). \]

If \(p\) is the residue characteristic of \(\overline{X}\), one has, according to 6, \(\pi_1^{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U})=\prod_{l\neq p }\mathbf{Z}_l(1)^{\operatorname{card} I}\). Let us denote by \(\mathbb{L}\) the set of prime numbers that divide the order of the finite group \(\mathrm{H}^0(\widetilde{U},\widetilde{F})\) and let \(K=\prod_{l\in\mathbb{L} -\{p\}\cap\mathbb{L} }\mathbf{Z}_l(1)^{\operatorname{card} I}\). It follows from [4] that one has

\[ \mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U},\overline{F})\simeq \mathrm{H}^1(K,\mathrm{H}^0(\widetilde{U},\widetilde{F})). \]

Since \(K\) is topologically of finite type and \(\mathrm{H}^0(\widetilde{U},\widetilde{F})\) finite, one deduces first of all that the fibres of the sheaf \(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}i_*F|_{X_I}\) are finite. On the other hand, the set \(\mathbb{L}\) does not depend on the point \(\overline{x}\). For every \(q\in\mathbb{L}\), let \(X_{I,q}\) be the closed subset of \(X_I\) with equation \(q=0\) and let \(X_{I'}\) be the open subset of \(X_I\) complementary to the union of the \(X_{I,q}\). Then \(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}i_*F|_{X_{I,q}}\) and \(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}i_*F|_{X_I'}\) are locally constant; indeed a specialization arrow of geometric points of \(X_{I,q}\) (resp. of \(X_{I'}\)) induces an isomorphism on the groups \(K\) (6.1) hence also on the sets \(\mathrm{H}^1_{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U},\overline{F})\), and one may apply SGA 4 IX 2.13.

Corollary 5

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism, \(D\) a divisor on \(X\) with normal crossings relative to \(S\) (1), \(Y=\operatorname{Supp} D\), \(U=X- Y\), \(i\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion. Let \(\Phi\) be a stack over \(U\) and suppose given étale surjective morphisms \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) and \(X_2\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times_{S}S_1\), a stack \(\Psi\) over \(S_1\) and an isomorphism \(\Phi|U_2\simeq\Psi|U_2\) (cf. 2).

Then, for every morphism \(h\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), if \(k=h_{(X)}\), the canonical functor

\[ \varphi\colon k^*i_*\Phi\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*'\Phi' \]

is fully faithful. If \(\Psi\) is constructible, the canonical functor

\[ \psi\colon k^*i_*^{\mathrm{t}}\Phi\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*^{\prime\mathrm{t}}\Phi' \]

is an equivalence of categories.

Moreover, if the stack \(\Psi\) is constructible (resp. if \(\Psi\) is \(1\)-constructible (0) and \(S\) locally Noetherian), \(i_*\Phi\) is constructible (resp. \(i_*^t\Phi\) is \(1\)-constructible).

Let us show that \(\varphi\) is fully faithful. It suffices to see that, for every geometric point \(\overline{x}'\) of \(X'\), the same holds for the functor

\[ \overline{\varphi}\colon \Phi(\overline{U})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\Phi'(\overline{U'}) \]

(we have resumed the notations of 4.2). Let \(a\), \(b\) be two elements of \(\Phi(\overline{U})\), \(a',\ b'\) their images under \(\overline{\varphi}\). Since the morphism \(\overline{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{S}\) is locally \(0\)-acyclic (7), one has an isomorphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{U},\overline{S\Phi}) \overset{\sim}{\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}}\mathrm{H}^0(S,\overline{S\Psi}). \]

Consequently \(a\) and \(b\) come by inverse image from elements of \(\Psi\), and the same therefore holds for \(F=\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\overline{U}}(a,b)\). Since the sheaf \(F'=\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\overline{U'}}(a',b')\) is the inverse image on \(\overline{U'}\) of \(F\), it follows from 4.1 that the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{U},F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{U'},F') \]

is an isomorphism, which proves that \(\overline{\varphi}\) is fully faithful.

Let us show that, for every geometric point \(\overline{x}'\) of \(X'\), the functor

\[ \overline{\psi}\colon i^{\mathrm{t}}_*\Phi(\overline{X'})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*^{\prime\mathrm{t}}\Phi'(\overline{X'}) \]

is an equivalence. By what precedes, \(\overline{\psi}\) is fully faithful. Let us show that \(\overline{\psi}\) is essentially surjective. Let \(a'\) be an element of \(\Phi'(\overline{U'})\) tamely ramified over \(X'\) relative to \(S'\) (2) and let us show that it is the image of a tamely ramified element of \(\Phi(\overline{U})\). It follows from 4 1) that the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{U},\overline{S}\overline{\Phi})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{U'},\overline{S}\overline{\Phi}') \]

is an isomorphism. Let \(G'\) be the maximal subgerbe of \(\Phi'\) generated by \(a'\); there then exists a maximal subgerbe \(G\) of \(\overline{\Phi}\), inverse image of a gerbe over \(\overline{S}\), such that one has

\[ \overline{m}^*G\simeq G', \]

where \(m\) is the morphism \(\overline{U'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{U}\). The canonical functor

\[ \tag{*} {\overline{k}^*\overline{i}_*^{\mathrm{t}}G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{i}_*^{\prime\mathrm{t}}G'} \]

is an equivalence, for \(G\) is identified with a gerbe of torsors under a constructible sheaf of groups coming from \(\overline{S}\), and one may apply 4 1). It then follows from (*) that there exists an element \(a\) of \(G(\overline{U})\), tamely ramified over \(X\) relative to \(S\), whose inverse image on \(\overline{U'}\) is \(a'\), which proves that \(\psi\) is an equivalence.

If \(\Psi\) is constructible, the same holds for \(i_*\Phi\); indeed an object \(x\) of \(i_*\Phi\) is, locally for the étale topology of \(S\), inverse image of an object \(y\) of \(\Psi\); it follows therefore from 4.1.1 that \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}} (x)\) is the inverse image of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}} (y)\), hence is constructible. Finally, if \(\Psi\) is \(1\)-constructible, the same holds for \(i^{\mathrm{t}}_*\Phi\) by 3 below.

Corollary 6

The notations are those of 4. Suppose that \(S\) is of characteristic zero at every point \(s\) such that one has \(Y_s\neq \emptyset.\) Then, if \(\mathscr{ F}\) is a locally constant constructible sheaf of groups on \(U\) (resp. a constructible stack over \(U\) which is locally over \(X\) and \(S\) inverse image of a constructible stack over \(S\)), \((\mathscr{ F},i)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1.\)

Since every constructible sheaf of sets on \(U\) is tamely ramified over \(X\) relative to \(S,\) the corollary follows from 4 (resp. 5).

Corollary 7

The notations are those of 4, but one moreover gives oneself a \(S\)-scheme \(T\), a proper morphism \(p\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T,\) and one supposes \(X\) and \(T\) of finite presentation over \(S\); let \(q=pi\). Let \(\mathscr{ F}\) be a constructible sheaf of sets on \(U\) satisfying one of the conditions a) or b) of 4 (resp. a sheaf of groups satisfying one of the conditions a), b) of 4, resp. a stack over \(U\) which is locally over \(X\) and \(S\) inverse image of a constructible stack \(G\) over \(S\)). Then one has the following conclusions:

  1. 1)

    \((\mathscr{ F},q)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. for every morphism \(h\colon S^{'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), if \(m=h_{(T)}\), the canonical morphism:

    \[ \Theta\colon m^*(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}q_*\mathscr{ F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}q_*^{'}\mathscr{ F}^{'} \]

    is an isomorphism, resp. for every morphism \(S^{'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), the canonical morphism

    \[ \xi \colon m^*(q_*^{\mathrm{t}}\mathscr{ F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {q'}_*^{{\mathrm{t}}}\mathscr{F}^{'} \]

    is an equivalence).

  2. 2)

    the sheaf \(q_*\mathscr{ F}\) (resp. the sheaf \(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{t}}^1q_*\mathscr{ F}\), resp. the stack \(q_*^{\mathrm{t}}\mathscr{ F}\)) is constructible. In the last case, if one supposes \(S\) locally Noetherian and \(G\) \(1\)-constructible, the same holds for \(q_*^{\mathrm{t}}\mathscr{ F}.\)

The first part follows immediately from 4, 5 and from the proof of 8. Let us prove 2). If \(\mathscr{ F}\) is a constructible sheaf of sets on \(U\) satisfying 4 a) or 4 b), it follows from 4 2) that \(i_*\mathscr{ F}\) is constructible; the same is therefore true of \(q_*\mathscr{ F}=p_*(i_*\mathscr{ F})\) (SGA 4 XIV 1.1).

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a constructible sheaf of groups on \(U\) satisfying 4 a) or 4 b), and let us prove that \(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{t}}^1q_*\mathscr{ F}\) is constructible. By passage to the limit (EGA IV 8.10.5 and 17.7.8) and using 1), one may assume \(S\) Noetherian. Let then \(\Phi\) be the stack on \(X\) whose fibre at every scheme \(X^{'}\) étale over \(X\) is formed of the torsors on \(U^{'}=U\times_{X}X^{'}\), with group \(\mathscr{F}|U\), which are tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\); one thus has

\[ S(i_*^{\mathrm{t}}\Phi)\simeq \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{t}}^1i_*\mathscr{ F} \]

and this sheaf is constructible according to 4 2). It therefore follows from 3 below that \(S(p_*\Phi)\) is constructible, i.e. that \(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}q_*\mathscr{ F}\) is constructible.

Finally, if \(\mathscr{ F}\) is a stack on \(U\) which is locally over \(X\) and \(S\) the inverse image of a constructible stack on \(S,\) \(i_*^{\mathrm{t}}\mathscr{ F}\) is constructible, and the same is therefore true of \(q_*^{\mathrm{t}}\mathscr{ F}=p_*(i_*^{\mathrm{t}}\mathscr{ F})\). If moreover \(S\) is locally Noetherian and \(SG\) constructible, \(S(i_*^{\mathrm{t}}\mathscr{ F})\) is constructible according to 6.3; the same is therefore true of \(S(q_*i_*^{\mathrm{t}}\mathscr{ F})\), i.e. of \(S(q_*^{\mathrm{t}}\mathscr{ F})\) 2.

Corollary 8

Let

\[ \xymatrix{{U}\ar[r]^{i}\ar[d]_{g}&{X}\ar[ld]^{f}\\{S}} \]

be a commutative diagram of schemes, in which \(U\) is the open complement in \(X\) of a divisor with normal crossings relatively to \(S\), \(f\) a proper morphism of finite presentation. Let \(\mathbb{L}\) be a set of prime numbers. Suppose \(g\) locally \(0\)-acyclic (resp. locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\)). Then, if \(\mathscr{ F}\) is a sheaf of sets on \(U\) (resp. a sheaf of \(\mathbb{L}\)-groups) on \(U,\) locally constant constructible, tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\), \(f_*\mathscr{ F}\) (resp. \(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}f_*\mathscr{ F}\)) is locally constant constructible and \((\mathscr{ F},f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. the formation of \(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}f_*\mathscr{ F}\) commutes with every base change \(S^{'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\)). In the non-resp. case, if \(\mathscr{ F}\) is a sheaf of groups, for every specialization \(\overline{s}_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{s}_2\) of geometric points of \(S\), the specialization morphism

\[ (\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}f_*\mathscr{ F})_{\overline{s}_2}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}f_*\mathscr{ F})_{\overline{s}_1} \]

is injective.

The corollary follows immediately from 6 and from 14 (resp. from the analogue of 14 for the \(\mathrm{R}^1_{\mathrm{t}}f_*\mathscr{ F}\), which is proved as in loc. cit. ).

Corollary 9

Let

\[ \xymatrix{{U}\ar[r]^{i}\ar[d]_{g}&{X}\ar[ld]^{f}\\{S}} \]

be a commutative diagram of schemes, in which \(U\) is the open complement in \(X\) of a divisor with normal crossings relatively to \(S\), \(f\) a proper smooth morphism of finite presentation. Let \(\mathbb{L} \) be the set of prime numbers distinct from the residue characteristics of \(S\). Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathbb{L} \)-groups locally constant constructible on \(U\), tamely ramified over \(X\) relatively to \(S\). Then \(\mathrm{R}^1 f_*\mathscr{ F}\) is locally constant constructible and \((\mathscr{ F},f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\).

The corollary follows from 8 and from the fact that one has \(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{t}}^1f_*\mathscr{ F}=\mathrm{R}^1f_*\mathscr{ F}\) (3 b)).

10

If \(U\) is a connected scheme, \(a\) a geometric point of \(U\), \(\mathbb{L}\) a set of prime numbers, one denotes by

\[ \tag{2.10.0} \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(U,a) \]

the inverse limit of the finite quotients of \(\pi_1(U,a)\) whose orders have all their prime factors in \(\mathbb{L}\).

We are going to define specialization morphisms for the fundamental group, generalizing X.2.

Let \(g\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a coherent morphism with geometrically connected fibres (resp. a morphism of the form \(g= fi\), where \(f\colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a proper morphism of finite presentation and where \(i\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is an open immersion such that \(U\) is the complement in \(X\) of a divisor with normal crossings relatively to \(S\) (cf. 8)). Let \({\mathbb{L}}\) be a set of prime numbers and suppose, in the non-resp. case, that, for every constant finite \({\mathbb{L}}\)-group \(C\), \((C_U,g)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\). Let \(\overline{s}_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \overline{s}_2\) be a specialization morphism of geometric points of \(S\), \(\overline{S}\) the strict localization of \(S\) at \(\overline{s}_2\), \(\overline{U}=U\times_S\overline{S}\). One has a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{U_{\overline{s}_1}}\ar[r]^{h_1}\ar[d]&{\overline{U}}\ar[d]^{\overline{g}}&{U_{\overline{s}_1}}\ar[l]_{h_2}\ar[d]\\{\overline{s}_1}\ar[r]&{\overline{S}}&{\,\overline{s}_2\,.}\ar[l]} \]

If \(a_1\) is a geometric point of \(U_{\overline{s}_1},\ a_2\) a geometric point of \(U_{\overline{s}_2}\) the morphisms \(h_1\) and \(h_2\) define canonical morphisms

\begin{align*} \pi_1\colon \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(U_{\overline{s}_1},a_1)&\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(\overline{U},a_1) & \pi_2\colon \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(U_{\overline{s}_2},a_2)&\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(\overline{U},a_2)\\ (\text{resp. ~} \pi_1\colon \pi_1^\mathrm{t} (U_{\overline{s}_1},a_1)&\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1^\mathrm{t} (\overline{U},a_1) & \pi_2\colon \pi_1^\mathrm{t}(U_{\overline{s}_2},a_2)&\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1^\mathrm{t}(\overline{U},a_2)) \end{align*}

(V 7 and 1.5.2). The hypotheses of cohomological properness (resp. 8) prove that \(\pi_2\) is an isomorphism. If one chooses a class of paths from \(a_1\) to \(a_2\), one obtains an isomorphism

\[ \pi_{12}\colon \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(\overline{U},a_1)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(\overline{U},a_2) \quad (\text{resp. ~} \pi_{12}\colon \pi_1^\mathrm{t} (\overline{U},a_1)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \pi_1^\mathrm{t}(\overline{U},a_2)) \]

whence a morphism \(\pi=\pi_2^{-1}\pi_{12}\pi_1\)

\[ \pi\colon \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(U_{\overline{s}_1},a_1)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(U_{\overline{s}_2},a_2) \quad (\text{resp. ~}\pi\colon \pi_1^\mathrm{t} (U_{\overline{s}_1},a_1)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1^\mathrm{t} (U_{\overline{s}_2},a_2)). \]

Changing the class of paths from \(a_1\) to \(a_2\) amounts to modifying \(\pi\) by an inner automorphism of \(\pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}_2},a_2)\) (resp. of \(\pi_1^\mathrm{t}(X_{\overline{s}_2},a_2)\)).
