Let \(X_{1\overline{s}}\) be the normalization of \((X_{\overline{s}})_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\); after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open and making a radicial extension of \(S\), we may assume that \(X_{1\overline{s}}\) comes from a scheme \(X_1\) normal over \(S\), and that the morphism \(X_{1\overline{s}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{\overline{s}}\) comes from a surjective finite morphism \(p\colon X_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) (EGA IV 8.8.2 and 9.6.1). Suppose the theorem proved for \(fp\). After possibly restricting \(S\) to an open, we may assume that, for every constant sheaf of sets \(F\) on \(X\), \((p^*F,fp)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and that \(f_*p_*(p^*F)\) is constructible. By 9, \((p_*p^*F,f)\) is then cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). The morphism

\[ F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} p_* p^* F=G \]

is a monomorphism. It already follows from this, \(f_*F\) being a subsheaf of \(f_*G\), that \(f_*F\) is constructible (SGA 4 IX 2.9 (ii)) and that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\).

Let \(X_2=X_1 \times_{X} X_1\), \(q \colon X_2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism. By 11 1), we have an exact sequence

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{F}\ar[r]&{G}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{q_*q^*F}}. \]

By what we have just proved, applied to \(fq\) instead of \(f\), we may assume, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open, that, for every constant sheaf of sets \(F\) on \(X\), \((q^*F,fq)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), hence that \((q_*q^*F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\). It then follows from 13 1) that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

1) 2. Reduction to the case where \(X\) is normal affine over \(S\).

Let \(U_s\) be an affine open of \(X_s\) dense in \(X_s\). After possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open, we may assume that \(U_s \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_s\) lifts to an open immersion \(i\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), schematically dominant relatively to \(S\) (EGA IV 8.9.1). Since the morphism \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is normal, we have by SGA 2 XIV 1.18:

\[ \operatorname{prof} \mathrm{\acute{e}t}_{S- U} (X) \geqslant 2 ; \]

consequently, for every constant sheaf \(F\) on \(X\), the canonical morphism

\[ F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*i^*F \]

is an isomorphism. It follows that, if we suppose the theorem proved for \(fi\) and \(i\), then, after restriction of \(S\) to a non-empty open, \((i^*F,i)\) and \((i^*F,fi)\) are cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). The same is therefore true of \((F,f)\) (6 2)). Since moreover \(f_* F=(fi)_*(i^*F)\) is constructible, this completes the reduction.

1) 3. End of the proof.

We may assume \(S\) normal (EGA IV 7.8.3). One can find a compactification of \(X_s\):

\[ \xymatrix{{X_s}\ar[r]^{j_s}\ar[d]_{f_s}&{P_s}\ar[ld]^{g_s}\\{Y_s}} \]

where \(j_s\) is a dominant open immersion and \(g_s\) a proper morphism; after possibly making a radicial extension of \(\mathit{k}(s)\) and replacing \(P_s\) by its normalization, which does not change \(X_s\), we may assume \(P_s\) geometrically normal. After possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open and making a surjective radicial extension, we may assume that the diagram above comes from a diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{X}\ar[r]^{j}\ar[d]_{f}&{P}\ar[ld]^{g}\\{Y}} \]

where \(P\) is a scheme normal over \(S\), \(j\) a schematically dominant open immersion relatively to \(S\) and \(g\) a proper morphism (EGA IV 6.9.1, 9.9.4 and 9.6.1). For every finite constant sheaf of sets \(F\) on \(X\) of value \(C\), \(j_*F\) is the constant sheaf of value \(C\) (SGA 4 2.14.1), and the same holds after every base change \(S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\); it follows that \((F,j)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). The same is therefore true of \((F,f)\), since \(g\) is proper (8). Since \(g\) is proper \(f_*F=g_*C_P\) is constructible, which completes the proof of 1) a).

2) Case of a constructible sheaf of sets

Let \(F\) be a constructible sheaf of sets on \(X\). By SGA 4 IX 2.14 (ii), one can find a finite family of morphisms \(p_i \colon Z_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), and, on each \(Z_i\), a finite constant sheaf of sets \(C_i\), so that one has a monomorphism

\[ j \colon F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \prod_{i} {p_i}_* C_i =G . \]

By 1) a), we may assume, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open, that the \((C_i,fp_i)\) are cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), and that the \(f_*{p_i}_*C_i\) are constructible. We already conclude that \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (9), hence that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), and that \(f_*F\) is constructible. Let \(K\) be the amalgamated sum \(K= G \coprod_{F} G\); since \(F\) and \(G\) are constructible, so is \(K\). We therefore conclude from the foregoing that, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open, we may assume that \((K,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\). It then follows from 13 1) that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

3) Case of constant sheaves of groups

If \(F\) is a constant sheaf of groups on \(X\), we denote by \(\Phi\) the stack of torsors under \(F\).

3) 1. Let us first show that, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, for every constant sheaf of groups \(F\) on \(X\), of order prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\), \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), and that \(f_*\Phi\) is constructible.

For this we reduce to the case where \(X\) is smooth over \(S\). After possibly making a finite extension of \(\mathit{k}(s)\), which is permissible since we can consider it as composed of an étale extension and a radicial extension, we can find a surjective proper morphism \(p_s \colon X_{1s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_s\), where \(X_{1s}\) is a scheme smooth over \(S\) of the same dimension as \(X_s\), and, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, we can assume that \(p_s\) comes from a surjective proper morphism \(p \colon X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), where \(X_1\) is a scheme smooth over \(S\) (EGA IV 9.6.1 and 12.1.6). Let \(X_2=X_1 \times_X X_1\), \(q \colon X_2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism. We have an exact diagram of stacks over \(X\)

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]&{p_*p^* \Phi}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{q_*q^*\Phi}} \]

(11 2)). The theorem being assumed proved in the smooth case, we see first of all that we can assume \(f_*p_*p^*\Phi\) constructible; the same therefore holds for \(f_*\Phi\) (1.1 below). Moreover, according to 6 2), we can assume \((p_*p^*\Phi,f)\) cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\); it follows that \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\). We can therefore assume that \((q_*q^*\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), and this entails that \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (12 1)).

We then reduce, as in 1)2, to the case where \(X\) is smooth and affine over \(S\). Let then

\[ \xymatrix{{X}\ar[r]^{i}\ar[d]_{f}&{P}\ar[ld]^{q}\\{Y}} \]

be a compactification of \(X\), where \(i\) is a dominant open immersion and \(q\) a proper morphism. Since we have \(\dim P_s=\dim X_s\), we can apply the hypothesis of resolution of singularities to \(P_{\overline s}\). After possibly making an étale extension and a radicial extension of \(S\), we can find a proper morphism \(r \colon Z \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P\), where \(Z\) is smooth over \(S\), \(r^{-1}(X)\simeq X\), and where \(r^{-1}(X)\) is the complement in \(Z\) of a divisor with normal crossings relative to \(S\). Every torsor under \(F\) is then tamely ramified on \(Z\) relative to \(S\) (3 b)). It follows therefore from 7 that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), which proves our assertion.

[4] 3) 2. Reduction to the case where \(X\) is smooth over \(S\).

After possibly making a finite extension of \(\mathit{k}(s)\), we can find a surjective proper morphism \(p_s\colon X_{1s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), where \(X_{1s}\) is a scheme smooth over \(s\), and we can assume that \(p_s\) comes from a surjective proper morphism \(p\colon X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), where \(X_1\) is smooth over \(S\). Suppose the theorem proved for \(fp\) and let us prove it for \(f\). Let \(F\) be a finite constant sheaf of groups on \(X\), of order prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\), and \(\Phi\) the stack of torsors under \(F\). Let \(X_2=X_1 \times_X X_1\), \(X_3=X_1 \times_X X_1 \times_X X_1\), \(q\colon X_2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), \(r\colon X_3 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphisms. According to 11 2), we have an exact diagram of stacks

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]&{p_*p^*\Phi}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{q_*q^*\Phi}\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]&{r_*r^*\Phi}}. \]

To prove that \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), it suffices to show that the same holds for \((p_*p^*\Phi,f)\), that \((q_*q^*\Phi, f )\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and that \((r_*r^*\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (12 2)). According to 3) 1 above we can assume that, for every finite constant sheaf of groups \(F\), \((q^*\Phi,fq)\), \((q^*\Phi,q)\), \((r^*\Phi,fr)\), \((r^*\Phi,r)\) are cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). It then follows from 6 2) that \((q_*q^*\Phi,f)\) and \((r_*r^*\Phi,f)\) are cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). The theorem being assumed proved in the smooth case, \((p^*\Phi, fp)\) and \((p^*\Phi,p)\), hence also \((p_*p^*\Phi,f)\) (6 2)), are cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\). This indeed shows that \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\).

Moreover \(f_*p_*p^*\Phi\) is \(1\)-constructible by hypothesis; according to 1 we can assume that \(f_*q_*q^*\Phi\) is constructible; it follows therefore from 1.1 below that \(f_*\Phi\) is \(1\)-constructible.

3) 3. Reduction to the case where \(X\) is smooth affine over \(S\).

According to 3)2, we can assume \(X\) smooth over \(S\). Let \((U_i)_{i\in I}\) be a finite covering of \(X\) by affine open sets, and let \(X_1\) be the direct sum of the \(U_i\), \(p\colon X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism. Since \(p\) is a morphism of effective descent for the category of étale sheaves of finite type on variable schemes, we see as in 3)2 that, if we assume the theorem proved for the \(U_i\), i.e. for \(X_1\), it is also true for \(X\).

3) 4. Case where \(X\) is smooth affine over \(S\).

We see as in 3)1 that, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set and making a surjective étale extension and a surjective radicial extension of \(S\), we can find a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{X}\ar[r]^{i}\ar[d]_{f}&{P}\ar[ld]^{q}\\{Y}} \]

where \(P\) is a scheme smooth over \(S\), \(X\) the complement in \(P\) of a divisor with normal crossings relative to \(S\) and \(g\) a proper morphism. If \(F\) is a constant sheaf of order prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\), every torsor under \(F\) is tamely ramified on \(P\) relative to \(S\) (3 b)). The fact that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\) and that \(f_*\Phi\) is constructible then follows from 7.

4) Proof of 2) b)

4) 1. Case where \(\Phi\) is a gerbe.

We can find a surjective étale morphism of finite type \(p \colon X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), such that \(p^*\Phi\) is a trivial gerbe. By descent, as in 3)2, we see that it suffices to prove the theorem for \(X_1\), \(X_1\times_X X_1\) and \(X_1\times_X X_1\times_X X_1\). We are therefore reduced to the case where \(\Phi\) is the gerbe of torsors under a constructible sheaf of groups \(F\), whose fibres are of order prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\).

According to SGA 4 IX 2.14, we can find a finite family of finite morphisms \(p_i \colon Z_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), and, for each \(i\), a finite constant sheaf of groups \(C_i\), of order prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\), such that we have a monomorphism

\[ j\colon F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \prod_{i} p_{i*}C_i=G . \]

Let \(\Phi_i\) be the stack of torsors under \(C_i\) and \(\Psi\) the stack of torsors under \(G\). It follows from 2) a) that, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, we can assume that the \((C_i,fp_i)\) are cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), and that the stacks \(f_*p_{i*}\Phi_i\) are \(1\)-constructible. It then follows from 9 that the \((p_{i*}C_i,f)\) are cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\); the same therefore holds for \((G,f)\). Moreover, since \(p_{i*}\Phi_i\) is equivalent to the stack of torsors under the group \(p_{i*}C_i\) (SGA 4 VIII 5.8), we see that \(f_*\Psi\) is \(1\)-constructible.

Since \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*G\) is constructible, we can find a sheaf representable by an étale \(Y\)-scheme of finite type \(T\) and an epimorphism

\[ a \colon T \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^1f_*G \]

(SGA 4 IX 2.7); moreover we can assume that the image of the identical section of \(T(T)\) is defined by a torsor \(Q\) on \(X\times_Y T=X_T\), with group \(G|X_T\). Let \(f_T \colon X_T \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) be the canonical morphism, \(F_T=F|X_T\), etc. According to 1) b) we can assume, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, that \((Q/F_T,f_T)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and that \(f_{T*}(Q/F_T)\) is constructible. It then follows from 1.2 that \(f_*\Psi\) is constructible.

Let us show that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\). By 13 2) it suffices to prove that, for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\) and for every torsor \(Q_1\) over \(X_1=X\times_Y Y_1\), if \(f_1 \colon X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_1\) is the canonical morphism, then \((Q_1/F_1,f_1)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). Now, by definition of \(T\), \(Q_1\) is, locally for the étale topology of \(Y_1\), the inverse image of \(Q\), which proves our reduction.

4) 2. General case.

Using lemma 1.1, 4)1 and 1) a) one sees that, after restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, one may assume \(S(f_*\Phi)\) constructible and \((S\Phi,f)\) cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). One may then find a sheaf representable by a finite-type étale \(Y\)-scheme \(T\) and an epimorphism

\[ a \colon T \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S(f_*\Phi). \]

We resume the notations of 4)1, and we set further \(Z=T\times_Y T\), \(X_Z=X\times_Y Z\) and we denote by \(f_Z \colon X_Z \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) the canonical morphism. One may assume \(T\) chosen so that the image \(q\) of the identity section of \(T(T)\) under \(a\) is defined by an object \(p\) of \((f_*\Phi)_T=\Phi_{X_T}\). Let \(p_1\) and \(p_2\) (resp. \(q_1\) and \(q_2\)) be the inverse images of \(p\) (resp. \(q\)) under the two projections of \(Z\) onto \(T\). One may assume, after restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, that \((\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{X_T}(p),f_T)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), that \((\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{X_Z}(p_1,p_2),f_Z)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), and that \(f_{T*}(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{X_T}(p))\), \(f_{Z*}(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{X_Z}(p_1,p_2))\) are constructible ( a)1) and 4)1)).

One first deduces from this that, for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\) and for every pair of objects \(y\), \(y_1\) of \((f_*\Phi)_{Y_1}\), the sheaf \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{Y_1}(y)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{Y_1}(y,y_1)\)) is constructible, such a sheaf being, locally for the étale topology of \(Y_1\), the inverse image of \(f_{T*}(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{X_T}(p))\) (resp. of \(f_{Z*}(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{X_Z}(p_1,p_2))\)).

It remains to prove that \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\). For this it suffices to show that, for every \(S'\)-scheme \(S\) and for every geometric point \(\overline{y}'\) of \(Y\), if one denotes by \(\overline{Y}\) the strict localization of \(Y\) at \(y'\), \(\overline{X}=X \times_Y \overline{Y}\), etc., then the canonical functor

\[ \overline{\varphi} \colon \Phi(\overline{X}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Phi'(\overline{X'}) \]

is an equivalence of categories.

Let us show that \(\overline{\varphi}\) is fully faithful. Let \(x\), \(y \in \Phi(\overline{X})\), \(x'\), \(y'\) their images in \(\Phi'(\overline{X'})\) and let us show that the canonical morphism

\[ \tag{*} {\operatorname{Hom}_{\overline{X}}(x,y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\overline{X'}}(x',y') } \]

is bijective. By definition of \(T\) there exist two morphisms of \(\overline{Y}\) into \(T\) such that \(x\) and \(y\) are the inverse images of \(p\) under these two morphisms. This amounts to saying that there is a morphism \(\overline {Y} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\), whence a morphism \(h\colon \overline{X} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_Z\) such that one has

\[ h^*(p_1)=x \qquad h^*(p_2)=y. \]

Consequently one has a canonical isomorphism

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\overline{X}}(x,y)=h^*(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{X_Z}(p_1,p_2) ). \]

but, taking into account the fact that \((\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{X_Z}(p_1,p_2),f_Z)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), one sees that the same holds for \((\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\overline{X}}(x,y),\overline{f})\), which proves that the morphism (*) is bijective.

Let us show that \(\overline{\varphi}\) is essentially surjective. Let \(x'\in \Phi'(\overline{X'})\). Since \((S\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X},\overline{S\Phi}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X'},\overline{S\Phi}') \]

is bijective. Let \(G'\) be the maximal subgerbe of \(\Phi'\) generated by \(x'\); there then exists a maximal subgerbe \(G\) of \(\overline{\Phi}\) whose inverse image on \(\overline{X'}\) is \(G'\). By 4)1 \((G,\overline{f})\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\); consequently the canonical functor

\[ G(\overline{X}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G'(\overline{X'}) \]

is an equivalence of categories, which proves the existence of an element \(x\) of \(\Phi(\overline{X})\) whose image in \(\Phi'(X')\) is isomorphic to \(x'\) and completes the proof of the theorem.

Lemma 1.1

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian scheme and

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]^p&{\Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[rr]^{p_1,p_2}\ar@<-2pt>[rr]&&{\Phi_2}} \]

an exact diagram of stacks over \(S\) (10.1). If \(\Phi_1\) is constructible, so is \(\Phi\). If, for every scheme \(S'\) étale over \(S\) and for every pair of objects \(x_1,~y_1\) of \((\Phi_1)_{S'}\) the sheaf \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{S'}(x_1,y_1)\) is constructible, then, for every pair of objects \(x,~y\) of \(\Phi_{S'}\), the same holds for \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{S'}(x,y)\). Suppose that \(\Phi_1\) is \(1\)-constructible (0) and that \(\Phi_2\) is constructible, then \(\Phi\) is \(1\)-constructible.

For every scheme \(S'\) étale over \(S\) and for every object \(x\) of \(\Phi_{S'}\), one has a monomorphism

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{S'}(x)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{S'}(p(x)). \]

It therefore follows from SGA 4 IX 2.9 that, if \(\Phi_1\) is constructible, so is \(\Phi\), and the second assertion of the lemma is proved in the same way.

Suppose now \(\Phi_1\) \(1\)-constructible and \(\Phi_2\) constructible. The morphism \(p\) induces on the sheaves of maximal subgerbes a morphism

\[ \varphi\colon S\Phi\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\Phi_1. \]

Let \(G\) be the image of \(S\Phi\) under \(\varphi\); by SGA 4 IX 2.9, \(G\) is a constructible sheaf. One may therefore find a sheaf representable by a finite-type étale \(S\)-scheme \(T\) and an epimorphism

\[ a\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G \]

(SGA 4 IX 2.7). Moreover one may choose \(T\) so that the image \(y\) of the identity section of \(T(T)\) under \(a\) is defined by an object \(x_1\) of \((\Phi_1)_T\) of the form \(x_1=p(x)\), where \(x\in\Phi_T\).

It suffices to show that, for every point \(s\) of \(S\), there exists a non-empty open set \(U\) of \(\overline{\{ s\} }\) such that \(S\Phi|U\) is locally constant constructible. Let \(s\in S\), \(\overline{s}\) a geometric point over \(s\) and \(\overline{y}_1,\dots,\overline{y}_n\) the elements of \(G_{\overline{s}}\). By definition of \(T\) there exist morphisms \(h_i\colon\overline{s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) such that one has \(h_i^*(y)=\overline{y}_i\). Let \(S'\) be the fibre product over \(S\) of \(n\) schemes isomorphic to \(T\), \(h\colon\overline{s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) the fibre product of the \(h_i\), \(y_i\) (resp. \(x_i\)) the inverse image of \(y\) (resp. \(x\)) under the \(i\)-th projection of \(S'\) onto \(T\). Let \(F_i\) be the subsheaf of \(S\Phi |S'\) inverse image of \(y_i\) and let us show that the \(F_i\) are constructible. The sheaf \(F_i\) is a quotient of the sheaf \(F'_i\) such that, for every scheme \(S''\) étale over \(S'\), one has

\begin{multline*} F'_i(S'') = \{\text{classes mod. isomorphism of objects $z$ of $\Phi_{S''}$ equipped}\\ \text{ with an isomorphism $i\colon p(z)\simeq p(x_i|S'')$}\}. \end{multline*}

It suffices to show that the \(F_i'\) are constructible. Now, if one sets \(z_i=p_1p(x_i)\), \(z_i'=p_2p(x_i)\), one has a monomorphism

\[ \Psi_i\colon F'_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{\mathbf{Isom}}_{S'}(z_i,z_i'), \]

obtained by associating, to every scheme \(S''\) étale over \(S'\) and to every object \(z\) of \(\Phi_{S''}\) such that one has an isomorphism \(i\colon p(z)\simeq p(x_i|S'')\), the isomorphism of \(z_i\) onto \(z_i'\) defined by the condition that the diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=1.2cm{{p_1p(z)}\ar[r]^-{p_1(i)}\ar[d]_-j&{z_i}\ar[d]\\{p_2p(z)}\ar[r]^-{p_2(i)}&{z'_i}} \]

be commutative (\(j\) is the canonical morphism associated with the exact diagram).

The morphism \(\Psi_i\) is injective, for to say that two objects \(z,~z'\), such that we have isomorphisms \(i\colon p(z)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p(x_i|S''),~i'\colon p(z')\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p(x_i|S'')\), define the same element of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Isom}}_{S''}(z_i,z'_i)\) amounts to saying that we have \(p_1(i'^{-1}i)=p_2(i'^{-1}i)\), i.e. that \(i'^{-1}i\) comes from an isomorphism \(z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} z'\). The sheaf \(F'_i\), being a subsheaf of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Isom}}_{S'}(z_i,z'_i)\), is constructible.

Since one can find a non-empty open set \(U\) of \(\overline{\{ s\}}\) such that \(y_1|U,\dots,y_n|U\) generate \(G\), it follows from lemma 1.2 below that \(S\Phi |U\) is constructible, hence, after possibly shrinking \(U\), \(S\Phi |U\) is locally constant constructible.

Lemma 1.2

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian scheme, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism, \(F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) a monomorphism of sheaves of groups on \(X\), \(\Psi\) (resp. \(\Psi_1\)) the stack of torsors under \(F\) (resp. under \(G\)), \(\Phi=f_*\Psi\), \(\Phi_1=f_*\Psi_1\). Suppose given a sheaf on \(S\) representable by an étale \(S\)-scheme of finite type \(T\) and a surjective morphism

\[ a\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\Phi_1\simeq\mathrm{R}^1f_*G, \]

so that there exists a torsor \(Q\) on \(X_T=X\times_ST\) with group \(G|X_T\) which defines in \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*G(T)\) the image under \(a\) of the identity section of \(T(T)\). Let \(f_T\colon X_T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) be the canonical morphism and set \(F_T=F|X_T\). Suppose that \(\Phi_1\) is \(1\)-constructible and that \(f_{T*}(Q/F_T)\) is constructible. Then \(\Phi\) is \(1\)-constructible.

It suffices indeed to copy the proof of 1.1, the fact that we have morphisms \(\Psi_i\) being replaced by the fact that we have isomorphisms

\[ F'_i\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} f_*(Q/F_T)|S'. \]
Remark 1.3

Suppose that \(k=\mathit{k}(s)\) is of characteristic zero; then the schemes of finite type over \(\overline{k}\) of dimension \(\leqslant\dim X\) are strongly desingularizable, and the proof of 1 allows one to prove the following results:

  1. a)

    There exists a non-empty open set \(S_1\) of \(S\) such that, for every scheme \(S'\) over \(S_1\) whose maximal points are of characteristic zero and for every locally constant constructible sheaf of sets \(F\) on \(X'=X\times_SS'\), \((F,f')\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

  2. b)

    If all the residue characteristics of \(S\) are zero, there exists a non-empty open set \(S_1\) of \(S\) such that, for every scheme \(S'\) over \(S_1\) and for every locally constant constructible sheaf of groups \(F\) on \(X'\), \((F,f')\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant1\).

It suffices indeed to copy the proof of 1.2) a). The proposition 7 used in 3) 4 applies to the case of a locally constant sheaf \(F\), for, resuming the notation of 3)4, every torsor under \(F\) is tamely ramified over \(P\) relative to \(S\) since all the residue characteristics of \(S\) are zero.

Corollary 2

Let \(k\) be a field of characteristic \(p\geqslant 0\), \(p'\) the set of prime numbers distinct from \(p\), \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k\) a coherent morphism.

  1. 1)

    For every sheaf of sets \(F\), \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

  2. 2)

    Suppose that one of the two following conditions is satisfied:

    1. a)

      \(f\) is of finite type and the schemes of finite type of dimension \(\leqslant\dim X\) over an algebraic closure of \(k\) are strongly desingularizable.

    2. b)

      The schemes of finite type over an algebraic closure of \(k\) are strongly desingularizable.

    Then, for every sheaf of ind-\(p'\)-groups \(F\), \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 1\).

Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets (resp. of ind-\(p'\)-groups). By SGA 4 IX 2.7.2 one can write \(F\) as a filtered direct limit

\[ F=\varinjlim F_i, \]

where the \(F_i\) are constructible sheaves of sets (resp. of ind-\(p'\)-groups). Since \(f\) is coherent, \(f_*\) (resp. \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*\)) commutes with direct limits (SGA 4 VII 3.3). If one knows that the \((F_i,f)\) are cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. that every sheaf of sets is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and that the \((F_i,f)\) are cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 1\)), the same will hold for \((F,f)\). We may therefore suppose \(F\) constructible.

If we suppose \(f\) of finite type (resp. satisfying a)), the proposition follows from 1 1) b) (resp. from 1 2) b)). Let us now prove 2 when we no longer suppose \(f\) of finite type. For every scheme \(S'\) over \(k\) and for every geometric point \(\overline{s}\) of \(S'\), we denote by \(\overline{k}\) (resp. \(\overline{S'}\)) the strict localization of \(k\) at \(\overline{s}\) (resp. of \(S'\) at \(\overline{s}\)), \(\overline{X}\) the inverse image of \(X\) over \(\overline{k}\), and we consider the cartesian square

\[ \xymatrix@C=1.2cm{{\overline{X}}\ar[d]_-{\overline{f}}&{\overline{X'}}\ar[l]_g\ar[d]^-{\overline{f}'}\\{\overline{k}}&{\overline{S'}}\ar[l]} \]

It suffices to prove that, for every \(S'\), for every \(\overline{s}\), the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X},\overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X'},\overline{F'})\qquad \text{(resp. $\mathrm{H}^1(\overline{X},\overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(\overline{X'},\overline{F'})$)} \]

is an isomorphism. It suffices to show that we have the relations

\[ \tag{*} \overline{F}\simeq g_*g^*\overline{F}\qquad \text{(resp. $\mathrm{R}^1g_*(g^*\overline{F})=0$).} \]

Now, under this form, the question is local on \(X\) for the étale topology. We may therefore suppose \(X\) affine; by passage to the limit we may suppose \(X\) of finite type over \(k\). One then knows that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. \(\leqslant 1\)) and that the same holds when one replaces \(X\) by an étale scheme of finite type over \(X\), which proves (*).

Theorem 3

Let \(S\) be an irreducible scheme with generic point \(s\), \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism of finite presentation. Suppose that the schemes of finite type of dimension \(\leqslant\dim X_s\) over an algebraic closure \(\overline{k}\) of \(k\) are desingularizable (EGA IV 7.9.1). Then, if \(\mathbb{L}\) denotes the set of prime numbers distinct from the residue characteristics of \(S\), one can find a non-empty open set \(S_1\) of \(S\) such that the morphism \(f|S_1\) is universally locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\).

We may suppose \(S\) integral and \(X\) reduced (SGA 4 VIII 1.1). By passage to the limit we may suppose \(S\) Noetherian. Moreover, to prove the theorem, it suffices to do so after a finite extension \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), where \(S_1\) is an integral scheme and where \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is composed of étale extensions and surjective radicial extensions.

Let us first show that, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, \(f\) is universally locally \(0\)-acyclic. After possibly making a radicial extension of \(\mathit{k}(s)\), we may suppose that the morphism \((X_s)_\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} s\) is separable; we may therefore suppose, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set and making a surjective radicial extension of \(S\), that the morphism \(f\) is flat, with separable geometric fibres (EGA IV 12.1.1), which entails that \(f\) is universally \(0\)-acyclic (SGA 4 XV 4.1).

Let us show that, up to restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, \(f\) is universally locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\). Up to making a finite extension of \(\mathit{k}(s)\), which is allowable since it can be regarded as compounded of an étale extension and a radicial extension, one can find a proper surjective morphism \(p_s\colon Y_s\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_s\), where \(Y_s\) is a scheme smooth over \(S\), of the same dimension as \(X_s\), and one may suppose that \(p_s\) comes from a proper surjective morphism \(p\colon Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), where \(Y\) is a scheme smooth over \(S\) (EGA IV 9.6.1 and 12.1.6). It suffices to show that, up to restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, for every diagram with cartesian squares

\[ \xymatrix{{S''}\ar[d]_i&{X ''}\ar[d]^j\ar[l]_{f''}\\{S'}\ar[d]&{X'}\ar[d]\ar[l]_{f'}\\{S}&{X,}\ar[l]_f} \]

where \(i\) is étale of finite presentation, and for every sheaf of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups \(F\) on \(S''\), if \(\Phi\) is the stack of torsors under \(F\), then the canonical morphism

\[ {f'}^*i_*\Phi \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} j_*{f''}^*\Phi \]

is an equivalence. Let \(Z=Y\times_{X}Y, T=Y\times_{X}Y\times_{X}Y\). We have in a natural way a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{S''}\ar[d]_i&{X''}\ar[l]_{f''}\ar[d]_j&{Y''}\ar[l]_{p''}\ar[d]&{Z''}\ar[d]\ar@<-.4ex>[l]\ar@<.4ex>[l]&{T''}\ar[d]\ar[l]\ar@<-.8ex>[l]\ar@<.8ex>[l]\\{S'}\ar[d]&{X'}\ar[l]_{f'}\ar[d]&{Y'}\ar[d]\ar[l]_{p'}&{Z'}\ar[d]\ar@<-.4ex>[l]\ar@<.4ex>[l]&{T'}\ar[d]\ar[l]\ar@<-.8ex>[l]\ar@<.8ex>[l]\\{S}&{X}\ar[l]_f&{Y}\ar[l]_p&{Z'}\ar@<-.4ex>[l]\ar@<.4ex>[l]&{T'}\ar[l]\ar@<-.8ex>[l]\ar@<.8ex>[l]} \]

Let \(q\colon Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and \(r\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be the canonical morphisms, the notations \(q', r', q'', r''\) having an evident meaning. By 11 2), we have the following essentially commutative diagram, whose rows are exact:

\[ \xymatrix{{f'^* i_*\Phi}\ar[r]\ar[d]_a&{p'_*p'^*(f'^*i_*\Phi)}\ar[d]_b\ar@<-.4ex>[r]\ar@<.4ex>[r]&{q'_*q'^*(f'^*i_*\Phi)}\ar[d]_c\ar[r]\ar@<-.8ex>[r]\ar@<.8ex>[r]&{r'_*r'^*(f'^*i_*\Phi)}\ar[d]_d\\{j_*f''^*\Phi}\ar[r]&{j_*p''_*p''^*(f''^*\Phi)}\ar@<-.4ex>[r]\ar@<.4ex>[r]&{j_*q''_*q''^*(f''^*\Phi)}\ar[r]\ar@<-.8ex>[r]\ar@<.8ex>[r]&{j_*r''_*r''^*(f''^*\Phi)}} \]

Since \(Y\) is smooth over \(S\), the morphism \(fp\) is universally locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\) (SGA XV 2.1), and it follows from [2] that \(b\) is an equivalence of categories. On the other hand, up to restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, one may suppose that the morphisms \(Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) and \(T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) are universally locally \(0\)-acyclic. It follows that the functors \(c\) and \(d\) are fully faithful, and the diagram above then shows that \(a\) is an equivalence, which completes the proof.

Corollary 4

Let \(k\) be a field of characteristic \(p\geqslant0\), \(p'\) the set of prime numbers distinct from \(p\), \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k\) a coherent morphism. Suppose one of the two following conditions is satisfied:

  1. a)

    \(f\) is of finite type and the schemes of finite type of dimension \(\leqslant\dim X\) over an algebraic closure of \(k\) are desingularizable.

  2. b)

    The schemes of finite type over an algebraic closure of \(k\) are desingularizable.

Then \(f\) is universally locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(p'\).

Case a) follows from 3. In case b), the question being local on \(X\), one may suppose \(X\) affine; by passage to the limit (SGA 4 XV 1.3), one reduces to the case where \(X\) is of finite type over \(k\).

Corollary 5

Let \(S\) be an irreducible scheme with generic point \(s\), \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism of finite presentation. Suppose that the schemes of finite type of dimension \(\leqslant\dim X_s\) over an algebraic closure \(\overline{k}\) of \(\mathit{k}(s)\) are strongly desingularizable (SGA 5 I 3.1.5). If \(\mathbb{L}\) denotes the set of prime numbers distinct from the residue characteristics of \(S\), one can find a non-empty open set \(S_1\) of \(S\) such that, for every specialization \(\overline{s}_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{s}_2\) of geometric points of \(S_1\), the specialization morphism (10)

\[ \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}_1})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}_2}) \]

is bijective.

By 1 and 3, one may, up to restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, suppose that \(f\) is locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\), and that, for every sheaf of finite constant \(\mathbb{L}\)-groups \(F\) on \(X\), \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 1\). It then follows from 14 that, for every specialization \(\overline{s}_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{s}_2\) of geometric points of \(S_1\), the specialization morphism

\[ \left(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F\right)_{\overline{s}_2} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \left(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F\right)_{\overline{s}_1} \]

is bijective. The corollary is nothing other than the translation of what precedes into the terms of fundamental groups.

blocks.html: 3 Cohomological properness and generic local acyclicity

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1

Let \(S\) be an irreducible scheme with generic point \(s\), let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two \(S\)-schemes of finite presentation, and \(f\colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) an \(S\)-morphism. For every \(S\)-scheme \(S'\), we denote by \(Y'\), \(X'\), etc. the inverse image of \(Y\), \(X\), etc. under the morphism \(S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). We have the following properties:

  1. 1)

    a) One can find a non-empty open \(S'\) of \(S\) such that, for every finite constant sheaf of sets \(F'\) on \(X'\), \(f'_*F'\) is constructible, and \((F',f')\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

    b) Let \(F\) be a constructible sheaf of sets on \(X\). Then one can find a non-empty open \(S'\) of \(S\) (depending on \(F\)) such that \(f'_*F'\) is constructible and \((F',f')\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

  2. 2)

    Suppose that the schemes of finite type of dimension \(\leqslant \dim X_s\) over an algebraic closure \(\overline{k}\) of \(\mathit{k}(s)\) are strongly desingularizable (SGA 5 I 3.1.5). Then we have in addition the following properties:

    a) One can find a non-empty open \(S'\) of \(S\) such that, for every finite constant sheaf of groups \(F'\) on \(X'\), of order prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\), if \(\Phi'\) is the stack of torsors under \(F'\), \(f'_*\Phi'\) is \(1\)-constructible, and \((F',f')\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\).

    b) Let \(\mathbb{L}\) be the set of prime numbers distinct from the residue characteristics of \(S\) and \(\Phi\) an ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-stack that is \(1\)-constructible on \(X\) (0), such that, for every scheme \(X_1\) étale over \(X\) and for every pair of objects \(x, x_1\) of \(\Phi_{X_1}\), the sheaf \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}} _{X_1}(x,x_1)\) is constructible. Suppose moreover \(S\) locally Noetherian. Then one can find a non-empty open \(S'\) of \(S\) such that \(f'_*\Phi'\) is \(1\)-constructible, that, for every pair of objects \(y, y_1\) of a fibre \((f'_*\Phi')_{Y_1}\), \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{Y_1}(y,y_1)\) is constructible, and that \((\Phi',f')\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\).
Proof

We may assume \(S\) affine; by SGA 4 VIII 1.1, we may assume \(S\) integral; finally, by passage to the limit, we may assume that \(S\) is the spectrum of an algebra of finite type over \(\mathbf{Z}\); in particular \(S\) is then Noetherian. Since the question is local on \(Y\), we may assume \(Y\) affine. Moreover it suffices, in order to prove the theorem, to do so after a finite extension \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), where \(S'\) is an integral scheme and where \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a composite of étale morphisms and of surjective radicial finite morphisms.
1) Case of constant sheaves of sets

1) 1. Reduction to the case where \(X\) is normal over \(S\). Let \(X_{1\overline{s}}\) be the normalization of \((X_{\overline{s}})_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\); after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open and making a radicial extension of \(S\), we may assume that \(X_{1\overline{s}}\) comes from a scheme \(X_1\) normal over \(S\), and that the morphism \(X_{1\overline{s}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{\overline{s}}\) comes from a surjective finite morphism \(p\colon X_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) (EGA IV 8.8.2 and 9.6.1). Suppose the theorem proved for \(fp\). After possibly restricting \(S\) to an open, we may assume that, for every constant sheaf of sets \(F\) on \(X\), \((p^*F,fp)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and that \(f_*p_*(p^*F)\) is constructible. By 9, \((p_*p^*F,f)\) is then cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). The morphism
\[ F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} p_* p^* F=G \]

is a monomorphism. It already follows from this, \(f_*F\) being a subsheaf of \(f_*G\), that \(f_*F\) is constructible (SGA 4 IX 2.9 (ii)) and that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\).
Let \(X_2=X_1 \times_{X} X_1\), \(q \colon X_2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism. By 11 1), we have an exact sequence
\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{F}\ar[r]&{G}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{q_*q^*F}}. \]

By what we have just proved, applied to \(fq\) instead of \(f\), we may assume, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open, that, for every constant sheaf of sets \(F\) on \(X\), \((q^*F,fq)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), hence that \((q_*q^*F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\). It then follows from 13 1) that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).
1) 2. Reduction to the case where \(X\) is normal affine over \(S\).

Let \(U_s\) be an affine open of \(X_s\) dense in \(X_s\). After possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open, we may assume that \(U_s \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_s\) lifts to an open immersion \(i\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), schematically dominant relatively to \(S\) (EGA IV 8.9.1). Since the morphism \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is normal, we have by SGA 2 XIV 1.18:
\[ \operatorname{prof} \mathrm{\acute{e}t}_{S- U} (X) \geqslant 2 ; \]
consequently, for every constant sheaf \(F\) on \(X\), the canonical morphism
\[ F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*i^*F \]

is an isomorphism. It follows that, if we suppose the theorem proved for \(fi\) and \(i\), then, after restriction of \(S\) to a non-empty open, \((i^*F,i)\) and \((i^*F,fi)\) are cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). The same is therefore true of \((F,f)\) (6 2)). Since moreover \(f_* F=(fi)_*(i^*F)\) is constructible, this completes the reduction.
1) 3. End of the proof.
We may assume \(S\) normal (EGA IV 7.8.3). One can find a compactification of \(X_s\):
\[ \xymatrix{{X_s}\ar[r]^{j_s}\ar[d]_{f_s}&{P_s}\ar[ld]^{g_s}\\{Y_s}} \]

where \(j_s\) is a dominant open immersion and \(g_s\) a proper morphism; after possibly making a radicial extension of \(\mathit{k}(s)\) and replacing \(P_s\) by its normalization, which does not change \(X_s\), we may assume \(P_s\) geometrically normal. After possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open and making a surjective radicial extension, we may assume that the diagram above comes from a diagram
\[ \xymatrix{{X}\ar[r]^{j}\ar[d]_{f}&{P}\ar[ld]^{g}\\{Y}} \]

where \(P\) is a scheme normal over \(S\), \(j\) a schematically dominant open immersion relatively to \(S\) and \(g\) a proper morphism (EGA IV 6.9.1, 9.9.4 and 9.6.1). For every finite constant sheaf of sets \(F\) on \(X\) of value \(C\), \(j_*F\) is the constant sheaf of value \(C\) (SGA 4 2.14.1), and the same holds after every base change \(S' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\); it follows that \((F,j)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). The same is therefore true of \((F,f)\), since \(g\) is proper (8). Since \(g\) is proper \(f_*F=g_*C_P\) is constructible, which completes the proof of 1) a).
2) Case of a constructible sheaf of sets

Let \(F\) be a constructible sheaf of sets on \(X\). By SGA 4 IX 2.14 (ii), one can find a finite family of morphisms \(p_i \colon Z_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), and, on each \(Z_i\), a finite constant sheaf of sets \(C_i\), so that one has a monomorphism
\[ j \colon F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \prod_{i} {p_i}_* C_i =G . \]

By 1) a), we may assume, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open, that the \((C_i,fp_i)\) are cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), and that the \(f_*{p_i}_*C_i\) are constructible. We already conclude that \((G,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (9), hence that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), and that \(f_*F\) is constructible. Let \(K\) be the amalgamated sum \(K= G \coprod_{F} G\); since \(F\) and \(G\) are constructible, so is \(K\). We therefore conclude from the foregoing that, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open, we may assume that \((K,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\). It then follows from 13 1) that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).
3) Case of constant sheaves of groups
If \(F\) is a constant sheaf of groups on \(X\), we denote by \(\Phi\) the stack of torsors under \(F\).

3) 1. Let us first show that, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, for every constant sheaf of groups \(F\) on \(X\), of order prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\), \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), and that \(f_*\Phi\) is constructible.

For this we reduce to the case where \(X\) is smooth over \(S\). After possibly making a finite extension of \(\mathit{k}(s)\), which is permissible since we can consider it as composed of an étale extension and a radicial extension, we can find a surjective proper morphism \(p_s \colon X_{1s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_s\), where \(X_{1s}\) is a scheme smooth over \(S\) of the same dimension as \(X_s\), and, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, we can assume that \(p_s\) comes from a surjective proper morphism \(p \colon X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), where \(X_1\) is a scheme smooth over \(S\) (EGA IV 9.6.1 and 12.1.6). Let \(X_2=X_1 \times_X X_1\), \(q \colon X_2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism. We have an exact diagram of stacks over \(X\)
\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]&{p_*p^* \Phi}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{q_*q^*\Phi}} \]

(11 2)). The theorem being assumed proved in the smooth case, we see first of all that we can assume \(f_*p_*p^*\Phi\) constructible; the same therefore holds for \(f_*\Phi\) (1.1 below). Moreover, according to 6 2), we can assume \((p_*p^*\Phi,f)\) cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\); it follows that \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\). We can therefore assume that \((q_*q^*\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\), and this entails that \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (12 1)).
We then reduce, as in 1)2, to the case where \(X\) is smooth and affine over \(S\). Let then
\[ \xymatrix{{X}\ar[r]^{i}\ar[d]_{f}&{P}\ar[ld]^{q}\\{Y}} \]

be a compactification of \(X\), where \(i\) is a dominant open immersion and \(q\) a proper morphism. Since we have \(\dim P_s=\dim X_s\), we can apply the hypothesis of resolution of singularities to \(P_{\overline s}\). After possibly making an étale extension and a radicial extension of \(S\), we can find a proper morphism \(r \colon Z \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P\), where \(Z\) is smooth over \(S\), \(r^{-1}(X)\simeq X\), and where \(r^{-1}(X)\) is the complement in \(Z\) of a divisor with normal crossings relative to \(S\). Every torsor under \(F\) is then tamely ramified on \(Z\) relative to \(S\) (3 b)). It follows therefore from 7 that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), which proves our assertion.
[4] 3) 2. Reduction to the case where \(X\) is smooth over \(S\).

After possibly making a finite extension of \(\mathit{k}(s)\), we can find a surjective proper morphism \(p_s\colon X_{1s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), where \(X_{1s}\) is a scheme smooth over \(s\), and we can assume that \(p_s\) comes from a surjective proper morphism \(p\colon X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), where \(X_1\) is smooth over \(S\). Suppose the theorem proved for \(fp\) and let us prove it for \(f\). Let \(F\) be a finite constant sheaf of groups on \(X\), of order prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\), and \(\Phi\) the stack of torsors under \(F\). Let \(X_2=X_1 \times_X X_1\), \(X_3=X_1 \times_X X_1 \times_X X_1\), \(q\colon X_2 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), \(r\colon X_3 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphisms. According to 11 2), we have an exact diagram of stacks
\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]&{p_*p^*\Phi}\ar@<2pt>[r]\ar@<-2pt>[r]&{q_*q^*\Phi}\ar@<4pt>[r]\ar[r]\ar@<-4pt>[r]&{r_*r^*\Phi}}. \]

To prove that \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), it suffices to show that the same holds for \((p_*p^*\Phi,f)\), that \((q_*q^*\Phi, f )\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and that \((r_*r^*\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant -1\) (12 2)). According to 3) 1 above we can assume that, for every finite constant sheaf of groups \(F\), \((q^*\Phi,fq)\), \((q^*\Phi,q)\), \((r^*\Phi,fr)\), \((r^*\Phi,r)\) are cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). It then follows from 6 2) that \((q_*q^*\Phi,f)\) and \((r_*r^*\Phi,f)\) are cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). The theorem being assumed proved in the smooth case, \((p^*\Phi, fp)\) and \((p^*\Phi,p)\), hence also \((p_*p^*\Phi,f)\) (6 2)), are cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\). This indeed shows that \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\).

Moreover \(f_*p_*p^*\Phi\) is \(1\)-constructible by hypothesis; according to 1 we can assume that \(f_*q_*q^*\Phi\) is constructible; it follows therefore from 1.1 below that \(f_*\Phi\) is \(1\)-constructible.
3) 3. Reduction to the case where \(X\) is smooth affine over \(S\).

According to 3)2, we can assume \(X\) smooth over \(S\). Let \((U_i)_{i\in I}\) be a finite covering of \(X\) by affine open sets, and let \(X_1\) be the direct sum of the \(U_i\), \(p\colon X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism. Since \(p\) is a morphism of effective descent for the category of étale sheaves of finite type on variable schemes, we see as in 3)2 that, if we assume the theorem proved for the \(U_i\), i.e. for \(X_1\), it is also true for \(X\).
3) 4. Case where \(X\) is smooth affine over \(S\).
We see as in 3)1 that, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set and making a surjective étale extension and a surjective radicial extension of \(S\), we can find a commutative diagram
\[ \xymatrix{{X}\ar[r]^{i}\ar[d]_{f}&{P}\ar[ld]^{q}\\{Y}} \]

where \(P\) is a scheme smooth over \(S\), \(X\) the complement in \(P\) of a divisor with normal crossings relative to \(S\) and \(g\) a proper morphism. If \(F\) is a constant sheaf of order prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\), every torsor under \(F\) is tamely ramified on \(P\) relative to \(S\) (3 b)). The fact that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\) and that \(f_*\Phi\) is constructible then follows from 7.
4) Proof of 2) b)
4) 1. Case where \(\Phi\) is a gerbe.

We can find a surjective étale morphism of finite type \(p \colon X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), such that \(p^*\Phi\) is a trivial gerbe. By descent, as in 3)2, we see that it suffices to prove the theorem for \(X_1\), \(X_1\times_X X_1\) and \(X_1\times_X X_1\times_X X_1\). We are therefore reduced to the case where \(\Phi\) is the gerbe of torsors under a constructible sheaf of groups \(F\), whose fibres are of order prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\).

According to SGA 4 IX 2.14, we can find a finite family of finite morphisms \(p_i \colon Z_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), and, for each \(i\), a finite constant sheaf of groups \(C_i\), of order prime to the residue characteristics of \(S\), such that we have a monomorphism
\[ j\colon F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \prod_{i} p_{i*}C_i=G . \]

Let \(\Phi_i\) be the stack of torsors under \(C_i\) and \(\Psi\) the stack of torsors under \(G\). It follows from 2) a) that, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, we can assume that the \((C_i,fp_i)\) are cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), and that the stacks \(f_*p_{i*}\Phi_i\) are \(1\)-constructible. It then follows from 9 that the \((p_{i*}C_i,f)\) are cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\); the same therefore holds for \((G,f)\). Moreover, since \(p_{i*}\Phi_i\) is equivalent to the stack of torsors under the group \(p_{i*}C_i\) (SGA 4 VIII 5.8), we see that \(f_*\Psi\) is \(1\)-constructible.
Since \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*G\) is constructible, we can find a sheaf representable by an étale \(Y\)-scheme of finite type \(T\) and an epimorphism
\[ a \colon T \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^1f_*G \]

(SGA 4 IX 2.7); moreover we can assume that the image of the identical section of \(T(T)\) is defined by a torsor \(Q\) on \(X\times_Y T=X_T\), with group \(G|X_T\). Let \(f_T \colon X_T \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) be the canonical morphism, \(F_T=F|X_T\), etc. According to 1) b) we can assume, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, that \((Q/F_T,f_T)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and that \(f_{T*}(Q/F_T)\) is constructible. It then follows from 1.2 that \(f_*\Psi\) is constructible.

Let us show that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\). By 13 2) it suffices to prove that, for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\) and for every torsor \(Q_1\) over \(X_1=X\times_Y Y_1\), if \(f_1 \colon X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y_1\) is the canonical morphism, then \((Q_1/F_1,f_1)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). Now, by definition of \(T\), \(Q_1\) is, locally for the étale topology of \(Y_1\), the inverse image of \(Q\), which proves our reduction.
4) 2. General case.

Using lemma 1.1, 4)1 and 1) a) one sees that, after restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, one may assume \(S(f_*\Phi)\) constructible and \((S\Phi,f)\) cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\). One may then find a sheaf representable by a finite-type étale \(Y\)-scheme \(T\) and an epimorphism
\[ a \colon T \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S(f_*\Phi). \]

We resume the notations of 4)1, and we set further \(Z=T\times_Y T\), \(X_Z=X\times_Y Z\) and we denote by \(f_Z \colon X_Z \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) the canonical morphism. One may assume \(T\) chosen so that the image \(q\) of the identity section of \(T(T)\) under \(a\) is defined by an object \(p\) of \((f_*\Phi)_T=\Phi_{X_T}\). Let \(p_1\) and \(p_2\) (resp. \(q_1\) and \(q_2\)) be the inverse images of \(p\) (resp. \(q\)) under the two projections of \(Z\) onto \(T\). One may assume, after restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, that \((\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{X_T}(p),f_T)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\), that \((\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{X_Z}(p_1,p_2),f_Z)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), and that \(f_{T*}(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{X_T}(p))\), \(f_{Z*}(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{X_Z}(p_1,p_2))\) are constructible ( a)1) and 4)1)).

One first deduces from this that, for every scheme \(Y_1\) étale over \(Y\) and for every pair of objects \(y\), \(y_1\) of \((f_*\Phi)_{Y_1}\), the sheaf \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{Y_1}(y)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{Y_1}(y,y_1)\)) is constructible, such a sheaf being, locally for the étale topology of \(Y_1\), the inverse image of \(f_{T*}(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{X_T}(p))\) (resp. of \(f_{Z*}(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{X_Z}(p_1,p_2))\)).

It remains to prove that \((\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\). For this it suffices to show that, for every \(S'\)-scheme \(S\) and for every geometric point \(\overline{y}'\) of \(Y\), if one denotes by \(\overline{Y}\) the strict localization of \(Y\) at \(y'\), \(\overline{X}=X \times_Y \overline{Y}\), etc., then the canonical functor
\[ \overline{\varphi} \colon \Phi(\overline{X}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Phi'(\overline{X'}) \]
is an equivalence of categories.
Let us show that \(\overline{\varphi}\) is fully faithful. Let \(x\), \(y \in \Phi(\overline{X})\), \(x'\), \(y'\) their images in \(\Phi'(\overline{X'})\) and let us show that the canonical morphism
\[ \tag{*} {\operatorname{Hom}_{\overline{X}}(x,y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\overline{X'}}(x',y') } \]

is bijective. By definition of \(T\) there exist two morphisms of \(\overline{Y}\) into \(T\) such that \(x\) and \(y\) are the inverse images of \(p\) under these two morphisms. This amounts to saying that there is a morphism \(\overline {Y} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\), whence a morphism \(h\colon \overline{X} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_Z\) such that one has
\[ h^*(p_1)=x \qquad h^*(p_2)=y. \]
Consequently one has a canonical isomorphism
\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\overline{X}}(x,y)=h^*(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{X_Z}(p_1,p_2) ). \]

but, taking into account the fact that \((\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{X_Z}(p_1,p_2),f_Z)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), one sees that the same holds for \((\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{\overline{X}}(x,y),\overline{f})\), which proves that the morphism (*) is bijective.

Let us show that \(\overline{\varphi}\) is essentially surjective. Let \(x'\in \Phi'(\overline{X'})\). Since \((S\Phi,f)\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\), the canonical morphism
\[ \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X},\overline{S\Phi}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X'},\overline{S\Phi}') \]

is bijective. Let \(G'\) be the maximal subgerbe of \(\Phi'\) generated by \(x'\); there then exists a maximal subgerbe \(G\) of \(\overline{\Phi}\) whose inverse image on \(\overline{X'}\) is \(G'\). By 4)1 \((G,\overline{f})\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\); consequently the canonical functor
\[ G(\overline{X}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G'(\overline{X'}) \]
is an equivalence of categories, which proves the existence of an element \(x\) of \(\Phi(\overline{X})\) whose image in \(\Phi'(X')\) is isomorphic to \(x'\) and completes the proof of the theorem.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian scheme and

\[ \xymatrix@C=.5cm{{\Phi}\ar[r]^p&{\Phi_1}\ar@<2pt>[rr]^{p_1,p_2}\ar@<-2pt>[rr]&&{\Phi_2}} \]

an exact diagram of stacks over \(S\) (10.1). If \(\Phi_1\) is constructible, so is \(\Phi\). If, for every scheme \(S'\) étale over \(S\) and for every pair of objects \(x_1,~y_1\) of \((\Phi_1)_{S'}\) the sheaf \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{S'}(x_1,y_1)\) is constructible, then, for every pair of objects \(x,~y\) of \(\Phi_{S'}\), the same holds for \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Hom}}_{S'}(x,y)\). Suppose that \(\Phi_1\) is \(1\)-constructible (0) and that \(\Phi_2\) is constructible, then \(\Phi\) is \(1\)-constructible.
For every scheme \(S'\) étale over \(S\) and for every object \(x\) of \(\Phi_{S'}\), one has a monomorphism
\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{S'}(x)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{S'}(p(x)). \]
It therefore follows from SGA 4 IX 2.9 that, if \(\Phi_1\) is constructible, so is \(\Phi\), and the second assertion of the lemma is proved in the same way.
Suppose now \(\Phi_1\) \(1\)-constructible and \(\Phi_2\) constructible. The morphism \(p\) induces on the sheaves of maximal subgerbes a morphism
\[ \varphi\colon S\Phi\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\Phi_1. \]

Let \(G\) be the image of \(S\Phi\) under \(\varphi\); by SGA 4 IX 2.9, \(G\) is a constructible sheaf. One may therefore find a sheaf representable by a finite-type étale \(S\)-scheme \(T\) and an epimorphism
\[ a\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G \]

(SGA 4 IX 2.7). Moreover one may choose \(T\) so that the image \(y\) of the identity section of \(T(T)\) under \(a\) is defined by an object \(x_1\) of \((\Phi_1)_T\) of the form \(x_1=p(x)\), where \(x\in\Phi_T\).

It suffices to show that, for every point \(s\) of \(S\), there exists a non-empty open set \(U\) of \(\overline{\{ s\} }\) such that \(S\Phi|U\) is locally constant constructible. Let \(s\in S\), \(\overline{s}\) a geometric point over \(s\) and \(\overline{y}_1,\dots,\overline{y}_n\) the elements of \(G_{\overline{s}}\). By definition of \(T\) there exist morphisms \(h_i\colon\overline{s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) such that one has \(h_i^*(y)=\overline{y}_i\). Let \(S'\) be the fibre product over \(S\) of \(n\) schemes isomorphic to \(T\), \(h\colon\overline{s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) the fibre product of the \(h_i\), \(y_i\) (resp. \(x_i\)) the inverse image of \(y\) (resp. \(x\)) under the \(i\)-th projection of \(S'\) onto \(T\). Let \(F_i\) be the subsheaf of \(S\Phi |S'\) inverse image of \(y_i\) and let us show that the \(F_i\) are constructible. The sheaf \(F_i\) is a quotient of the sheaf \(F'_i\) such that, for every scheme \(S''\) étale over \(S'\), one has
\begin{multline*} F'_i(S'') = \{\text{classes mod. isomorphism of objects $z$ of $\Phi_{S''}$ equipped}\\ \text{ with an isomorphism $i\colon p(z)\simeq p(x_i|S'')$}\}. \end{multline*}
It suffices to show that the \(F_i'\) are constructible. Now, if one sets \(z_i=p_1p(x_i)\), \(z_i'=p_2p(x_i)\), one has a monomorphism
\[ \Psi_i\colon F'_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{\mathbf{Isom}}_{S'}(z_i,z_i'), \]

obtained by associating, to every scheme \(S''\) étale over \(S'\) and to every object \(z\) of \(\Phi_{S''}\) such that one has an isomorphism \(i\colon p(z)\simeq p(x_i|S'')\), the isomorphism of \(z_i\) onto \(z_i'\) defined by the condition that the diagram
\[ \xymatrix@C=1.2cm{{p_1p(z)}\ar[r]^-{p_1(i)}\ar[d]_-j&{z_i}\ar[d]\\{p_2p(z)}\ar[r]^-{p_2(i)}&{z'_i}} \]
be commutative (\(j\) is the canonical morphism associated with the exact diagram).

The morphism \(\Psi_i\) is injective, for to say that two objects \(z,~z'\), such that we have isomorphisms \(i\colon p(z)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p(x_i|S''),~i'\colon p(z')\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p(x_i|S'')\), define the same element of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Isom}}_{S''}(z_i,z'_i)\) amounts to saying that we have \(p_1(i'^{-1}i)=p_2(i'^{-1}i)\), i.e. that \(i'^{-1}i\) comes from an isomorphism \(z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} z'\). The sheaf \(F'_i\), being a subsheaf of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Isom}}_{S'}(z_i,z'_i)\), is constructible.

Since one can find a non-empty open set \(U\) of \(\overline{\{ s\}}\) such that \(y_1|U,\dots,y_n|U\) generate \(G\), it follows from lemma 1.2 below that \(S\Phi |U\) is constructible, hence, after possibly shrinking \(U\), \(S\Phi |U\) is locally constant constructible.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian scheme, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism, \(F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) a monomorphism of sheaves of groups on \(X\), \(\Psi\) (resp. \(\Psi_1\)) the stack of torsors under \(F\) (resp. under \(G\)), \(\Phi=f_*\Psi\), \(\Phi_1=f_*\Psi_1\). Suppose given a sheaf on \(S\) representable by an étale \(S\)-scheme of finite type \(T\) and a surjective morphism

\[ a\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\Phi_1\simeq\mathrm{R}^1f_*G, \]

so that there exists a torsor \(Q\) on \(X_T=X\times_ST\) with group \(G|X_T\) which defines in \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*G(T)\) the image under \(a\) of the identity section of \(T(T)\). Let \(f_T\colon X_T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) be the canonical morphism and set \(F_T=F|X_T\). Suppose that \(\Phi_1\) is \(1\)-constructible and that \(f_{T*}(Q/F_T)\) is constructible. Then \(\Phi\) is \(1\)-constructible.
It suffices indeed to copy the proof of 1.1, the fact that we have morphisms \(\Psi_i\) being replaced by the fact that we have isomorphisms
\[ F'_i\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} f_*(Q/F_T)|S'. \]

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.3

Suppose that \(k=\mathit{k}(s)\) is of characteristic zero; then the schemes of finite type over \(\overline{k}\) of dimension \(\leqslant\dim X\) are strongly desingularizable, and the proof of 1 allows one to prove the following results:

  1. a)

    There exists a non-empty open set \(S_1\) of \(S\) such that, for every scheme \(S'\) over \(S_1\) whose maximal points are of characteristic zero and for every locally constant constructible sheaf of sets \(F\) on \(X'=X\times_SS'\), \((F,f')\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

  2. b)

    If all the residue characteristics of \(S\) are zero, there exists a non-empty open set \(S_1\) of \(S\) such that, for every scheme \(S'\) over \(S_1\) and for every locally constant constructible sheaf of groups \(F\) on \(X'\), \((F,f')\) is cohomologically proper relative to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant1\).

It suffices indeed to copy the proof of 1.2) a). The proposition 7 used in 3) 4 applies to the case of a locally constant sheaf \(F\), for, resuming the notation of 3)4, every torsor under \(F\) is tamely ramified over \(P\) relative to \(S\) since all the residue characteristics of \(S\) are zero.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

Let \(k\) be a field of characteristic \(p\geqslant 0\), \(p'\) the set of prime numbers distinct from \(p\), \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k\) a coherent morphism.

  1. 1)

    For every sheaf of sets \(F\), \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

  2. 2)

    Suppose that one of the two following conditions is satisfied:

    1. a)

      \(f\) is of finite type and the schemes of finite type of dimension \(\leqslant\dim X\) over an algebraic closure of \(k\) are strongly desingularizable.

    2. b)

      The schemes of finite type over an algebraic closure of \(k\) are strongly desingularizable.

    Then, for every sheaf of ind-\(p'\)-groups \(F\), \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 1\).
Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets (resp. of ind-\(p'\)-groups). By SGA 4 IX 2.7.2 one can write \(F\) as a filtered direct limit
\[ F=\varinjlim F_i, \]

where the \(F_i\) are constructible sheaves of sets (resp. of ind-\(p'\)-groups). Since \(f\) is coherent, \(f_*\) (resp. \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*\)) commutes with direct limits (SGA 4 VII 3.3). If one knows that the \((F_i,f)\) are cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. that every sheaf of sets is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and that the \((F_i,f)\) are cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 1\)), the same will hold for \((F,f)\). We may therefore suppose \(F\) constructible.

If we suppose \(f\) of finite type (resp. satisfying a)), the proposition follows from 1 1) b) (resp. from 1 2) b)). Let us now prove 2 when we no longer suppose \(f\) of finite type. For every scheme \(S'\) over \(k\) and for every geometric point \(\overline{s}\) of \(S'\), we denote by \(\overline{k}\) (resp. \(\overline{S'}\)) the strict localization of \(k\) at \(\overline{s}\) (resp. of \(S'\) at \(\overline{s}\)), \(\overline{X}\) the inverse image of \(X\) over \(\overline{k}\), and we consider the cartesian square
\[ \xymatrix@C=1.2cm{{\overline{X}}\ar[d]_-{\overline{f}}&{\overline{X'}}\ar[l]_g\ar[d]^-{\overline{f}'}\\{\overline{k}}&{\overline{S'}}\ar[l]} \]
It suffices to prove that, for every \(S'\), for every \(\overline{s}\), the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X},\overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(\overline{X'},\overline{F'})\qquad \text{(resp. $\mathrm{H}^1(\overline{X},\overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(\overline{X'},\overline{F'})$)} \]
is an isomorphism. It suffices to show that we have the relations
\[ \tag{*} \overline{F}\simeq g_*g^*\overline{F}\qquad \text{(resp. $\mathrm{R}^1g_*(g^*\overline{F})=0$).} \]

Now, under this form, the question is local on \(X\) for the étale topology. We may therefore suppose \(X\) affine; by passage to the limit we may suppose \(X\) of finite type over \(k\). One then knows that \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (resp. \(\leqslant 1\)) and that the same holds when one replaces \(X\) by an étale scheme of finite type over \(X\), which proves (*).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3

Let \(S\) be an irreducible scheme with generic point \(s\), \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism of finite presentation. Suppose that the schemes of finite type of dimension \(\leqslant\dim X_s\) over an algebraic closure \(\overline{k}\) of \(k\) are desingularizable (EGA IV 7.9.1). Then, if \(\mathbb{L}\) denotes the set of prime numbers distinct from the residue characteristics of \(S\), one can find a non-empty open set \(S_1\) of \(S\) such that the morphism \(f|S_1\) is universally locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\).

We may suppose \(S\) integral and \(X\) reduced (SGA 4 VIII 1.1). By passage to the limit we may suppose \(S\) Noetherian. Moreover, to prove the theorem, it suffices to do so after a finite extension \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), where \(S_1\) is an integral scheme and where \(S_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is composed of étale extensions and surjective radicial extensions.

Let us first show that, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, \(f\) is universally locally \(0\)-acyclic. After possibly making a radicial extension of \(\mathit{k}(s)\), we may suppose that the morphism \((X_s)_\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} s\) is separable; we may therefore suppose, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set and making a surjective radicial extension of \(S\), that the morphism \(f\) is flat, with separable geometric fibres (EGA IV 12.1.1), which entails that \(f\) is universally \(0\)-acyclic (SGA 4 XV 4.1).

Let us show that, up to restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, \(f\) is universally locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\). Up to making a finite extension of \(\mathit{k}(s)\), which is allowable since it can be regarded as compounded of an étale extension and a radicial extension, one can find a proper surjective morphism \(p_s\colon Y_s\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_s\), where \(Y_s\) is a scheme smooth over \(S\), of the same dimension as \(X_s\), and one may suppose that \(p_s\) comes from a proper surjective morphism \(p\colon Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), where \(Y\) is a scheme smooth over \(S\) (EGA IV 9.6.1 and 12.1.6). It suffices to show that, up to restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, for every diagram with cartesian squares
\[ \xymatrix{{S''}\ar[d]_i&{X ''}\ar[d]^j\ar[l]_{f''}\\{S'}\ar[d]&{X'}\ar[d]\ar[l]_{f'}\\{S}&{X,}\ar[l]_f} \]
where \(i\) is étale of finite presentation, and for every sheaf of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups \(F\) on \(S''\), if \(\Phi\) is the stack of torsors under \(F\), then the canonical morphism
\[ {f'}^*i_*\Phi \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} j_*{f''}^*\Phi \]
is an equivalence. Let \(Z=Y\times_{X}Y, T=Y\times_{X}Y\times_{X}Y\). We have in a natural way a commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{S''}\ar[d]_i&{X''}\ar[l]_{f''}\ar[d]_j&{Y''}\ar[l]_{p''}\ar[d]&{Z''}\ar[d]\ar@<-.4ex>[l]\ar@<.4ex>[l]&{T''}\ar[d]\ar[l]\ar@<-.8ex>[l]\ar@<.8ex>[l]\\{S'}\ar[d]&{X'}\ar[l]_{f'}\ar[d]&{Y'}\ar[d]\ar[l]_{p'}&{Z'}\ar[d]\ar@<-.4ex>[l]\ar@<.4ex>[l]&{T'}\ar[d]\ar[l]\ar@<-.8ex>[l]\ar@<.8ex>[l]\\{S}&{X}\ar[l]_f&{Y}\ar[l]_p&{Z'}\ar@<-.4ex>[l]\ar@<.4ex>[l]&{T'}\ar[l]\ar@<-.8ex>[l]\ar@<.8ex>[l]} \]

Let \(q\colon Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and \(r\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be the canonical morphisms, the notations \(q', r', q'', r''\) having an evident meaning. By 11 2), we have the following essentially commutative diagram, whose rows are exact:

\[ \xymatrix{{f'^* i_*\Phi}\ar[r]\ar[d]_a&{p'_*p'^*(f'^*i_*\Phi)}\ar[d]_b\ar@<-.4ex>[r]\ar@<.4ex>[r]&{q'_*q'^*(f'^*i_*\Phi)}\ar[d]_c\ar[r]\ar@<-.8ex>[r]\ar@<.8ex>[r]&{r'_*r'^*(f'^*i_*\Phi)}\ar[d]_d\\{j_*f''^*\Phi}\ar[r]&{j_*p''_*p''^*(f''^*\Phi)}\ar@<-.4ex>[r]\ar@<.4ex>[r]&{j_*q''_*q''^*(f''^*\Phi)}\ar[r]\ar@<-.8ex>[r]\ar@<.8ex>[r]&{j_*r''_*r''^*(f''^*\Phi)}} \]

Since \(Y\) is smooth over \(S\), the morphism \(fp\) is universally locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\) (SGA XV 2.1), and it follows from [2] that \(b\) is an equivalence of categories. On the other hand, up to restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, one may suppose that the morphisms \(Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) and \(T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) are universally locally \(0\)-acyclic. It follows that the functors \(c\) and \(d\) are fully faithful, and the diagram above then shows that \(a\) is an equivalence, which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

Let \(k\) be a field of characteristic \(p\geqslant0\), \(p'\) the set of prime numbers distinct from \(p\), \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k\) a coherent morphism. Suppose one of the two following conditions is satisfied:

  1. a)

    \(f\) is of finite type and the schemes of finite type of dimension \(\leqslant\dim X\) over an algebraic closure of \(k\) are desingularizable.

  2. b)

    The schemes of finite type over an algebraic closure of \(k\) are desingularizable.

Then \(f\) is universally locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(p'\).

Case a) follows from 3. In case b), the question being local on \(X\), one may suppose \(X\) affine; by passage to the limit (SGA 4 XV 1.3), one reduces to the case where \(X\) is of finite type over \(k\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5

Let \(S\) be an irreducible scheme with generic point \(s\), \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism of finite presentation. Suppose that the schemes of finite type of dimension \(\leqslant\dim X_s\) over an algebraic closure \(\overline{k}\) of \(\mathit{k}(s)\) are strongly desingularizable (SGA 5 I 3.1.5). If \(\mathbb{L}\) denotes the set of prime numbers distinct from the residue characteristics of \(S\), one can find a non-empty open set \(S_1\) of \(S\) such that, for every specialization \(\overline{s}_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{s}_2\) of geometric points of \(S_1\), the specialization morphism (10)
