\[ \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}_1})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}_2}) \]

is bijective.

By 1 and 3, one may, up to restricting \(S\) to a non-empty open set, suppose that \(f\) is locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\), and that, for every sheaf of finite constant \(\mathbb{L}\)-groups \(F\) on \(X\), \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 1\). It then follows from 14 that, for every specialization \(\overline{s}_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{s}_2\) of geometric points of \(S_1\), the specialization morphism
\[ \left(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F\right)_{\overline{s}_2} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \left(\mathrm{R}^1 f_* F\right)_{\overline{s}_1} \]
is bijective. The corollary is nothing other than the translation of what precedes into the terms of fundamental groups.

pages.title: Homotopy exact sequences
pages.html:
4 Homotopy exact sequences

0

Let \(X\) and \(S\) be two connected schemes, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism, \(a\) a geometric point of \(X\), \(\mathbb{L}\) a set of prime numbers. Let \(K\) be the kernel of the canonical homomorphism \(\pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(S,a)\) and \(N\) the smallest normal pro-subgroup of \(K\) such that \(K/N\) is a pro-\(\mathbb{L}\)-group \(K^{\mathbb{L}}\). Then \(N\) is normal in \(\pi_1(X,a)\) and we denote by

\[ \pi_1'(X,a) \]

the quotient of \(\pi_1(X,a)\) by \(N\). If \(a\) is a geometric point of a geometric fibre \(X_{\overline{s}}\), the canonical morphisms

\[ \pi_1(X_{\overline{s}},a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(S,a) \]

allow one to define canonical morphisms

\[ \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\xrightarrow{u} \pi_1'(X,a) \xrightarrow{v} \pi_1(S,a) \]

We have \(vu=0\).

Proposition 1

Let \(S\) be a connected scheme, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a locally \(0\)-acyclic morphism (SGA 4 XV 1.11); suppose moreover that \(f\) is \(0\)-acyclic (which, when \(f\) is coherent, amounts to saying that the geometric fibres of \(f\) are connected (SGA 4 XV 1.16)). Let \(\mathbb{L}\) be a set of prime numbers. If \(S'\) is a scheme étale over \(S\), we denote by \(X'\), \(f'\) the inverse images of \(X\), \(f\) over \(S'\). Suppose that, for every étale covering \(S'\) of \(S\) and for every étale covering \(E\) of \(X'\), a quotient of a Galois covering whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group, \((E,f')\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and \(f'_* E\) constructible. Then, if \(\overline{s}\) is a geometric point of \(S\) and \(a\) a geometric point of the fibre \(X_{\overline{s}}\), the sequence of group homomorphisms

\[ \tag{1.1} \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\xrightarrow{u} \pi_1'(X,a)\xrightarrow{v}\pi_1(S,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}1 \]

is exact

This statement generalizes X.4, whose proof we are going to copy.

Let us first show that \(v\) is surjective. It suffices to show that, for every connected étale covering \(S'\) of \(S\), \(X'\) is also connected (V 9). Let \(C\) be a set having at least two elements. It follows from the fact that \(f\) is \(0\)-acyclic that the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^0(S',C_{S'})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(X',C_{X'}) \]

is bijective, hence that \(X'\) is connected, whence the surjectivity of \(v\).

By definition of \(K^{\mathbb{L}}\) (0), we have the exact sequence

\[ 1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K^{\mathbb{L}} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1'(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(S,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1 \]

Let \(\widetilde{S}\) be the universal covering of \(S\) and \(\widetilde{X}=\widetilde{S}\times_SX\); the group \(K^{\mathbb{L}}\) classifies the Galois coverings \(P\) whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group, such that there exists an étale covering \(S'\) of \(S\) and a Galois covering \(Q\) of \(X'=X\times_SS'\) such that one has an isomorphism \(P\simeq Q\times_{X'}\widetilde{X}\). In order for the sequence (1.1) to be exact, it is necessary and sufficient that the canonical morphism

\[ \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K^{\mathbb{L}} \]

be surjective. According to the interpretation of \(K^{\mathbb{L}}\), this amounts to saying that, for every étale covering \(S'\) of \(S\) and for every Galois covering \(Q\) of \(X'\) whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group, such that \(P=Q\times_{X'}\widetilde{X}\) is connected, then \(Q|X_{\overline{s}}\) is connected. Let us show that this last condition is satisfied. Indeed, let \(S'\) be an étale covering of \(S\), \(Q\) a Galois covering of \(X'\) whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group \(F\), such that \(Q|X_{\overline{s}}\) is disconnected, and let us show that, after replacing \(S'\) by an étale covering if necessary, \(Q\) becomes disconnected. There exists a subgroup \(G\) of \(F\) distinct from \(F\) and a torsor \(R\) under \(G|X_{\overline{s}}\) such that \(Q|X_{\overline{s}}\) is obtained by extension of the structural group \(G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F\) from \(R\). The étale covering \(E=Q/G\) of \(X'\) is such that \(E|X_{\overline{s}}\) has a section. According to 16, \(f_*E\) is locally constant constructible and, after replacing \(S'\) by an étale covering if necessary, one may even suppose that \(f_*E\) is constant. Since \((E,f')\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant0\) and since \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_{\overline{s}}, E|X_{\overline{s}})\) is nonempty, one sees that \(E\) has a section. But this proves that \(Q\) is disconnected, which completes the proof.

From 1 one deduces the following lemma, which will be used in 6.

Lemma 2

Let \(S\) be a connected scheme, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism locally \(0\)-acyclic and \(0\)-acyclic, \(\mathbb{L}\) a set of prime numbers. Suppose that, for every sheaf of finite constant \(\mathbb{L}\)-groups \(F\) on \(X\), \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant1\), and that, for every étale covering \(S'\) of \(S\) and for every étale covering \(E\) of \(X'\), a quotient of a Galois covering whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group, \(f'_*E\) is constructible. Then, if \(\overline{s}\) is a geometric point of \(S\) and \(a\) a geometric point of the fibre \(X_{\overline{s}}\), the sequence of group homomorphisms

\[ \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1'(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(S,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}1 \]

is exact.

the hypotheses of 1 are satisfied. It indeed follows from 13 3) that, for every scheme \(S'\) étale over \(S\) and for every étale covering \(E\) of \(X'\), a quotient of a Galois covering whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group, \((E,f')\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

Proposition 3

Let \(S\) be a connected scheme, \(\mathbb{L}\) a set of prime numbers, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a \(0\)-acyclic morphism, locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\) (SGA 4 XV 1.11), \(g\colon S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) a section of \(f\). Let \(\overline{s}\) be a geometric point of \(S\), \(a\) a geometric point of the fibre \(X_{\overline{s}}\). Suppose that, for every sheaf of constant \(\mathbb{L}\)-groups \(F\), \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant1\), that the direct image by \(f\) of the stack of torsors under \(F\) is a \(1\)-constructible stack (0) and that, for every étale covering \(E\) of \(X'\), a quotient of a Galois covering whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group, \(f'_*E\) is constructible. Then the sequence of group homomorphisms

\[ \tag{3.1} 1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\xrightarrow{u} \pi_1'(X,a)\xrightarrow{v}\pi_1(S,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}1 \]

is exact.

Taking 2 into account, it suffices to show the injectivity of the morphism \(u\), i.e. to prove that, for every principal covering \(\overline{Z}\) of \(X_{\overline{s}}\) whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group \(C\), there exists an étale covering \(Z\) of \(X\) and a morphism from a connected component of \(Z|X_{\overline{s}}\) into \(\overline{Z}\) (V 8). So let \(\overline{Z}\) be a principal covering of \(X_{\overline{s}}\) whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group \(C\) and \(\overline{z}\) its class in \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_{\overline{s}},C_{X_{\overline{s}}})\). According to 5 d), one has a canonical isomorphism

\[ \left(\mathrm{R}^1f_*C_X\right)_{\overline{s}}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1(X_{\overline{s}},C_{X_{\overline{s}}}), \]

and by 16, \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*C_X\) is a locally constant constructible sheaf. One can therefore find an étale covering \(S'\) of \(S\) such that \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*C_X|S'\) is constant. If \(\overline{s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) is a geometric point above the geometric point \(\overline{s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), there exists an element \(z\) of \(\mathrm{H}^0(S', \mathrm{R}^1f_*C_X)\) whose image in \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_{\overline{s}},C_{X_{\overline{s}}})\) is \(\overline{z}\). By Lemma 3.1 below, one can find an étale covering \(S'_1\) of \(S'\) and a torsor \(P\) on \(X'_1\) under the group \(C\) whose image in \(\mathrm{H}^0(S'_1,\mathrm{R}^1f_*C)\) is equal to the restriction of \(z\). The torsor \(P\) is representable by an étale covering \(Z\) of \(X'_1=X\times_SS'_1\) such that \(Z\times_{X'_1}X_{\overline{s}}\) is isomorphic to \(\overline{Z}\). If one considers \(Z\) as an étale covering of \(X\), one then has a morphism from \(Z\times_XX_{\overline{s}}\) into \(\overline{Z}\), which completes the proof.

Lemma 3.1

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a \(0\)-acyclic and locally \(0\)-acyclic morphism, and \(g\) a section of \(f\). Let \(C\) be a finite constant group such that \((C_X,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant0\) and such that the direct image by \(f\) of the stack of torsors under \(C_X\) is constructible. Then, for every section \(z\) of \(\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathrm{R}^1f_*C_X)\), one can find an étale covering \(S_1\) of \(S\) and, if \(X_1=X\times_SS_1\), an element of \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_1,C_{X_1})\), whose image under the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(X_1,C_{X_1})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(S_1,\mathrm{R}^1f_*C_{X_1}) \]

is equal to the restriction of \(z\) to \(\mathrm{H}^0(S_1,\mathrm{R}^1f_*C_{X_1})\).

For every scheme \(S'\) étale over \(S\), one sets \(X'=X\times_SS'\), and one denotes by \(g'\) (resp. \(F'\), etc.) the inverse image of \(g\) (resp. \(F\), etc.) by the morphism \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). The presheaf \(G\) on \(S\) defined by

\[ G(S') = \left\{\text{classes mod. isomorphism of torsors $P$ on~$X'$ under the group $C_{X'}$, equipped with an isomorphism $g'^*P\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} C_{S'}$ } \right\} \]

is then a sheaf. This indeed follows by descent from the fact that an isomorphism of a torsor \(P\) on \(X'\) is well determined by its restriction to \(g'(S')\). Moreover, one has a surjective morphism

\[ G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^1f_*F. \]

Let \(z\) be an element of \(\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathrm{R}^1f_*C_X)\) and \(H\) the subsheaf of \(G\) that is the inverse image of \(z\). It suffices to show that \(H\) is a locally constant constructible sheaf. Now, this property being local on \(S\), one may assume that \(z\) comes from an element of \(\mathrm{H}^1(X,C_X)\) represented by a torsor \(P\) such that \(g^*P\) is isomorphic to \(C_S\). To give an isomorphism \(i\colon g^*P\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} C_S\) amounts to giving a global section of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{C_S}(g^*P)\), and two isomorphisms \(i\) and \(i'\) define the same element of \(G(X)\) if and only if \(ii'^{-1}\) is the image of an element of \(\operatorname{Aut}_{C_X}(P)\). If one considers the canonical injection

\[ f_*\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{C_X}(P)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{C_S}(g^*P)\simeq C, \]

\(H\) is thus identified with the quotient of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{C_S}(g^*P)\) by \(f_*\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{C_X}(P)\). By 16 \(f_*\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{C_X}(P)\) is locally constant; the same is therefore true of \(H\), which completes the proof.

Examples 4

Let us note that, if \(S\) is a connected scheme, the hypotheses of 1 are satisfied when the morphism \(f\) is proper flat of finite presentation, with separable connected geometric fibres, \(\mathbb{L}\) being arbitrary (cf. X 3). The hypotheses of 3 are satisfied if moreover \(f\) is smooth and has a section, provided one denotes by \(\mathbb{L}\) the set of prime numbers distinct from the residue characteristics of \(S\) (SGA 4 XV 2.1 and XVI 5.2).

The hypotheses of 1 are also satisfied if \(S\) is connected, if one has a proper scheme \(Z\) of finite presentation, flat over \(S\), with separable connected geometric fibres, such that \(X\) is the complement in \(Z\) of a divisor with normal crossings relative to \(S\), \(\mathbb{L}\) being the set of prime numbers distinct from the residue characteristics of \(S\) (9). The hypotheses of 3 are satisfied if moreover \(f\) is smooth and has a section.

5

Let us resume the notations and hypotheses of 3. If \(\overline{s}\) is a geometric point of \(S\) and \(a=g(\overline{s})\), the section \(g\) allows one to define a morphism

\[ w\colon \pi_1(S,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1'(X,a), \]

so that \(\pi_1'(X,a)\) is identified with the semi-direct product of \(\pi_1(S,a)\) by \(\pi_1(X_{\overline{s}},a)\). The pro-finite group \(\pi_1(S,a)\) thus operates on \(\pi_1(X_{\overline{s}}, a)\). Since \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\) is a strict inverse limit of groups invariant under the action of \(\pi_1(S,a)\), the datum of \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\) equipped with this action is equivalent to the datum of a strict inverse system of finite étale group schemes over \(S\), which one denotes by

\[ \pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X/S,g,\overline{s})\quad\text{or simply}\quad\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X/S,g). \]

One then has the following properties:

5.1

For every finite étale group scheme \(G\) over \(S\), whose fibres are \(\mathbb{L}\)-groups, the set \(E\) of classes of torsors \(P\) under the inverse image \(G_X\) of \(G\) on \(X\), equipped with an isomorphism \(g^*P\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} G\), is canonically isomorphic to the set

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_S(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X/S,g,\overline{s}),G) \quad\text{mod. inner automorphisms of~$G$.} \]

5.2

For every finite étale group scheme \(G\) over \(S\) whose fibres are \(\mathbb{L}\)-groups, the sheaf \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*G_X\) is canonically isomorphic to the sheaf associated with the presheaf

\[ S'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{Hom}_{S'}(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X/S,g,\overline{s}),G)\ \text{mod. inner automorphisms of~$G$.} \]

(\(S'\) denotes a scheme étale over \(S\)).

5.3

Let \(S'\) be a connected \(S\)-scheme, \(\overline{s}\) a geometric point of \(S'\), \(X'\), \(g'\) the respective inverse images of \(X,g\) on \(S'\). Then \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X'/S',g',\overline{s})\) is canonically isomorphic to the inverse image of \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X/S,g,\overline{s})\) on \(S'\). For every geometric point \(\xi\) of \(S\), the fibre \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X/S,g,\overline{s})_\xi\) is isomorphic to \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_\xi)\).

The datum of \(G\) is indeed equivalent to the datum of an abstract \(\mathbb{L}\)-group \(\mathbf{G}\) on which \(\pi_1(S,a)\) operates, whence an action of \(\pi_1'(X,a)\) on \(\mathbf{G}\). The isomorphism defined in 5.1 is then obtained by restriction to the subset \(E\) from the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(\pi_1'(X,a),\mathbf{G})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a),\mathbf{G}) = \operatorname{Hom}(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a),\mathbf{G})/\text{aut. int. }G, \]

the set \(E\) mapping bijectively onto the subset of the morphisms of \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\) into \(\mathbf{G}\) that are compatible with the action of \(\pi_1(S,a)\). The assertion 5.3 then follows from the definition of \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X/S,g,\overline{s})\) taking account of the exact homotopy sequence (3.1), and 5.2 is deduced from 5.1 and 5.3.

Proposition 6 (Künneth Formula)

Let \(k\) be a separably closed field of characteristic \(p\geqslant 0\), \(X\) and \(Y\) two connected \(k\)-schemes, \(a\) a geometric point of \(X\), \(b\) a geometric point of \(Y\), \(c\) a geometric point of \(X\times_k Y\) lying above \(a\) and \(b\). Suppose that one of the two following conditions is satisfied:

  1. a)

    \(X\) is of finite type over \(k\) and the schemes of finite type over an algebraic closure \(\overline{k}\) of \(k\), of dimension \(\leqslant\dim X\), are strongly desingularizable. (SGA 5 I 3.1.5).

  2. b)

    \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated and every scheme of finite type over \(\overline{k}\) is strongly desingularizable.

Then, if \(p'\) is the set of prime numbers distinct from \(p\), the morphism

\[ \tag{6.0} {} \pi_1^{p'}(X\times_k Y,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(X,a)\times\pi_1^{p'}(Y,b), \]

deduced from the homomorphisms on the fundamental groups associated with the projections

\[ X\times_k Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \quad \text{and} \quad X\times_k Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y, \]

is an isomorphism.

We may assume \(k\) algebraically closed and \(X\) reduced (SGA 4 VIII 1.1). Let \(Z=X\times_k Y\), \(g\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k\) and \(f\colon Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be the canonical morphisms. The morphism \(g\), and hence also \(f\), is universally locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(p'\) by 5. Since \(X\) is connected, \(f\) is \(0\)-acyclic (SGA 4 XV 1.16). On the other hand it follows from 2 that, for every finite \(p'\)-group \(C\), \((C_X,g)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(k\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\). It follows that \((C_Z,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(Y\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\) (5 c)) and that \(f_*C_Z\) and \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*C_Z\) are constant sheaves. Consequently \(g\) satisfies all the hypotheses of 2. We thus have the exact sequence

\[ \pi_1^{p'}(X_b,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(Z,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(Y,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1. \]

Moreover the composite morphism

\[ \pi_1^{p'}(X_b,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(Z,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(Y,b) \]

is an isomorphism and we thus have the exact sequence

\[ 1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(Z,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(Y,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1. \]

On the other hand the morphism (6.0) allows one to define a morphism from this exact sequence into the exact sequence

\[ 1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1^{p'}(X,a)\times\pi_1^{p'}(Y,b)\times\pi_1^{p'}(Y,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1, \]

and it follows that the morphism (6.0) is an isomorphism.

7

Let

\[ \xymatrix@C=1.2cm{{X}\ar[d]_-f&{X_U}\ar[l]\ar[d]_-{f_U}&{X_{\overline{s}}}\ar[l]\ar[d]\\{S}&{U}\ar[l]&{\overline{s}}\ar[l]} \]

be a diagram whose squares are Cartesian, where \(S\) is an arcwise connected scheme (SGA 4 IX 2.12), \(U\) a connected open of \(S\), \(\overline{s}\) a geometric point of \(U\). Let \(a\) be a geometric point of \(X_{\overline{s}}\), \(\mathbb{L}\) a set of prime numbers. Let \(g\) be a section of \(f\) and suppose that the following conditions are satisfied:

  1. a)

    The morphism \(f\) is \(0\)-acyclic, locally \(0\)-acyclic, and, for every étale covering \(S'\) of \(S\) and every étale covering \(E\) of \(X\times_S S'\) which is a quotient of a Galois covering whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group, \((E,f_{(S')})\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

  2. b)

    The morphism \(f_U\) is locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\), and, for every finite constant \(\mathbb{L}\)-group sheaf \(F\) on \(X_U\), \((F,f_U)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 1\) and the fibres of \(\mathrm{R}^1 f_{U*}F\) are finite.

One then deduces from 1 and 3 the following commutative diagram whose rows are exact:

\[ \tag{7.0} {} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{{1}\ar[r]&{\pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}},a)}\ar[r]\ar@{=}[d]&{\pi'_1(X_U,a)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{\pi_1(U,a)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{1}\\ &{\pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}},a)}\ar[r]&{\pi'_1(X,a)}\ar[r]&{\pi_1(S,a)}\ar[r]&{1}} \end{array} \]

Thanks to the section \(g\), we have morphisms

\[ \pi_1(U,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi'_1(X_U,a),\quad \pi_1(S,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi'_1(X,a)\,\; \]

from which one deduces a morphism from the amalgamated sum of \(\pi_1(S,a)\) and \(\pi'_1(X_U,a)\) over \(\pi_1(U,a)\) into \(\pi'_1(X,a)\):

\[ \tag{7.1} {} \varphi\colon\pi=\pi_1(S,a)\coprod_{\pi_1(U,a)}\pi'_1(X_U,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi'_1(X,a). \]

Suppose that the following condition is satisfied:

  1. c)

    If \(T=S-U\), we have \(\operatorname{prof} \mathrm{\acute{e}t}_T(S)\geqslant 2\) (SGA 2 XIV 1.1).

Then the functor which, to an étale covering of \(S\), makes correspond its restriction to \(U\) is fully faithful (SGA 2 XVI 1.4). It follows that the morphism \(\pi_1(U,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(S,a)\) is surjective (V 9) and one deduces from the diagram (7.0) that the same holds for the morphism \(\pi'_1(X_U,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi'_1(X,a)\); a fortiori \(\varphi\) is an epimorphism. Let

\[ \tag{7.2} {} K=\operatorname{Ker}(\pi_1(U,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(S,a)). \]

The group \(\pi\) of (7.1) is identified with the quotient of \(\pi'_1(X_U,a)\) by the closed invariant subgroup generated by the image \(L\) of \(K\) in \(\pi'_1(X_U,a)\). Consider \(\pi'_1(X_U,a)\) as a semi-direct product of \(\pi_1(U,a)\) by \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L} (X_{\overline{s}},a)\). The group \(K\) then acts by inner automorphisms on \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L} (X_{\overline{s}},a)\), and the quotient \(\pi=\pi'_1(X_U,a)/L\) is identified with the semi-direct product of \(\pi_1(U,a)/K=\pi_1(S,a)\) by the group \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)_K\) of coinvariants of \(\pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\) under \(K\). We finally have an epimorphism

\[ \tag{7.3} {} \varphi\colon\pi=\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)_K\cdot\pi_1(S,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi'_1(X,a). \]

The following proposition gives conditions under which the morphism \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.

Proposition 7.4

The notation is that of 7. We suppose that, in addition to the conditions a), b), c), the following conditions are satisfied:

  1. d)

    For every point \(t\) of \(T=S-U\), the morphism \(f\) is locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\) at \(g(t)\).

  2. e)

    For every point \(t\in T\), every irreducible component of the fibre \(X_t\) contains \(g(t)\) and, for every point \(x\) of \(X_t-\{g(t)\}\) which is not maximal, we have

    \[ \operatorname{prof}\operatorname{hop}_x(X)\geqslant 3 \quad\textup{(SGA~2 XIV~1.2)} \]

    and the ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is Noetherian.

Then the morphism (7.3) is an isomorphism.

As was said previously, the group \(\pi\) is identified with the quotient of \(\pi'_1(X_U,a)\) by the closed invariant subgroup \(L\) generated by the image of \(K\) (7.2) in \(\pi'_1(X_U,a)\). This amounts to saying that \(\pi\) classifies the principal coverings \(Z\) of \(X_U\) such that \(g^{-1}_U(Z)\) extends to an étale covering of \(S\), and which induce on \(X_{\overline{s}}\) a covering obtained by extension of the structural group from a principal covering whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group. To prove that \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, it suffices to show that such a covering \(Z\) extends to \(X\) entirely.

Let us first show that \(Z\) extends to an étale covering of an open set containing \(X_U\) and \(g(S)\). Let \(W\) be a scheme étale over \(X\) whose image contains \(X_U\) and \(g(S)\), and set \(W_U=W\times_S U\). From the fact that the morphism \(W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is \(0\)-acyclic and from the relation \(\mathrm{prof}.\,\mathrm{\acute{e}t}_T S\geqslant 2\), it follows that we have

\[ \mathrm{prof}.\,\mathrm{\acute{e}t}_{(W-W_U)} W\geqslant 2 \]

(SGA 2 XIV 1.13); consequently, if \(Z|W_U\) extends to an étale covering of \(W\), this extension is unique up to unique isomorphism. It follows that the problem of extending \(Z\) to a neighbourhood of \(g(S)\cup X_U\) is local for the étale topology in a neighbourhood of the points of \(g(T)\). If \(t\) is a point of \(T\), we set \(x=g(t)\), and we denote by \(\overline{X}\) (resp. \(\overline{S}\)) the strict localization of \(X\) at \(\overline{x}\) (resp. of \(S\) at \(\overline{t}\)), \(\overline{U}=U\times_S \overline{S}\), \(\overline{X}_U=\overline{X}\times_X X_U\), \(\overline{g}\colon\overline{S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{X}\) the morphism deduced from \(g\). It suffices to show that, for every point \(t\) of \(T\), the inverse image \(\overline{Z}\) of \(Z\) on \(\overline{X}_U\) extends to \(\overline{X}\) or, what amounts to the same, is trivial. Now, by definition of \(Z\), the inverse image of \(\overline{Z}\) on \(\overline{U}\) is trivial. To prove that \(\overline{Z}\) is trivial, it suffices to show that it is of the form \(\overline{f}^*_U E\), where \(E\) is a principal covering of \(\overline{U}\); indeed one will have \(E\simeq\overline{g}^*_U\overline{f}^*_U E \simeq\overline{g}^*_U\overline{Z}\), whence the result since \(\overline{g}^*_U\overline{Z}\) is trivial. Now the morphism \(\overline{f}_U\) being \(0\)-acyclic and locally \(0\)-acyclic, it suffices, in order to prove that \(\overline{Z}\) comes from \(\overline{U}\), to show that, for every geometric point algebraic over a point of \(\overline{U}\), which one may assume to be the point \(\overline{s}\), \(\overline{Z}|X_{\overline{s}}\) is trivial (SGA 4 XV 1.15). But \(\overline{Z}|X_{\overline{s}}\) being obtained by extension of the structural group starting from a principal covering with group an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group, this follows from the fact that the morphism \(\overline{f}\) is \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\).

We have thus proved that there exists an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(g(S)\cup X_U\) such that \(Z\) extends to an étale covering \(Z_V\) of \(V\). Let us show that \(Z_V\) extends to \(X\) in its entirety. It suffices to see that, for every point \(x\) of \(X-V\), we have

\[ \operatorname{prof}\operatorname{hop}_x X\geqslant 3. \]

Now this follows from hypothesis e) and from the fact that a point \(x\) of \(X-V\) cannot be maximal in its fibre \(X_t\) since, every irreducible component of \(X_t\) containing \(g(t)\), every maximal point of \(X_t\) belongs to \(V\).

Corollary 8

The hypotheses are those of 7.4 but one assumes in addition that one has \(\pi_1(S,a)=1\). Then one has an isomorphism

\[ \tag{*} {}\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X,a)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)_K. \]

In particular, if \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\) is topologically of finite presentation and if \(K\) operates on \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\) by means of a group of finite type, then \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X,a)\) is topologically of finite presentation.

The isomorphism (*) was proved in 7.4. Suppose \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\) is a quotient of the free pro-\(\mathbb{L}\)-group on \(n\) generators \(L(x_1,\dots,x_n)\) by the closed invariant subgroup generated by the elements \(y_1,\dots,y_p\) of \(L(x_1,\dots,x_n)\), and suppose that \(K\) acts by means of a group generated by elements \(k_1,\dots,k_q\). If for every \(i\in[1,n]\), \(j\in[1,q]\), one denotes by \(z_{ij}\) an element of \(L(x_1,\dots,x_n)\) lifting the element \((k_j\cdot x_i)x_i^{-1}\), then \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)_K\) is a quotient of \(L(x_1,\dots,x_n)\) by the closed invariant subgroup generated by the elements \((y_i)_{i\in[1,p]}\), \((z_{ij})_{i\in[1,n],j\in[1,q]}\).

Remarks 9

a) The conditions a) to e) of 7 are satisfied when \(S\) is a normal connected scheme, \(U\) a retrocompact dense open set of \(S\), \(f\) a proper morphism of finite presentation, with geometrically connected and irreducible fibres at every point \(t\) of \(T\), \(f\) being moreover separable, smooth at the points of \(X_U\cup g(T)\), \(\mathbb{L}\) being the set of prime numbers distinct from the residue characteristics of \(S\), and \(X\) being regular at every point of \(X_t\). The condition a) indeed follows from SGA 4 XV 4.1 and 1.4; the conditions b) and d) follow from 4 and SGA 4 XV 2.1 and XVI 5.2. Finally e) follows from SGA XIV 1.11.

b) The corollary 5 applies for computing the fundamental group \(\pi_1^{p'}(X)\) of a surface \(X\) proper and smooth over a separably closed field \(k\) of characteristic \(p\) (\(p'\) denoting the set of prime numbers distinct from \(p\)). The method was communicated to us by J.P. Murre; it consists in reducing, by blowing up \(X\), to the case where one has a fibration \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{P}^1_k\) and an open set \(U\) of \(\mathbf{P}^1_k\) satisfying the hypotheses of 7 (see SGA 7 for more details). The same method can be used more generally (loc. cit. ) to prove that, if \(X\) is a connected \(k\)-scheme of finite type, and, if the schemes of finite type of dimension \(\leqslant\dim X\) over an algebraic closure of \(k\) are strongly desingularizable (SGA 5 I 3.1.5), then \(\pi_1^{p'}(X)\) is topologically of finite presentation.

blocks.html: 4 Homotopy exact sequences

Let \(X\) and \(S\) be two connected schemes, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism, \(a\) a geometric point of \(X\), \(\mathbb{L}\) a set of prime numbers. Let \(K\) be the kernel of the canonical homomorphism \(\pi_1(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(S,a)\) and \(N\) the smallest normal pro-subgroup of \(K\) such that \(K/N\) is a pro-\(\mathbb{L}\)-group \(K^{\mathbb{L}}\). Then \(N\) is normal in \(\pi_1(X,a)\) and we denote by
\[ \pi_1'(X,a) \]
the quotient of \(\pi_1(X,a)\) by \(N\). If \(a\) is a geometric point of a geometric fibre \(X_{\overline{s}}\), the canonical morphisms
\[ \pi_1(X_{\overline{s}},a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X,a) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(S,a) \]
allow one to define canonical morphisms
\[ \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\xrightarrow{u} \pi_1'(X,a) \xrightarrow{v} \pi_1(S,a) \]
We have \(vu=0\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1

Let \(S\) be a connected scheme, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a locally \(0\)-acyclic morphism (SGA 4 XV 1.11); suppose moreover that \(f\) is \(0\)-acyclic (which, when \(f\) is coherent, amounts to saying that the geometric fibres of \(f\) are connected (SGA 4 XV 1.16)). Let \(\mathbb{L}\) be a set of prime numbers. If \(S'\) is a scheme étale over \(S\), we denote by \(X'\), \(f'\) the inverse images of \(X\), \(f\) over \(S'\). Suppose that, for every étale covering \(S'\) of \(S\) and for every étale covering \(E\) of \(X'\), a quotient of a Galois covering whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group, \((E,f')\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\) and \(f'_* E\) constructible. Then, if \(\overline{s}\) is a geometric point of \(S\) and \(a\) a geometric point of the fibre \(X_{\overline{s}}\), the sequence of group homomorphisms

\[ \tag{1.1} \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\xrightarrow{u} \pi_1'(X,a)\xrightarrow{v}\pi_1(S,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}1 \]

is exact
This statement generalizes X.4, whose proof we are going to copy.

Let us first show that \(v\) is surjective. It suffices to show that, for every connected étale covering \(S'\) of \(S\), \(X'\) is also connected (V 9). Let \(C\) be a set having at least two elements. It follows from the fact that \(f\) is \(0\)-acyclic that the canonical morphism
\[ \mathrm{H}^0(S',C_{S'})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^0(X',C_{X'}) \]
is bijective, hence that \(X'\) is connected, whence the surjectivity of \(v\).
By definition of \(K^{\mathbb{L}}\) (0), we have the exact sequence

\[ 1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K^{\mathbb{L}} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1'(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(S,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1 \]

Let \(\widetilde{S}\) be the universal covering of \(S\) and \(\widetilde{X}=\widetilde{S}\times_SX\); the group \(K^{\mathbb{L}}\) classifies the Galois coverings \(P\) whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group, such that there exists an étale covering \(S'\) of \(S\) and a Galois covering \(Q\) of \(X'=X\times_SS'\) such that one has an isomorphism \(P\simeq Q\times_{X'}\widetilde{X}\). In order for the sequence (1.1) to be exact, it is necessary and sufficient that the canonical morphism
\[ \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K^{\mathbb{L}} \]

be surjective. According to the interpretation of \(K^{\mathbb{L}}\), this amounts to saying that, for every étale covering \(S'\) of \(S\) and for every Galois covering \(Q\) of \(X'\) whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group, such that \(P=Q\times_{X'}\widetilde{X}\) is connected, then \(Q|X_{\overline{s}}\) is connected. Let us show that this last condition is satisfied. Indeed, let \(S'\) be an étale covering of \(S\), \(Q\) a Galois covering of \(X'\) whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group \(F\), such that \(Q|X_{\overline{s}}\) is disconnected, and let us show that, after replacing \(S'\) by an étale covering if necessary, \(Q\) becomes disconnected. There exists a subgroup \(G\) of \(F\) distinct from \(F\) and a torsor \(R\) under \(G|X_{\overline{s}}\) such that \(Q|X_{\overline{s}}\) is obtained by extension of the structural group \(G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F\) from \(R\). The étale covering \(E=Q/G\) of \(X'\) is such that \(E|X_{\overline{s}}\) has a section. According to 16, \(f_*E\) is locally constant constructible and, after replacing \(S'\) by an étale covering if necessary, one may even suppose that \(f_*E\) is constant. Since \((E,f')\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant0\) and since \(\mathrm{H}^0(X_{\overline{s}}, E|X_{\overline{s}})\) is nonempty, one sees that \(E\) has a section. But this proves that \(Q\) is disconnected, which completes the proof.
From 1 one deduces the following lemma, which will be used in 6.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2

Let \(S\) be a connected scheme, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism locally \(0\)-acyclic and \(0\)-acyclic, \(\mathbb{L}\) a set of prime numbers. Suppose that, for every sheaf of finite constant \(\mathbb{L}\)-groups \(F\) on \(X\), \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S\) in dimension \(\leqslant1\), and that, for every étale covering \(S'\) of \(S\) and for every étale covering \(E\) of \(X'\), a quotient of a Galois covering whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group, \(f'_*E\) is constructible. Then, if \(\overline{s}\) is a geometric point of \(S\) and \(a\) a geometric point of the fibre \(X_{\overline{s}}\), the sequence of group homomorphisms

\[ \pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1'(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(S,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}1 \]

is exact.

the hypotheses of 1 are satisfied. It indeed follows from 13 3) that, for every scheme \(S'\) étale over \(S\) and for every étale covering \(E\) of \(X'\), a quotient of a Galois covering whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group, \((E,f')\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3

Let \(S\) be a connected scheme, \(\mathbb{L}\) a set of prime numbers, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a \(0\)-acyclic morphism, locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\) (SGA 4 XV 1.11), \(g\colon S\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) a section of \(f\). Let \(\overline{s}\) be a geometric point of \(S\), \(a\) a geometric point of the fibre \(X_{\overline{s}}\). Suppose that, for every sheaf of constant \(\mathbb{L}\)-groups \(F\), \((F,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant1\), that the direct image by \(f\) of the stack of torsors under \(F\) is a \(1\)-constructible stack (0) and that, for every étale covering \(E\) of \(X'\), a quotient of a Galois covering whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group, \(f'_*E\) is constructible. Then the sequence of group homomorphisms

\[ \tag{3.1} 1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\xrightarrow{u} \pi_1'(X,a)\xrightarrow{v}\pi_1(S,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}1 \]

is exact.

Taking 2 into account, it suffices to show the injectivity of the morphism \(u\), i.e. to prove that, for every principal covering \(\overline{Z}\) of \(X_{\overline{s}}\) whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group \(C\), there exists an étale covering \(Z\) of \(X\) and a morphism from a connected component of \(Z|X_{\overline{s}}\) into \(\overline{Z}\) (V 8). So let \(\overline{Z}\) be a principal covering of \(X_{\overline{s}}\) whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group \(C\) and \(\overline{z}\) its class in \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_{\overline{s}},C_{X_{\overline{s}}})\). According to 5 d), one has a canonical isomorphism
\[ \left(\mathrm{R}^1f_*C_X\right)_{\overline{s}}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\mathrm{H}^1(X_{\overline{s}},C_{X_{\overline{s}}}), \]

and by 16, \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*C_X\) is a locally constant constructible sheaf. One can therefore find an étale covering \(S'\) of \(S\) such that \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*C_X|S'\) is constant. If \(\overline{s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) is a geometric point above the geometric point \(\overline{s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), there exists an element \(z\) of \(\mathrm{H}^0(S', \mathrm{R}^1f_*C_X)\) whose image in \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_{\overline{s}},C_{X_{\overline{s}}})\) is \(\overline{z}\). By Lemma 3.1 below, one can find an étale covering \(S'_1\) of \(S'\) and a torsor \(P\) on \(X'_1\) under the group \(C\) whose image in \(\mathrm{H}^0(S'_1,\mathrm{R}^1f_*C)\) is equal to the restriction of \(z\). The torsor \(P\) is representable by an étale covering \(Z\) of \(X'_1=X\times_SS'_1\) such that \(Z\times_{X'_1}X_{\overline{s}}\) is isomorphic to \(\overline{Z}\). If one considers \(Z\) as an étale covering of \(X\), one then has a morphism from \(Z\times_XX_{\overline{s}}\) into \(\overline{Z}\), which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a \(0\)-acyclic and locally \(0\)-acyclic morphism, and \(g\) a section of \(f\). Let \(C\) be a finite constant group such that \((C_X,f)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant0\) and such that the direct image by \(f\) of the stack of torsors under \(C_X\) is constructible. Then, for every section \(z\) of \(\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathrm{R}^1f_*C_X)\), one can find an étale covering \(S_1\) of \(S\) and, if \(X_1=X\times_SS_1\), an element of \(\mathrm{H}^1(X_1,C_{X_1})\), whose image under the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(X_1,C_{X_1})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathrm{H}^0(S_1,\mathrm{R}^1f_*C_{X_1}) \]

is equal to the restriction of \(z\) to \(\mathrm{H}^0(S_1,\mathrm{R}^1f_*C_{X_1})\).

For every scheme \(S'\) étale over \(S\), one sets \(X'=X\times_SS'\), and one denotes by \(g'\) (resp. \(F'\), etc.) the inverse image of \(g\) (resp. \(F\), etc.) by the morphism \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). The presheaf \(G\) on \(S\) defined by
\[ G(S') = \left\{\text{classes mod. isomorphism of torsors $P$ on~$X'$ under the group $C_{X'}$, equipped with an isomorphism $g'^*P\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} C_{S'}$ } \right\} \]

is then a sheaf. This indeed follows by descent from the fact that an isomorphism of a torsor \(P\) on \(X'\) is well determined by its restriction to \(g'(S')\). Moreover, one has a surjective morphism
\[ G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{R}^1f_*F. \]

Let \(z\) be an element of \(\mathrm{H}^0(S,\mathrm{R}^1f_*C_X)\) and \(H\) the subsheaf of \(G\) that is the inverse image of \(z\). It suffices to show that \(H\) is a locally constant constructible sheaf. Now, this property being local on \(S\), one may assume that \(z\) comes from an element of \(\mathrm{H}^1(X,C_X)\) represented by a torsor \(P\) such that \(g^*P\) is isomorphic to \(C_S\). To give an isomorphism \(i\colon g^*P\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} C_S\) amounts to giving a global section of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{C_S}(g^*P)\), and two isomorphisms \(i\) and \(i'\) define the same element of \(G(X)\) if and only if \(ii'^{-1}\) is the image of an element of \(\operatorname{Aut}_{C_X}(P)\). If one considers the canonical injection
\[ f_*\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{C_X}(P)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{C_S}(g^*P)\simeq C, \]

\(H\) is thus identified with the quotient of \(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{C_S}(g^*P)\) by \(f_*\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{C_X}(P)\). By 16 \(f_*\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{C_X}(P)\) is locally constant; the same is therefore true of \(H\), which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Examples
blocks.html:
Examples 4

Let us note that, if \(S\) is a connected scheme, the hypotheses of 1 are satisfied when the morphism \(f\) is proper flat of finite presentation, with separable connected geometric fibres, \(\mathbb{L}\) being arbitrary (cf. X 3). The hypotheses of 3 are satisfied if moreover \(f\) is smooth and has a section, provided one denotes by \(\mathbb{L}\) the set of prime numbers distinct from the residue characteristics of \(S\) (SGA 4 XV 2.1 and XVI 5.2).

The hypotheses of 1 are also satisfied if \(S\) is connected, if one has a proper scheme \(Z\) of finite presentation, flat over \(S\), with separable connected geometric fibres, such that \(X\) is the complement in \(Z\) of a divisor with normal crossings relative to \(S\), \(\mathbb{L}\) being the set of prime numbers distinct from the residue characteristics of \(S\) (9). The hypotheses of 3 are satisfied if moreover \(f\) is smooth and has a section.
Let us resume the notations and hypotheses of 3. If \(\overline{s}\) is a geometric point of \(S\) and \(a=g(\overline{s})\), the section \(g\) allows one to define a morphism
\[ w\colon \pi_1(S,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1'(X,a), \]

so that \(\pi_1'(X,a)\) is identified with the semi-direct product of \(\pi_1(S,a)\) by \(\pi_1(X_{\overline{s}},a)\). The pro-finite group \(\pi_1(S,a)\) thus operates on \(\pi_1(X_{\overline{s}}, a)\). Since \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\) is a strict inverse limit of groups invariant under the action of \(\pi_1(S,a)\), the datum of \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\) equipped with this action is equivalent to the datum of a strict inverse system of finite étale group schemes over \(S\), which one denotes by
\[ \pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X/S,g,\overline{s})\quad\text{or simply}\quad\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X/S,g). \]
One then has the following properties:

For every finite étale group scheme \(G\) over \(S\), whose fibres are \(\mathbb{L}\)-groups, the set \(E\) of classes of torsors \(P\) under the inverse image \(G_X\) of \(G\) on \(X\), equipped with an isomorphism \(g^*P\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} G\), is canonically isomorphic to the set
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_S(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X/S,g,\overline{s}),G) \quad\text{mod. inner automorphisms of~$G$.} \]
For every finite étale group scheme \(G\) over \(S\) whose fibres are \(\mathbb{L}\)-groups, the sheaf \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*G_X\) is canonically isomorphic to the sheaf associated with the presheaf
\[ S'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{Hom}_{S'}(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X/S,g,\overline{s}),G)\ \text{mod. inner automorphisms of~$G$.} \]
(\(S'\) denotes a scheme étale over \(S\)).

Let \(S'\) be a connected \(S\)-scheme, \(\overline{s}\) a geometric point of \(S'\), \(X'\), \(g'\) the respective inverse images of \(X,g\) on \(S'\). Then \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X'/S',g',\overline{s})\) is canonically isomorphic to the inverse image of \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X/S,g,\overline{s})\) on \(S'\). For every geometric point \(\xi\) of \(S\), the fibre \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X/S,g,\overline{s})_\xi\) is isomorphic to \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_\xi)\).

The datum of \(G\) is indeed equivalent to the datum of an abstract \(\mathbb{L}\)-group \(\mathbf{G}\) on which \(\pi_1(S,a)\) operates, whence an action of \(\pi_1'(X,a)\) on \(\mathbf{G}\). The isomorphism defined in 5.1 is then obtained by restriction to the subset \(E\) from the canonical morphism

\[ \mathrm{H}^1(\pi_1'(X,a),\mathbf{G})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{H}^1(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a),\mathbf{G}) = \operatorname{Hom}(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a),\mathbf{G})/\text{aut. int. }G, \]

the set \(E\) mapping bijectively onto the subset of the morphisms of \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\) into \(\mathbf{G}\) that are compatible with the action of \(\pi_1(S,a)\). The assertion 5.3 then follows from the definition of \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X/S,g,\overline{s})\) taking account of the exact homotopy sequence (3.1), and 5.2 is deduced from 5.1 and 5.3.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6 (Künneth Formula)

Let \(k\) be a separably closed field of characteristic \(p\geqslant 0\), \(X\) and \(Y\) two connected \(k\)-schemes, \(a\) a geometric point of \(X\), \(b\) a geometric point of \(Y\), \(c\) a geometric point of \(X\times_k Y\) lying above \(a\) and \(b\). Suppose that one of the two following conditions is satisfied:

  1. a)

    \(X\) is of finite type over \(k\) and the schemes of finite type over an algebraic closure \(\overline{k}\) of \(k\), of dimension \(\leqslant\dim X\), are strongly desingularizable. (SGA 5 I 3.1.5).

  2. b)

    \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated and every scheme of finite type over \(\overline{k}\) is strongly desingularizable.

Then, if \(p'\) is the set of prime numbers distinct from \(p\), the morphism

\[ \tag{6.0} {} \pi_1^{p'}(X\times_k Y,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(X,a)\times\pi_1^{p'}(Y,b), \]

deduced from the homomorphisms on the fundamental groups associated with the projections

\[ X\times_k Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \quad \text{and} \quad X\times_k Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y, \]

is an isomorphism.

We may assume \(k\) algebraically closed and \(X\) reduced (SGA 4 VIII 1.1). Let \(Z=X\times_k Y\), \(g\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k\) and \(f\colon Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be the canonical morphisms. The morphism \(g\), and hence also \(f\), is universally locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(p'\) by 5. Since \(X\) is connected, \(f\) is \(0\)-acyclic (SGA 4 XV 1.16). On the other hand it follows from 2 that, for every finite \(p'\)-group \(C\), \((C_X,g)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(k\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\). It follows that \((C_Z,f)\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(Y\) in dimension \(\leqslant 1\) (5 c)) and that \(f_*C_Z\) and \(\mathrm{R}^1f_*C_Z\) are constant sheaves. Consequently \(g\) satisfies all the hypotheses of 2. We thus have the exact sequence

\[ \pi_1^{p'}(X_b,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(Z,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(Y,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1. \]
Moreover the composite morphism
\[ \pi_1^{p'}(X_b,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(Z,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(Y,b) \]
is an isomorphism and we thus have the exact sequence

\[ 1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(Z,c)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(Y,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1. \]
On the other hand the morphism (6.0) allows one to define a morphism from this exact sequence into the exact sequence

\[ 1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1^{p'}(X,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1^{p'}(X,a)\times\pi_1^{p'}(Y,b)\times\pi_1^{p'}(Y,b)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1, \]
and it follows that the morphism (6.0) is an isomorphism.
Let
\[ \xymatrix@C=1.2cm{{X}\ar[d]_-f&{X_U}\ar[l]\ar[d]_-{f_U}&{X_{\overline{s}}}\ar[l]\ar[d]\\{S}&{U}\ar[l]&{\overline{s}}\ar[l]} \]

be a diagram whose squares are Cartesian, where \(S\) is an arcwise connected scheme (SGA 4 IX 2.12), \(U\) a connected open of \(S\), \(\overline{s}\) a geometric point of \(U\). Let \(a\) be a geometric point of \(X_{\overline{s}}\), \(\mathbb{L}\) a set of prime numbers. Let \(g\) be a section of \(f\) and suppose that the following conditions are satisfied:

1. a)

    The morphism \(f\) is \(0\)-acyclic, locally \(0\)-acyclic, and, for every étale covering \(S'\) of \(S\) and every étale covering \(E\) of \(X\times_S S'\) which is a quotient of a Galois covering whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group, \((E,f_{(S')})\) is cohomologically proper relatively to \(S'\) in dimension \(\leqslant 0\).

  2. b)

    The morphism \(f_U\) is locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\), and, for every finite constant \(\mathbb{L}\)-group sheaf \(F\) on \(X_U\), \((F,f_U)\) is cohomologically proper in dimension \(\leqslant 1\) and the fibres of \(\mathrm{R}^1 f_{U*}F\) are finite.
One then deduces from 1 and 3 the following commutative diagram whose rows are exact:

\[ \tag{7.0} {} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{{1}\ar[r]&{\pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}},a)}\ar[r]\ar@{=}[d]&{\pi'_1(X_U,a)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{\pi_1(U,a)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{1}\\ &{\pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}},a)}\ar[r]&{\pi'_1(X,a)}\ar[r]&{\pi_1(S,a)}\ar[r]&{1}} \end{array} \]
Thanks to the section \(g\), we have morphisms
\[ \pi_1(U,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi'_1(X_U,a),\quad \pi_1(S,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi'_1(X,a)\,\; \]
from which one deduces a morphism from the amalgamated sum of \(\pi_1(S,a)\) and \(\pi'_1(X_U,a)\) over \(\pi_1(U,a)\) into \(\pi'_1(X,a)\):
\[ \tag{7.1} {} \varphi\colon\pi=\pi_1(S,a)\coprod_{\pi_1(U,a)}\pi'_1(X_U,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi'_1(X,a). \]
Suppose that the following condition is satisfied:

1. c)

    If \(T=S-U\), we have \(\operatorname{prof} \mathrm{\acute{e}t}_T(S)\geqslant 2\) (SGA 2 XIV 1.1).

Then the functor which, to an étale covering of \(S\), makes correspond its restriction to \(U\) is fully faithful (SGA 2 XVI 1.4). It follows that the morphism \(\pi_1(U,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(S,a)\) is surjective (V 9) and one deduces from the diagram (7.0) that the same holds for the morphism \(\pi'_1(X_U,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi'_1(X,a)\); a fortiori \(\varphi\) is an epimorphism. Let
\[ \tag{7.2} {} K=\operatorname{Ker}(\pi_1(U,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(S,a)). \]

The group \(\pi\) of (7.1) is identified with the quotient of \(\pi'_1(X_U,a)\) by the closed invariant subgroup generated by the image \(L\) of \(K\) in \(\pi'_1(X_U,a)\). Consider \(\pi'_1(X_U,a)\) as a semi-direct product of \(\pi_1(U,a)\) by \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L} (X_{\overline{s}},a)\). The group \(K\) then acts by inner automorphisms on \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L} (X_{\overline{s}},a)\), and the quotient \(\pi=\pi'_1(X_U,a)/L\) is identified with the semi-direct product of \(\pi_1(U,a)/K=\pi_1(S,a)\) by the group \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)_K\) of coinvariants of \(\pi_1^{\mathbb{L}}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\) under \(K\). We finally have an epimorphism
\[ \tag{7.3} {} \varphi\colon\pi=\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)_K\cdot\pi_1(S,a)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi'_1(X,a). \]
The following proposition gives conditions under which the morphism \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.4

The notation is that of 7. We suppose that, in addition to the conditions a), b), c), the following conditions are satisfied:

  1. d)

    For every point \(t\) of \(T=S-U\), the morphism \(f\) is locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\) at \(g(t)\).

  2. e)

    For every point \(t\in T\), every irreducible component of the fibre \(X_t\) contains \(g(t)\) and, for every point \(x\) of \(X_t-\{g(t)\}\) which is not maximal, we have

    \[ \operatorname{prof}\operatorname{hop}_x(X)\geqslant 3 \quad\textup{(SGA~2 XIV~1.2)} \]

    and the ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is Noetherian.

Then the morphism (7.3) is an isomorphism.

As was said previously, the group \(\pi\) is identified with the quotient of \(\pi'_1(X_U,a)\) by the closed invariant subgroup \(L\) generated by the image of \(K\) (7.2) in \(\pi'_1(X_U,a)\). This amounts to saying that \(\pi\) classifies the principal coverings \(Z\) of \(X_U\) such that \(g^{-1}_U(Z)\) extends to an étale covering of \(S\), and which induce on \(X_{\overline{s}}\) a covering obtained by extension of the structural group from a principal covering whose group is an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group. To prove that \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, it suffices to show that such a covering \(Z\) extends to \(X\) entirely.

Let us first show that \(Z\) extends to an étale covering of an open set containing \(X_U\) and \(g(S)\). Let \(W\) be a scheme étale over \(X\) whose image contains \(X_U\) and \(g(S)\), and set \(W_U=W\times_S U\). From the fact that the morphism \(W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is \(0\)-acyclic and from the relation \(\mathrm{prof}.\,\mathrm{\acute{e}t}_T S\geqslant 2\), it follows that we have
\[ \mathrm{prof}.\,\mathrm{\acute{e}t}_{(W-W_U)} W\geqslant 2 \]

(SGA 2 XIV 1.13); consequently, if \(Z|W_U\) extends to an étale covering of \(W\), this extension is unique up to unique isomorphism. It follows that the problem of extending \(Z\) to a neighbourhood of \(g(S)\cup X_U\) is local for the étale topology in a neighbourhood of the points of \(g(T)\). If \(t\) is a point of \(T\), we set \(x=g(t)\), and we denote by \(\overline{X}\) (resp. \(\overline{S}\)) the strict localization of \(X\) at \(\overline{x}\) (resp. of \(S\) at \(\overline{t}\)), \(\overline{U}=U\times_S \overline{S}\), \(\overline{X}_U=\overline{X}\times_X X_U\), \(\overline{g}\colon\overline{S}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{X}\) the morphism deduced from \(g\). It suffices to show that, for every point \(t\) of \(T\), the inverse image \(\overline{Z}\) of \(Z\) on \(\overline{X}_U\) extends to \(\overline{X}\) or, what amounts to the same, is trivial. Now, by definition of \(Z\), the inverse image of \(\overline{Z}\) on \(\overline{U}\) is trivial. To prove that \(\overline{Z}\) is trivial, it suffices to show that it is of the form \(\overline{f}^*_U E\), where \(E\) is a principal covering of \(\overline{U}\); indeed one will have \(E\simeq\overline{g}^*_U\overline{f}^*_U E \simeq\overline{g}^*_U\overline{Z}\), whence the result since \(\overline{g}^*_U\overline{Z}\) is trivial. Now the morphism \(\overline{f}_U\) being \(0\)-acyclic and locally \(0\)-acyclic, it suffices, in order to prove that \(\overline{Z}\) comes from \(\overline{U}\), to show that, for every geometric point algebraic over a point of \(\overline{U}\), which one may assume to be the point \(\overline{s}\), \(\overline{Z}|X_{\overline{s}}\) is trivial (SGA 4 XV 1.15). But \(\overline{Z}|X_{\overline{s}}\) being obtained by extension of the structural group starting from a principal covering with group an \(\mathbb{L}\)-group, this follows from the fact that the morphism \(\overline{f}\) is \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\).

We have thus proved that there exists an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(g(S)\cup X_U\) such that \(Z\) extends to an étale covering \(Z_V\) of \(V\). Let us show that \(Z_V\) extends to \(X\) in its entirety. It suffices to see that, for every point \(x\) of \(X-V\), we have
\[ \operatorname{prof}\operatorname{hop}_x X\geqslant 3. \]

Now this follows from hypothesis e) and from the fact that a point \(x\) of \(X-V\) cannot be maximal in its fibre \(X_t\) since, every irreducible component of \(X_t\) containing \(g(t)\), every maximal point of \(X_t\) belongs to \(V\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8

The hypotheses are those of 7.4 but one assumes in addition that one has \(\pi_1(S,a)=1\). Then one has an isomorphism

\[ \tag{*} {}\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X,a)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)_K. \]

In particular, if \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\) is topologically of finite presentation and if \(K\) operates on \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\) by means of a group of finite type, then \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X,a)\) is topologically of finite presentation.

The isomorphism (*) was proved in 7.4. Suppose \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)\) is a quotient of the free pro-\(\mathbb{L}\)-group on \(n\) generators \(L(x_1,\dots,x_n)\) by the closed invariant subgroup generated by the elements \(y_1,\dots,y_p\) of \(L(x_1,\dots,x_n)\), and suppose that \(K\) acts by means of a group generated by elements \(k_1,\dots,k_q\). If for every \(i\in[1,n]\), \(j\in[1,q]\), one denotes by \(z_{ij}\) an element of \(L(x_1,\dots,x_n)\) lifting the element \((k_j\cdot x_i)x_i^{-1}\), then \(\pi_1^\mathbb{L}(X_{\overline{s}},a)_K\) is a quotient of \(L(x_1,\dots,x_n)\) by the closed invariant subgroup generated by the elements \((y_i)_{i\in[1,p]}\), \((z_{ij})_{i\in[1,n],j\in[1,q]}\).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 9

a) The conditions a) to e) of 7 are satisfied when \(S\) is a normal connected scheme, \(U\) a retrocompact dense open set of \(S\), \(f\) a proper morphism of finite presentation, with geometrically connected and irreducible fibres at every point \(t\) of \(T\), \(f\) being moreover separable, smooth at the points of \(X_U\cup g(T)\), \(\mathbb{L}\) being the set of prime numbers distinct from the residue characteristics of \(S\), and \(X\) being regular at every point of \(X_t\). The condition a) indeed follows from SGA 4 XV 4.1 and 1.4; the conditions b) and d) follow from 4 and SGA 4 XV 2.1 and XVI 5.2. Finally e) follows from SGA XIV 1.11.

b) The corollary 5 applies for computing the fundamental group \(\pi_1^{p'}(X)\) of a surface \(X\) proper and smooth over a separably closed field \(k\) of characteristic \(p\) (\(p'\) denoting the set of prime numbers distinct from \(p\)). The method was communicated to us by J.P. Murre; it consists in reducing, by blowing up \(X\), to the case where one has a fibration \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{P}^1_k\) and an open set \(U\) of \(\mathbf{P}^1_k\) satisfying the hypotheses of 7 (see SGA 7 for more details). The same method can be used more generally (loc. cit. ) to prove that, if \(X\) is a connected \(k\)-scheme of finite type, and, if the schemes of finite type of dimension \(\leqslant\dim X\) over an algebraic closure of \(k\) are strongly desingularizable (SGA 5 I 3.1.5), then \(\pi_1^{p'}(X)\) is topologically of finite presentation.

pages.title: Appendix I: Variations on Abhyankar's lemma
pages.html:
5 Appendix I: Variations on Abhyankar's lemma

This appendix contains various variants of Abhyankar's lemma.

Proposition 1

Let \(X=\operatorname{Spec} A\) be a regular local scheme, \(D=\sum_{1\leqslant i\leqslant r} \operatorname{div} f_i\) a normal crossings divisor, where the \(f_i\) are elements of the maximal ideal of \(A\) forming part of a regular system of parameters. Let \(n_i\), \(1\leqslant i\leqslant r\), be integers \(\geqslant 0\) and set

\[ X'=X[T_1,\dots,T_r]/(T_1^{n_1}-f_1,\dots,T_r^{n_r}-f_r), \]

\(U'=U\times_X X'\). Then \(X'\) is regular and \(U'\) is the complement in \(X'\) of the normal crossings divisor \(\sum_{1\leqslant i\leqslant r}\operatorname{div} T_i\). If the integers \(n_i\) are prime to the residue characteristic \(p\) of \(X\), \(U'\) is a connected étale covering of \(U\), tamely ramified relatively to \(D\) (3 c)).

Indeed \(X'\) is the spectrum of a local ring \(A'\) whose maximal ideal is generated by \(T_1,\dots,T_r\). (One may assume \(r=\dim(A)\).) Since \(A'\) is finite and flat over \(A\), hence of dimension \(r\), \(A'\) is regular (EGA \(0_{\textup{IV}}\) 17.1.1) and the \(T_i\) form a regular system of parameters of \(A'\). Suppose the \(n_i\) prime to \(p\). Since all the \(f_i\) are invertible on \(U\), the fact that \(U'\) is étale over \(U\) follows from I 4. Moreover \(U'\) is tamely ramified relatively to \(D\); indeed let \(x_i\) be the generic point of \(V(f_i)\), \(\overline{R}\) the strict localization of \(R=\mathscr{O}_{X,x_i}\), \(\overline{K}\) the field of fractions of \(\overline{R}\). Then the \(\overline{K}\)-algebra which represents \(U'|\overline{K}\) is obtained from the field \(\overline{K}[T_i]/(T_i^{n_i}-f_i)\) by making an unramified extension; it is therefore tamely ramified over \(\overline{R}\).

Proposition 2 (Absolute Abhyankar lemma)

Let \(X\) be a regular local scheme,

\[ D=\sum_{1\leqslant i\leqslant r} \operatorname{div} f_i \]

a normal crossings divisor as in 1, \(Y=\operatorname{Supp} D\), \(U=X-Y\). Let \(V\) be an étale covering of \(U\) tamely ramified relatively to \(D\). If \(x_i\) is the generic point of the closed set \(V(f_i)\), \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x_i}\) is a discrete valuation ring with field of fractions \(K_i\), and one has \(V|K_i=\operatorname{Spec}(\prod_{j\in J_i}L_j)\), where the \(L_j\) are finite separable extensions of \(K_i\); let us denote by \(n_j\) the order of the inertia group of a Galois extension generated by \(L_j\) and by \(n_i\) the l.c.m. of the \(n_j\) as \(j\) ranges over \(J_i\). If one sets

\[ X'=X[T_1,\dots,T_r]/(T_1^{n_1}-f_1,\dots,T_r^{n_r}-f_r), \]

\(U'=U_{(X')}\), \(V'=V_{(X')}\), etc., the étale covering \(V'\) of \(U'\) extends uniquely, up to unique isomorphism, to an étale covering of \(X'\), and the \(n_i\) are prime to the residue characteristic \(p\) of \(X\).

The uniqueness follows from the fact that \(X'\) is normal (1); indeed an étale covering of \(X'\) which extends \(V'\) is isomorphic to the normalization of \(X'\) in the fibre of \(V'\) at the generic point of \(X'\) (2). If \(\overline{x}'\) is a geometric point of \(Y'\), one denotes by \(\overline{X}'\) the strict localization of \(X'\) at \(\overline{x}'\), \(\overline{V}'=V'_{(\overline{X}')}\), etc. By descent, taking into account uniqueness, it suffices to show that, for every geometric point \(\overline{x}'\) of \(Y'\), the étale covering \(\overline{V}'\) of \(\overline{U}'\) extends to \(\overline{X}'\). Given that an étale covering of an open set of the regular scheme \(X'\) which contains all the points \(x'\) such that one has \(\dim \mathscr{O}_{X',x'}\leqslant 1\) extends to the whole of \(X\) (SGA 2 XIV 1.11), one may even restrict to the points \(\overline{x}'\) which project onto a maximal point of \(Y'\). Now, at such a point \(\overline{x}'\), the fact that \(\overline{V}'\) extends to an étale covering of \(\overline{X}'\) follows from 6.

Let us show that the \(n_i\) are prime to \(p\). Indeed, if this were not the case, one would have for instance \(p|n_1\). Up to replacing \(X\) by

\[ X[T_1,\dots,T_r]/(T_1^{n_1/p}-f_1,T_2^{n_2}-f_2,\dots,T_r^{n_r}-f_r), \]

one reduces to the case where one has \(X'=X[T_1]/(T_1^p-f_1)\). It suffices to show that \(V\) extends to an étale covering of \(X\), for one will then have \(n_1=1\) contrary to the hypothesis. For this one may suppose that \(X\) is strictly local. Let \(Z\) be the closed subscheme of \(X\) with equation \(p=0\) and \(Z_1=Z\cap X_{f_1}\); \(Z_1\) is a non-empty open set of \(Z\). By what precedes, the étale covering \(V'\) of \(U'\) extends to an étale covering \(W'\) of \(X'\). Let \(W''_1\) and \(W''_2\) be the inverse images of \(W'\) by the two projections \(X''=X'\times_X X'\rightrightarrows X'\), and let us show that the descent isomorphism \(u\colon W''_1|U''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} W''_2|U''\) extends to an \(X''\)-morphism \(W''_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} W''_2\) which will necessarily be a descent datum on \(W'\) relatively to \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\). Let \(Z''\) (resp. \(Z''_1\)) be the inverse image of \(Z\) (resp. \(Z_1\)) in \(X''\). Since the morphism \(Z''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) is radicial, there exists an isomorphism \(v\colon W''_1|Z''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} W''_2|Z''\) which extends the isomorphism \(u|Z''_1\). But, since \(X\) is Henselian, one has a bijection

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{X''}(W''_1,W''_2)\simeq\operatorname{Hom}_{Z''}(W''_1|Z'',W_2''|Z''), \]

whence a morphism \(w\colon W''_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} W''_2\) lifting \(v\). The subscheme, both open and closed, of \(X''\) above which \(u\) and \(w\) coincide contains \(Z''_1\), hence equal to \(X''\), whence the fact that \(V\) extends to \(X\).

3.0

Let us resume the hypotheses and notations of 2, supposing moreover \(X\) strictly local. Then it follows from loc. cit. that every connected étale covering of \(U\) tamely ramified relatively to \(D\) is a quotient of a tamely ramified covering of the form

\[ U'=U[T_1,\dots,T_r]/(T_1^{n_1}-f_1,\dots,T_r^{n_r}-f_r), \]

where the \(n_i\) are integers prime to \(p\). Let \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) be the group of \(n\)-th roots of unity of \(U\). The group of \(U\)-automorphisms of \(U'\) is none other than the group \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n_1}\times\dots\times\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n_r}\), an \(n_i\)-th root of unity \(\xi_i\) operating on \(U'\) by transforming \(T_i\) into \(\xi_i T_i\). One thus has the following statement:

Corollary 3

Let \(X\) be a strictly local regular scheme of residue characteristic \(p\geqslant 0\), \(D=\sum_{1\leqslant i\leqslant n} \operatorname{div} f_i\) a normal crossings divisor on \(X\), \(U=X-\operatorname{Supp} D\). Set

\[ \widetilde{U}=\varprojlim_{(n_i)} U[T_1,\dots,T_r]/(T_1^{n_1}-f_1,\dots,T_r^{n_r}-f_r), \]

the inverse limit being taken along the filtered ordered set (for the divisibility relation) of the families of integers \(n_i>0\), prime to \(p\). Then \(\widetilde{U}\) is a universal tamely ramified covering of \(U\). Consequently the tamely ramified fundamental group of \(U\) is

\[ \pi_1^{\mathrm{t}}(U)\simeq \prod_{\ell\neq p} \mathbf{Z}_\ell [1]^r \qquad \text{(canonical isomorphism)} \]

where one has set \(\mathbf{Z}_\ell[1]=\varprojlim_{n>0}\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\ell^n}\). The group \(\pi_1^{\mathrm{t}}(U)\) is non-canonically isomorphic to \(\prod_{\ell\neq p} \mathbf{Z}_\ell^r\).

Proposition 4

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(D=\sum_{1\leqslant i\leqslant r} \operatorname{div} f_i\) a normal crossings divisor relatively to \(S\) (1), where, for each point \(x\) of \(Y=\operatorname{Supp} D\), if \(I(x)\subset[1,r]\) is the set of the \(i\) such that one has \(f_i(x)=0\), the subscheme \(V((f_i)_{i\in I(x)})\) is smooth over \(S\) of codimension \(\operatorname{card} I(x)\) in \(X\). Let \(U=X-Y\). Let \(x\) be a point of \(Y\), \(X_1=\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\), \(U_1=U\times_X X_1\), \(n_i\), \(i\in I(x)\) integers and

\[ X'=X[T_i]_{i\in I(x)}/(T_i^{n_i}-f_i). \]

Then, if \(x'\) is the point of \(X'\) above \(x\), \(X'\) is smooth over \(S\) at \(x'\). If the integers \(n_i\) are prime to the characteristic \(p\) of \(\mathit{k}(x)\), \(U'_1=U_1\times_X X'\) is a connected étale covering of \(U_1\) tamely ramified over \(X_1\) relatively to \(S_1\) (1.1).

If \(s=f(x)\), the geometric fibre \(X'_{\overline{s}}\) is regular at the point \(x'\) (1); since \(X'\) is flat over \(S\) in a neighbourhood of \(Y\), this proves that \(X'\) is smooth over \(S\) at \(x'\) (EGA IV 12.1.6). If the integers \(n_i\) are prime to \(p\), \(U'_1\) is an étale covering of \(U_1\) (4); it is tamely ramified over \(X_1\) relatively to \(S\) because this is so on the geometric fibres at each point of \(S\) (1). Finally the fact that \(U'_1\) is connected follows from (SGA 4 XVI 3.2).

Proposition 5 (Relative Abhyankar Lemma)

Let \(X\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(D\) a normal crossings divisor relatively to \(S\), as in (4). Let \(Y=X-\operatorname{Supp} D\), \(U=X- Y\), let \(x\) be a point of \(Y\), \(X_{1}\) the strict localization of \(X\) at a geometric point above \(x\), \(U_{1}=U\times_{X}X_{1}\), \(V_{1}\) an étale covering of \(U_{1}\). We suppose that, for every maximal point \(s\) of \(S\), \(V_{1\overline{s}}\) is tamely ramified over \(X_{1\overline{s}}\) relatively to \(\overline{s}\). Then one can find integers \(n_{i}\) prime to the characteristic \(p\) of \(\mathit{k}(x)\), with \(i\in I(x)\), such that, if one sets

\[ X'_{1}=X_{1}[T_{i}]_{i\in I(x)}\big/ (T_{i}^{n_{i}}-f_{i}) , \]

\(U'_{1}=U_{1}\times_{X_{1}}X'_{1}\), etc., the étale covering \(V'_{1}\) of \(U'_{1}\) extends in a unique way up to unique isomorphism to an étale covering of \(X'_{1}\). In particular \(V_{1}\) is tamely ramified over \(X_{1}\) relatively to \(S\).

One may suppose \(S\) local Noetherian with closed point \(f(x)\). For each maximal point \(s\) of \(S\) and for each \(i\in I(x)\), let \(x_{i}\) be the generic point of the closed set \(V(f_{i})\) of the fibre \(X_{1\overline{s}}\). The local ring \((\mathscr{O}_{X_{1},x_{i}})_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is a discrete valuation ring with field of fractions \(K_{i}\) and one has \(V_{1}|K_{i}=\operatorname{Spec}(\prod_{j\in I(x_{i})}L_{j})\), where \(L_{j}\) is a finite separable extension of \(K\); one denotes by \(n_{j}\) the order of the inertia group of a Galois extension generated by \(L_{j}\) and by \(n_{i}\) the l.c.m. of the \(n_{j}\) when \(s\) runs through the maximal points of \(S\) and \(j\in I(x_{i})\).

The \(n_{i}\) being thus chosen, we are going to show that \(V'_{1}\) extends in a unique way to an étale covering of \(X'_{1}\). The uniqueness follows from the fact that, \(X'\) being smooth over \(S\) at the points of \(Y\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}\mathrm{\acute{e}t}_{Y'_{1}}(X'_{1})\geqslant 2\) (SGA 4 XVI 3.2 or SGA 2 XIV 1.19). Let \(x'_{1}\) be a point of \(Y'_{1}\), \(\overline{x}'_{1}\) a geometric point above \(x'_{1}\), and let us denote by \(\overline{X'_{1}}\) the strict localization of \(X'_{1}\) at \(\overline{x}'_{1}\), \(\overline{U'_{1}}=U'_{1(\overline{X'_{1}})}\), etc. By descent, taking account of the uniqueness, it suffices to show that \(\overline{V}'_{1}\) extends to \(\overline{X'_{1}}\). Moreover one may restrict oneself to taking for \(x'_{1}\) the maximal points of \(Y'_{1}\); indeed one will then have an extension of \(V'_{1}\) over an open set \(W'_{1}\) of \(X'_{1}\) containing the maximal points of \(Y'_{1}\); now, if \(Z'_{1}=X'_{1}- W'_{1}\), one has \(\operatorname{codim}(Z'_{1s},X'_{1s})\geqslant 2\) if \(s\) is a maximal point of \(S\) and \(\operatorname{codim}(Z'_{1s},X'_{1s})\geqslant 1\), \(\operatorname{prof}\mathrm{\acute{e}t}_{s}(S)\geqslant 1\) if \(s\) is a point of \(S\) which is not maximal; the fact that \(V'_{1}\) extends to the whole of \(X'_{1}\) then follows from (SGA 2 XIV 1.20). But, at a geometric point \(\overline{x}'_{1}\) above a maximal point of \(Y'_{1}\), \(X'_{1\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, and the fact that \(\overline{V'_{1}}\) extends to \(\overline{X'_{1}}\) then follows from X 6.

Let us show that the \(n_{i}\) are prime to \(p\). Indeed, if this were not so, one would have an index \(i_{0}\in I(x)\) such that \(p\) divides \(n_{i_{0}}\). Up to replacing \(X\) by \(X_{1}[T_{i_{0}},T_{i}]_{i\in I(x)}\big/ (T_{i}^{n_{i_{0}}/p}-f_{i_{0}},T_{i}^{n_{i}}-f_{i})\), one reduces to the case where \(X'_{1}=X_{1}[T]/T^{p}-f_{i_{0}}\). By what precedes the étale covering \(V'_{1}\) of \(U'_{1}\) extends to an étale covering \(E'_{1}\) of \(X'_{1}\). Let \(\eta\) be the closed point of \(S\); since the morphism \(X'_{1\eta}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{1\eta}\) is radicial, \(V_{1\eta}\) extends to an étale covering \(E_{1\eta}\) of \(X_{1\eta}\). One then deduces from this, as in 2, that \(E'_{1}\) is equipped with a descent datum relatively to the morphism \(X'_{1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{1}\), which extends the natural descent datum that one has on \(E'_{1}|U'_{1}\). It follows that \(V_{1}\) extends to \(X_{1}\); but this entails that one has \(n_{i_{0}}=1\) contrary to the hypothesis \(n_{i_{0}}=p\).

Corollary 6

Let \(X\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(D=\sum_{1\leqslant i\leqslant r}\operatorname{div} f_{i}\) a normal crossings divisor relatively to \(S\), as in 4. Let \(\overline{x}\) be a geometric point of \(X\), \(\overline{X}\) the strict localization of \(X\) at \(\overline{x}\), \(\overline{Y}=Y_{(\overline{X})}\), \(\overline{U}=\overline{X}-\overline{Y}\) and

\[ \widetilde{U}= \varprojlim_{(n_{i})} \overline{U}[T_{i}]_{i\in I(x)}\big/ (T_{i}^{n_{i}}-f_{i}) , \]

the inverse limit being taken over the filtered set of families of integers \(n_{i}>0\), prime to the characteristic \(p\) of \(\mathit{k}(x)\). Then \(\widetilde{U}\) is a universal tamely ramified covering of \(\overline{U}\) relatively to \(S\). Consequently the tamely ramified fundamental group of \(\overline{U}\) is

\[ \pi_{1}^{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U})\simeq \prod_{\ell\neq p}\mathbf{Z}_{\ell}[1]^{I(x)} \qquad \textrm{(canonical isomorphism).} \]

The group \(\pi_{1}^{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U})\) is non-canonically isomorphic to \(\prod_{\ell\neq p}\mathbf{Z}_{\ell}^{I(x)}\).

Remark 6.1

Let \(X\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(D=\sum_{1\leqslant i\leqslant r}\operatorname{div} f_{i}\) a normal crossings divisor relatively to \(S\), as in 4, \(U=X-\operatorname{Supp} D\). For every subset \(I\subset[1,r]\) let

\[ X_{I}= \Big(\mathop{\textstyle\bigcap}\limits_{i\in I}V(f_{i})\Big) \cap \Big(\mathop{\textstyle\bigcap}\limits_{i\in\complement I}X_{f_{i}}\Big) . \]

Let \(p\) be a prime or zero integer and let \(Z\) be a subset of \(X_{I}\) all of whose points are of characteristic \(p\). Let

\[ \widetilde{U}_{I}= \varprojlim_{(n_{i})} U[T_{i}]_{i\in I} \big/ (T_{i}^{n_{i}}-f_{i}) , \]

where the inverse limit is taken over the filtered set of families of integers \(n_{i}>0\), prime to \(p\). Then, for every geometric point \(\overline{x}\) of \(Z\), the inverse image of \(\widetilde{U}_{I}\) over \(\overline{U}\) is identified with the universal tamely ramified covering of \(\overline{U}\).

Corollary 7

The notations are those of 6. Let \(\overline{S}\) be the strict localization of \(S\) at \(\overline{x}\),

\[ \overline{g}\colon\overline{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{S} \quad \text{and} \quad \widetilde{g}\colon\widetilde{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\widetilde{S} \]

the canonical morphisms. Then the morphisms \(\overline{g}\) and \(\widetilde{g}\) are \(0\)-acyclic (SGA 4 XV 1.3). Let \(G\) be a constructible sheaf of groups on \(\overline{S}\), \(F=\overline{g}^*G\), \(P\) a torsor under \(F\). Then, in order that \(P\) be tamely ramified over \(\overline{X}\) relatively to \(\overline{S}\), it is necessary and sufficient that its inverse image \(\widetilde{P}\) over \(\widetilde{U}\) be trivial.
