Indeed, for every scheme \(\overline{X'}=\overline{X}[T_{i}]_{i\in I(x)}\big/ (T_{i}^{n_{i}}-f_{i})\), where the \(n_{i}\) are integers \(>0\) prime to \(p\), the morphism \(\overline{f}'\colon\overline{X'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{S}\) is \(0\)-acyclic. The geometric fibres of \(\overline{f}'\) at the different points of \(\overline{S}\) are therefore connected and even irreducible. The same therefore holds for the geometric fibres of the morphisms \(\overline{g}'\colon\overline{U'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{S}\), which proves that the \(\overline{g}'\), and hence also \(\widetilde{g}\), are \(0\)-acyclic (SGA 4 XV 1.16).

It is clear that a torsor \(P\) over \(\overline{U}\) with group \(F\), whose inverse image over \(\widetilde{U}\) is trivial, is tamely ramified over \(\overline{X}\) relatively to \(\overline{S}\). Let us show conversely that, if \(P\) is tamely ramified over \(\overline{X}\) relatively to \(\overline{S}\), its inverse image over \(\widetilde{U}\) is trivial.

It follows from (SGA 4 IX 2.14 (ii)) that one can find a finite morphism \(n\colon S_{1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{S}\), a constant sheaf of groups \(C\) on \(S_{1}\), a monomorphism \(G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} n_*C\). Let us consider the following commutative diagram formed of cartesian squares:

\[ \xymatrix{{\widetilde{U}_{1}}\ar[r]\ar[d]_{r}&{U_{1}}\ar[r]^{g_{1}}\ar[d]_{q}&{S_{1}}\ar[d]_{n}\\{\widetilde{U}}\ar[r]&{\overline{U}}\ar[r]^{\overline{g}}&{\overline{S} \rlap{.}}\\ } \]

Let \(C_{1}\) (resp. \(\widetilde{C}_{1}\)) be the inverse image of \(C\) on \(U_{1}\) (resp. on \(\widetilde{U}_{1}\)). We have a commutative diagram, in which \(i\) and \(j\) are isomorphisms (SGA 4 VIII 5.8):

\[ \tag{*} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{{\mathrm{H}^{1}(\overline{U},q_*C_{1})}\ar[r]^{i}\ar[d]&{\mathrm{H}^{1}(U_{1},C_{1})}\ar[d]\\{\mathrm{H}^{1}(\widetilde{U},r_*\widetilde{C}_{1})}\ar[r]^{j}&{\mathrm{H}^{1}(\widetilde{U}_{1},\widetilde{C}_{1}) \rlap{.}}\\ } \end{array} \]

Let \(Q\) be the torsor under \(q_*C_{1}\) deduced from \(P\) by extension of the structural group \(F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} q_*C_{1}\). By 1.4, \(Q\) is tamely ramified over \(\overline{X}\) relatively to \(\overline{S}\). To the torsor \(Q\) there corresponds, thanks to \(i\), a torsor \(Q_{1}\) under \(C_{1}\), and it is clear that \(Q_{1}\) is tamely ramified over \(X_{1}=\overline{X}\times_{\overline{S}}S_{1}\) relatively to \(S_{1}\). It therefore follows from 6 that the inverse image \(\widetilde{Q}_{1}\) of \(Q_{1}\) on \(\widetilde{U}_{1}\) is trivial, and the diagram (*) then shows that the inverse image \(\widetilde{Q}\) of \(Q\) on \(\widetilde{U}\) is trivial.

Let us consider the following commutative diagram, whose second row is exact (SGA 4 XII 3.1):

\[ \tag{**} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{{\mathrm{H}^{0}(\overline{S},n_*C/G)}\ar[r]\ar[d]_{k}&{\mathrm{H}^{1}(\overline{S},G)\rlap{$~=~1$}}\ar[d]\\{\mathrm{H}^{0}(\widetilde{U},r_*\widetilde{C}_{1}/\widetilde{F})}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{H}^{1}(\widetilde{U},\widetilde{F})}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{H}^{1}(\widetilde{U},r_*\widetilde{C}_{1}) \rlap{.}}\\ } \end{array} \]

Since the morphism \(\widetilde{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{S}\) is \(0\)-acyclic, \(k\) is an isomorphism. The fact that \(\widetilde{P}\) is trivial then follows from (**).

blocks.html: 5 Appendix I: Variations on Abhyankar's lemma
This appendix contains various variants of Abhyankar's lemma.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1

Let \(X=\operatorname{Spec} A\) be a regular local scheme, \(D=\sum_{1\leqslant i\leqslant r} \operatorname{div} f_i\) a normal crossings divisor, where the \(f_i\) are elements of the maximal ideal of \(A\) forming part of a regular system of parameters. Let \(n_i\), \(1\leqslant i\leqslant r\), be integers \(\geqslant 0\) and set

\[ X'=X[T_1,\dots,T_r]/(T_1^{n_1}-f_1,\dots,T_r^{n_r}-f_r), \]

\(U'=U\times_X X'\). Then \(X'\) is regular and \(U'\) is the complement in \(X'\) of the normal crossings divisor \(\sum_{1\leqslant i\leqslant r}\operatorname{div} T_i\). If the integers \(n_i\) are prime to the residue characteristic \(p\) of \(X\), \(U'\) is a connected étale covering of \(U\), tamely ramified relatively to \(D\) (3 c)).

Indeed \(X'\) is the spectrum of a local ring \(A'\) whose maximal ideal is generated by \(T_1,\dots,T_r\). (One may assume \(r=\dim(A)\).) Since \(A'\) is finite and flat over \(A\), hence of dimension \(r\), \(A'\) is regular (EGA \(0_{\textup{IV}}\) 17.1.1) and the \(T_i\) form a regular system of parameters of \(A'\). Suppose the \(n_i\) prime to \(p\). Since all the \(f_i\) are invertible on \(U\), the fact that \(U'\) is étale over \(U\) follows from I 4. Moreover \(U'\) is tamely ramified relatively to \(D\); indeed let \(x_i\) be the generic point of \(V(f_i)\), \(\overline{R}\) the strict localization of \(R=\mathscr{O}_{X,x_i}\), \(\overline{K}\) the field of fractions of \(\overline{R}\). Then the \(\overline{K}\)-algebra which represents \(U'|\overline{K}\) is obtained from the field \(\overline{K}[T_i]/(T_i^{n_i}-f_i)\) by making an unramified extension; it is therefore tamely ramified over \(\overline{R}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2 (Absolute Abhyankar lemma)

Let \(X\) be a regular local scheme,

\[ D=\sum_{1\leqslant i\leqslant r} \operatorname{div} f_i \]

a normal crossings divisor as in 1, \(Y=\operatorname{Supp} D\), \(U=X-Y\). Let \(V\) be an étale covering of \(U\) tamely ramified relatively to \(D\). If \(x_i\) is the generic point of the closed set \(V(f_i)\), \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x_i}\) is a discrete valuation ring with field of fractions \(K_i\), and one has \(V|K_i=\operatorname{Spec}(\prod_{j\in J_i}L_j)\), where the \(L_j\) are finite separable extensions of \(K_i\); let us denote by \(n_j\) the order of the inertia group of a Galois extension generated by \(L_j\) and by \(n_i\) the l.c.m. of the \(n_j\) as \(j\) ranges over \(J_i\). If one sets

\[ X'=X[T_1,\dots,T_r]/(T_1^{n_1}-f_1,\dots,T_r^{n_r}-f_r), \]

\(U'=U_{(X')}\), \(V'=V_{(X')}\), etc., the étale covering \(V'\) of \(U'\) extends uniquely, up to unique isomorphism, to an étale covering of \(X'\), and the \(n_i\) are prime to the residue characteristic \(p\) of \(X\).

The uniqueness follows from the fact that \(X'\) is normal (1); indeed an étale covering of \(X'\) which extends \(V'\) is isomorphic to the normalization of \(X'\) in the fibre of \(V'\) at the generic point of \(X'\) (2). If \(\overline{x}'\) is a geometric point of \(Y'\), one denotes by \(\overline{X}'\) the strict localization of \(X'\) at \(\overline{x}'\), \(\overline{V}'=V'_{(\overline{X}')}\), etc. By descent, taking into account uniqueness, it suffices to show that, for every geometric point \(\overline{x}'\) of \(Y'\), the étale covering \(\overline{V}'\) of \(\overline{U}'\) extends to \(\overline{X}'\). Given that an étale covering of an open set of the regular scheme \(X'\) which contains all the points \(x'\) such that one has \(\dim \mathscr{O}_{X',x'}\leqslant 1\) extends to the whole of \(X\) (SGA 2 XIV 1.11), one may even restrict to the points \(\overline{x}'\) which project onto a maximal point of \(Y'\). Now, at such a point \(\overline{x}'\), the fact that \(\overline{V}'\) extends to an étale covering of \(\overline{X}'\) follows from 6.
Let us show that the \(n_i\) are prime to \(p\). Indeed, if this were not the case, one would have for instance \(p|n_1\). Up to replacing \(X\) by
\[ X[T_1,\dots,T_r]/(T_1^{n_1/p}-f_1,T_2^{n_2}-f_2,\dots,T_r^{n_r}-f_r), \]

one reduces to the case where one has \(X'=X[T_1]/(T_1^p-f_1)\). It suffices to show that \(V\) extends to an étale covering of \(X\), for one will then have \(n_1=1\) contrary to the hypothesis. For this one may suppose that \(X\) is strictly local. Let \(Z\) be the closed subscheme of \(X\) with equation \(p=0\) and \(Z_1=Z\cap X_{f_1}\); \(Z_1\) is a non-empty open set of \(Z\). By what precedes, the étale covering \(V'\) of \(U'\) extends to an étale covering \(W'\) of \(X'\). Let \(W''_1\) and \(W''_2\) be the inverse images of \(W'\) by the two projections \(X''=X'\times_X X'\rightrightarrows X'\), and let us show that the descent isomorphism \(u\colon W''_1|U''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} W''_2|U''\) extends to an \(X''\)-morphism \(W''_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} W''_2\) which will necessarily be a descent datum on \(W'\) relatively to \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\). Let \(Z''\) (resp. \(Z''_1\)) be the inverse image of \(Z\) (resp. \(Z_1\)) in \(X''\). Since the morphism \(Z''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\) is radicial, there exists an isomorphism \(v\colon W''_1|Z''\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} W''_2|Z''\) which extends the isomorphism \(u|Z''_1\). But, since \(X\) is Henselian, one has a bijection
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{X''}(W''_1,W''_2)\simeq\operatorname{Hom}_{Z''}(W''_1|Z'',W_2''|Z''), \]

whence a morphism \(w\colon W''_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} W''_2\) lifting \(v\). The subscheme, both open and closed, of \(X''\) above which \(u\) and \(w\) coincide contains \(Z''_1\), hence equal to \(X''\), whence the fact that \(V\) extends to \(X\).

Let us resume the hypotheses and notations of 2, supposing moreover \(X\) strictly local. Then it follows from loc. cit. that every connected étale covering of \(U\) tamely ramified relatively to \(D\) is a quotient of a tamely ramified covering of the form
\[ U'=U[T_1,\dots,T_r]/(T_1^{n_1}-f_1,\dots,T_r^{n_r}-f_r), \]

where the \(n_i\) are integers prime to \(p\). Let \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) be the group of \(n\)-th roots of unity of \(U\). The group of \(U\)-automorphisms of \(U'\) is none other than the group \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n_1}\times\dots\times\boldsymbol{\mu}_{n_r}\), an \(n_i\)-th root of unity \(\xi_i\) operating on \(U'\) by transforming \(T_i\) into \(\xi_i T_i\). One thus has the following statement:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

Let \(X\) be a strictly local regular scheme of residue characteristic \(p\geqslant 0\), \(D=\sum_{1\leqslant i\leqslant n} \operatorname{div} f_i\) a normal crossings divisor on \(X\), \(U=X-\operatorname{Supp} D\). Set

\[ \widetilde{U}=\varprojlim_{(n_i)} U[T_1,\dots,T_r]/(T_1^{n_1}-f_1,\dots,T_r^{n_r}-f_r), \]

the inverse limit being taken along the filtered ordered set (for the divisibility relation) of the families of integers \(n_i>0\), prime to \(p\). Then \(\widetilde{U}\) is a universal tamely ramified covering of \(U\). Consequently the tamely ramified fundamental group of \(U\) is

\[ \pi_1^{\mathrm{t}}(U)\simeq \prod_{\ell\neq p} \mathbf{Z}_\ell [1]^r \qquad \text{(canonical isomorphism)} \]

where one has set \(\mathbf{Z}_\ell[1]=\varprojlim_{n>0}\boldsymbol{\mu}_{\ell^n}\). The group \(\pi_1^{\mathrm{t}}(U)\) is non-canonically isomorphic to \(\prod_{\ell\neq p} \mathbf{Z}_\ell^r\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(D=\sum_{1\leqslant i\leqslant r} \operatorname{div} f_i\) a normal crossings divisor relatively to \(S\) (1), where, for each point \(x\) of \(Y=\operatorname{Supp} D\), if \(I(x)\subset[1,r]\) is the set of the \(i\) such that one has \(f_i(x)=0\), the subscheme \(V((f_i)_{i\in I(x)})\) is smooth over \(S\) of codimension \(\operatorname{card} I(x)\) in \(X\). Let \(U=X-Y\). Let \(x\) be a point of \(Y\), \(X_1=\operatorname{Spec}\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\), \(U_1=U\times_X X_1\), \(n_i\), \(i\in I(x)\) integers and

\[ X'=X[T_i]_{i\in I(x)}/(T_i^{n_i}-f_i). \]

Then, if \(x'\) is the point of \(X'\) above \(x\), \(X'\) is smooth over \(S\) at \(x'\). If the integers \(n_i\) are prime to the characteristic \(p\) of \(\mathit{k}(x)\), \(U'_1=U_1\times_X X'\) is a connected étale covering of \(U_1\) tamely ramified over \(X_1\) relatively to \(S_1\) (1.1).

If \(s=f(x)\), the geometric fibre \(X'_{\overline{s}}\) is regular at the point \(x'\) (1); since \(X'\) is flat over \(S\) in a neighbourhood of \(Y\), this proves that \(X'\) is smooth over \(S\) at \(x'\) (EGA IV 12.1.6). If the integers \(n_i\) are prime to \(p\), \(U'_1\) is an étale covering of \(U_1\) (4); it is tamely ramified over \(X_1\) relatively to \(S\) because this is so on the geometric fibres at each point of \(S\) (1). Finally the fact that \(U'_1\) is connected follows from (SGA 4 XVI 3.2).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5 (Relative Abhyankar Lemma)

Let \(X\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(D\) a normal crossings divisor relatively to \(S\), as in (4). Let \(Y=X-\operatorname{Supp} D\), \(U=X- Y\), let \(x\) be a point of \(Y\), \(X_{1}\) the strict localization of \(X\) at a geometric point above \(x\), \(U_{1}=U\times_{X}X_{1}\), \(V_{1}\) an étale covering of \(U_{1}\). We suppose that, for every maximal point \(s\) of \(S\), \(V_{1\overline{s}}\) is tamely ramified over \(X_{1\overline{s}}\) relatively to \(\overline{s}\). Then one can find integers \(n_{i}\) prime to the characteristic \(p\) of \(\mathit{k}(x)\), with \(i\in I(x)\), such that, if one sets

\[ X'_{1}=X_{1}[T_{i}]_{i\in I(x)}\big/ (T_{i}^{n_{i}}-f_{i}) , \]

\(U'_{1}=U_{1}\times_{X_{1}}X'_{1}\), etc., the étale covering \(V'_{1}\) of \(U'_{1}\) extends in a unique way up to unique isomorphism to an étale covering of \(X'_{1}\). In particular \(V_{1}\) is tamely ramified over \(X_{1}\) relatively to \(S\).

One may suppose \(S\) local Noetherian with closed point \(f(x)\). For each maximal point \(s\) of \(S\) and for each \(i\in I(x)\), let \(x_{i}\) be the generic point of the closed set \(V(f_{i})\) of the fibre \(X_{1\overline{s}}\). The local ring \((\mathscr{O}_{X_{1},x_{i}})_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is a discrete valuation ring with field of fractions \(K_{i}\) and one has \(V_{1}|K_{i}=\operatorname{Spec}(\prod_{j\in I(x_{i})}L_{j})\), where \(L_{j}\) is a finite separable extension of \(K\); one denotes by \(n_{j}\) the order of the inertia group of a Galois extension generated by \(L_{j}\) and by \(n_{i}\) the l.c.m. of the \(n_{j}\) when \(s\) runs through the maximal points of \(S\) and \(j\in I(x_{i})\).

The \(n_{i}\) being thus chosen, we are going to show that \(V'_{1}\) extends in a unique way to an étale covering of \(X'_{1}\). The uniqueness follows from the fact that, \(X'\) being smooth over \(S\) at the points of \(Y\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}\mathrm{\acute{e}t}_{Y'_{1}}(X'_{1})\geqslant 2\) (SGA 4 XVI 3.2 or SGA 2 XIV 1.19). Let \(x'_{1}\) be a point of \(Y'_{1}\), \(\overline{x}'_{1}\) a geometric point above \(x'_{1}\), and let us denote by \(\overline{X'_{1}}\) the strict localization of \(X'_{1}\) at \(\overline{x}'_{1}\), \(\overline{U'_{1}}=U'_{1(\overline{X'_{1}})}\), etc. By descent, taking account of the uniqueness, it suffices to show that \(\overline{V}'_{1}\) extends to \(\overline{X'_{1}}\). Moreover one may restrict oneself to taking for \(x'_{1}\) the maximal points of \(Y'_{1}\); indeed one will then have an extension of \(V'_{1}\) over an open set \(W'_{1}\) of \(X'_{1}\) containing the maximal points of \(Y'_{1}\); now, if \(Z'_{1}=X'_{1}- W'_{1}\), one has \(\operatorname{codim}(Z'_{1s},X'_{1s})\geqslant 2\) if \(s\) is a maximal point of \(S\) and \(\operatorname{codim}(Z'_{1s},X'_{1s})\geqslant 1\), \(\operatorname{prof}\mathrm{\acute{e}t}_{s}(S)\geqslant 1\) if \(s\) is a point of \(S\) which is not maximal; the fact that \(V'_{1}\) extends to the whole of \(X'_{1}\) then follows from (SGA 2 XIV 1.20). But, at a geometric point \(\overline{x}'_{1}\) above a maximal point of \(Y'_{1}\), \(X'_{1\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, and the fact that \(\overline{V'_{1}}\) extends to \(\overline{X'_{1}}\) then follows from X 6.

Let us show that the \(n_{i}\) are prime to \(p\). Indeed, if this were not so, one would have an index \(i_{0}\in I(x)\) such that \(p\) divides \(n_{i_{0}}\). Up to replacing \(X\) by \(X_{1}[T_{i_{0}},T_{i}]_{i\in I(x)}\big/ (T_{i}^{n_{i_{0}}/p}-f_{i_{0}},T_{i}^{n_{i}}-f_{i})\), one reduces to the case where \(X'_{1}=X_{1}[T]/T^{p}-f_{i_{0}}\). By what precedes the étale covering \(V'_{1}\) of \(U'_{1}\) extends to an étale covering \(E'_{1}\) of \(X'_{1}\). Let \(\eta\) be the closed point of \(S\); since the morphism \(X'_{1\eta}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{1\eta}\) is radicial, \(V_{1\eta}\) extends to an étale covering \(E_{1\eta}\) of \(X_{1\eta}\). One then deduces from this, as in 2, that \(E'_{1}\) is equipped with a descent datum relatively to the morphism \(X'_{1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X_{1}\), which extends the natural descent datum that one has on \(E'_{1}|U'_{1}\). It follows that \(V_{1}\) extends to \(X_{1}\); but this entails that one has \(n_{i_{0}}=1\) contrary to the hypothesis \(n_{i_{0}}=p\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6

Let \(X\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(D=\sum_{1\leqslant i\leqslant r}\operatorname{div} f_{i}\) a normal crossings divisor relatively to \(S\), as in 4. Let \(\overline{x}\) be a geometric point of \(X\), \(\overline{X}\) the strict localization of \(X\) at \(\overline{x}\), \(\overline{Y}=Y_{(\overline{X})}\), \(\overline{U}=\overline{X}-\overline{Y}\) and

\[ \widetilde{U}= \varprojlim_{(n_{i})} \overline{U}[T_{i}]_{i\in I(x)}\big/ (T_{i}^{n_{i}}-f_{i}) , \]

the inverse limit being taken over the filtered set of families of integers \(n_{i}>0\), prime to the characteristic \(p\) of \(\mathit{k}(x)\). Then \(\widetilde{U}\) is a universal tamely ramified covering of \(\overline{U}\) relatively to \(S\). Consequently the tamely ramified fundamental group of \(\overline{U}\) is

\[ \pi_{1}^{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U})\simeq \prod_{\ell\neq p}\mathbf{Z}_{\ell}[1]^{I(x)} \qquad \textrm{(canonical isomorphism).} \]

The group \(\pi_{1}^{\mathrm{t}}(\overline{U})\) is non-canonically isomorphic to \(\prod_{\ell\neq p}\mathbf{Z}_{\ell}^{I(x)}\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 6.1

Let \(X\) be an \(S\)-scheme, \(D=\sum_{1\leqslant i\leqslant r}\operatorname{div} f_{i}\) a normal crossings divisor relatively to \(S\), as in 4, \(U=X-\operatorname{Supp} D\). For every subset \(I\subset[1,r]\) let

\[ X_{I}= \Big(\mathop{\textstyle\bigcap}\limits_{i\in I}V(f_{i})\Big) \cap \Big(\mathop{\textstyle\bigcap}\limits_{i\in\complement I}X_{f_{i}}\Big) . \]

Let \(p\) be a prime or zero integer and let \(Z\) be a subset of \(X_{I}\) all of whose points are of characteristic \(p\). Let

\[ \widetilde{U}_{I}= \varprojlim_{(n_{i})} U[T_{i}]_{i\in I} \big/ (T_{i}^{n_{i}}-f_{i}) , \]

where the inverse limit is taken over the filtered set of families of integers \(n_{i}>0\), prime to \(p\). Then, for every geometric point \(\overline{x}\) of \(Z\), the inverse image of \(\widetilde{U}_{I}\) over \(\overline{U}\) is identified with the universal tamely ramified covering of \(\overline{U}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7

The notations are those of 6. Let \(\overline{S}\) be the strict localization of \(S\) at \(\overline{x}\),

\[ \overline{g}\colon\overline{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{S} \quad \text{and} \quad \widetilde{g}\colon\widetilde{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\widetilde{S} \]

the canonical morphisms. Then the morphisms \(\overline{g}\) and \(\widetilde{g}\) are \(0\)-acyclic (SGA 4 XV 1.3). Let \(G\) be a constructible sheaf of groups on \(\overline{S}\), \(F=\overline{g}^*G\), \(P\) a torsor under \(F\). Then, in order that \(P\) be tamely ramified over \(\overline{X}\) relatively to \(\overline{S}\), it is necessary and sufficient that its inverse image \(\widetilde{P}\) over \(\widetilde{U}\) be trivial.

Indeed, for every scheme \(\overline{X'}=\overline{X}[T_{i}]_{i\in I(x)}\big/ (T_{i}^{n_{i}}-f_{i})\), where the \(n_{i}\) are integers \(>0\) prime to \(p\), the morphism \(\overline{f}'\colon\overline{X'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{S}\) is \(0\)-acyclic. The geometric fibres of \(\overline{f}'\) at the different points of \(\overline{S}\) are therefore connected and even irreducible. The same therefore holds for the geometric fibres of the morphisms \(\overline{g}'\colon\overline{U'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{S}\), which proves that the \(\overline{g}'\), and hence also \(\widetilde{g}\), are \(0\)-acyclic (SGA 4 XV 1.16).

It is clear that a torsor \(P\) over \(\overline{U}\) with group \(F\), whose inverse image over \(\widetilde{U}\) is trivial, is tamely ramified over \(\overline{X}\) relatively to \(\overline{S}\). Let us show conversely that, if \(P\) is tamely ramified over \(\overline{X}\) relatively to \(\overline{S}\), its inverse image over \(\widetilde{U}\) is trivial.

It follows from (SGA 4 IX 2.14 (ii)) that one can find a finite morphism \(n\colon S_{1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{S}\), a constant sheaf of groups \(C\) on \(S_{1}\), a monomorphism \(G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} n_*C\). Let us consider the following commutative diagram formed of cartesian squares:
\[ \xymatrix{{\widetilde{U}_{1}}\ar[r]\ar[d]_{r}&{U_{1}}\ar[r]^{g_{1}}\ar[d]_{q}&{S_{1}}\ar[d]_{n}\\{\widetilde{U}}\ar[r]&{\overline{U}}\ar[r]^{\overline{g}}&{\overline{S} \rlap{.}}\\ } \]

Let \(C_{1}\) (resp. \(\widetilde{C}_{1}\)) be the inverse image of \(C\) on \(U_{1}\) (resp. on \(\widetilde{U}_{1}\)). We have a commutative diagram, in which \(i\) and \(j\) are isomorphisms (SGA 4 VIII 5.8):

\[ \tag{*} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{{\mathrm{H}^{1}(\overline{U},q_*C_{1})}\ar[r]^{i}\ar[d]&{\mathrm{H}^{1}(U_{1},C_{1})}\ar[d]\\{\mathrm{H}^{1}(\widetilde{U},r_*\widetilde{C}_{1})}\ar[r]^{j}&{\mathrm{H}^{1}(\widetilde{U}_{1},\widetilde{C}_{1}) \rlap{.}}\\ } \end{array} \]

Let \(Q\) be the torsor under \(q_*C_{1}\) deduced from \(P\) by extension of the structural group \(F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} q_*C_{1}\). By 1.4, \(Q\) is tamely ramified over \(\overline{X}\) relatively to \(\overline{S}\). To the torsor \(Q\) there corresponds, thanks to \(i\), a torsor \(Q_{1}\) under \(C_{1}\), and it is clear that \(Q_{1}\) is tamely ramified over \(X_{1}=\overline{X}\times_{\overline{S}}S_{1}\) relatively to \(S_{1}\). It therefore follows from 6 that the inverse image \(\widetilde{Q}_{1}\) of \(Q_{1}\) on \(\widetilde{U}_{1}\) is trivial, and the diagram (*) then shows that the inverse image \(\widetilde{Q}\) of \(Q\) on \(\widetilde{U}\) is trivial.
Let us consider the following commutative diagram, whose second row is exact (SGA 4 XII 3.1):

\[ \tag{**} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{{\mathrm{H}^{0}(\overline{S},n_*C/G)}\ar[r]\ar[d]_{k}&{\mathrm{H}^{1}(\overline{S},G)\rlap{$~=~1$}}\ar[d]\\{\mathrm{H}^{0}(\widetilde{U},r_*\widetilde{C}_{1}/\widetilde{F})}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{H}^{1}(\widetilde{U},\widetilde{F})}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{H}^{1}(\widetilde{U},r_*\widetilde{C}_{1}) \rlap{.}}\\ } \end{array} \]

Since the morphism \(\widetilde{U}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{S}\) is \(0\)-acyclic, \(k\) is an isomorphism. The fact that \(\widetilde{P}\) is trivial then follows from (**).

pages.title:
Appendix II: finiteness theorem for the direct images
of stacks
pages.html:
6 Appendix II: finiteness theorem for the direct images of stacks

Proposition 1

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian scheme, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism. If \(S'\) is an \(S\)-scheme, we denote by \(X'\) (resp. \(f'\), etc.) the inverse image of \(X\) (resp. \(f\), etc.) under the morphism \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). Suppose that, for every scheme \(S'\) étale over \(S\), for every constructible sheaf of sets \(F\) on \(X'\), \(f'_*F\) is constructible, and that, for every constructible sheaf of groups \(F\) on \(X'\), \(\mathrm{R}^{1}f'_*F\) is constructible. Let \(\Phi\) be a \(1\)-constructible stack on \(X\) (0). Then \(f_*\Phi\) is \(1\)-constructible.

For every scheme \(S'\) étale over \(S\) and for every object \(x\) of \((f_*\Phi)_{S'}\), we have an isomorphism

\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{S'}(x)\simeq f'_*\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{X'}(x) , \]

where, in the right-hand side of the equality, \(x\) is considered as an object of \(\Phi_{X'}\). The hypotheses made thus imply that \(f_*\Phi\) is constructible. Let \(S\Phi\) be the sheaf of maximal subgerbes of \(\Phi\) [1]. Since \(f_*(S\Phi)\) is constructible, we may apply (SGA 4 IX 2.7) to it, and the fact that \(f_*\Phi\) is \(1\)-constructible then follows from the lemma below.

Lemma 1.1

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian scheme, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism, \(\Phi\) a stack on \(X\). Suppose given a sheaf on \(S\), representable by an étale \(S\)-scheme of finite type \(T\), a surjective morphism

\[ a\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_*(S\Phi ) \]

and an object \(p\) of the fibre \(\Phi_{X_T}\) (where \(X_T=X\times_ST\)), defining in \(f_*(S\Phi )(T)= S\Phi (X_T)\) an element equal to the image \(q\) under \(a\) of the identity section of \(T(T)\). Let \(f_T\colon X_T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) be the canonical morphism and suppose that the sheaf \(\mathrm{R}^1f_{T*}(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{X_T}(p))\) is constructible; then the same holds for \(S(f_*\Phi )\).

The canonical morphism \(f^*f_*\Phi\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\Phi\) gives a morphism

\[ S(f^*f_*\Phi )\simeq f^*(S(f_*\Phi ))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\Phi , \]

whence a canonical morphism

\[ \varphi\colon S(f_*\Phi )\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_*(S\Phi ). \]

Let \(F=S(f_*\Phi )\) and \(G\) the image of \(F\) under \(\varphi\); by (SGA 4 IX, 2.9) \(G\) is a constructible sheaf.

It suffices to show that, for every point \(s\) of \(S\), there exists a non-empty open \(U\) of \(s\) such that \(F|U\) is locally constant constructible. Let \(s\in S\), \(\overline{s}\) a geometric point above \(s\), \(\overline{q}_1,\dots,\overline{q}_n\) the elements of \(G_{\overline{s}}\). By definition of \(T\), there exist \(S\)-morphisms \(h_i\colon\overline{s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) such that we have \(\overline{q}_i=h_i^*(q)\). Let \(S'\) be the fibre product over \(S\) of \(n\) schemes isomorphic to \(T\), \(\overline{s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) the fibre product of the \(h_i\), \(X'=X\times_SS'\), \(q_i\) (resp. \(p_i\)) the inverse image of \(q\) (resp. \(p\)) under the \(i\)-th projection of \(S'\) onto \(T\). If \(\Psi_i\) is the maximal subgerbe of \(\Phi|X'\) generated by \(p_i\), the sheaf \(F_i=\mathrm{R}^1f_*'(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{X'}(p_i))\) is none other than the sheaf \(S(f_*'\Psi_i)\) of maximal subgerbes of \(f_*'\Psi_i\). In particular the canonical injection \(\Psi_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\Phi |X'\) gives a morphism

\[ \alpha_i\colon F_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F|S'. \]

We shall show that \(\alpha_i\) is a bijection of \(F_i\) onto the inverse image of \(q_i\) in \(F|S'\). For every scheme \(S''\) étale over \(S'\) every section \(y\) of \(F_i(S'')\) has as image \(q_i|S''\) in \(F(S'')\), because, locally for the étale topology on \(S''\), \(y\) is defined by an object \(x\) of \(\Phi_{X''}\) which is isomorphic to \(p_i|X''\). Conversely, if \(y\in F(S'')\) has as image \(q_i|S''\) in \(F(S'')\), locally for the étale topology on \(S''\), \(y\) is defined by an object \(x\) of \(\Phi_{X''}\) which is isomorphic to \(p_i\); consequently \(x\) is an object of \(\Psi_{iX''}\) and consequently \(y\in F_i(S'')\).

The proof is completed by using 1.2 below. Indeed one can find an open neighbourhood \(U'\) of \(s\) such that \(q_1|U',\dots,q_n|U'\) are sections of \(G(U')\) and generate this sheaf. Since the \(F_i|U'\) and \(G|U'\) are constructible, the same holds for \(F|U'\) by 1.2; possibly replacing \(U\) by a smaller open, \(F|U\) is locally constant, which completes the proof.

Lemma 1.2

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian scheme, \(F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) a surjective morphism of sheaves of groups on \(S\). Let \(q_i\) be a finite family of sections of \(G\) over \(X\) which generate \(G\), and, for each \(i\), let \(F_i\) be the subsheaf of \(F\) inverse image of \(q_i\). Then, if \(G\) and the \(F_i\) are constructible, the same holds for \(F\).

To prove that \(F\) is constructible, it suffices to show that, for every point \(s\) of \(S\), there exists an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\) such that \(F|U\) is locally constant constructible. So let \(s\) be a point of \(S\). Since the sheaves \(F_i\) and \(G\) are constructible, one can find an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\) such that \(F_i|U\) and \(G|U\) are locally constant. Let us then show that \(F|U\) is locally constant. By (SGA 4 IX 2.13 (i)), it suffices to see that, if \(\overline{s}\) is a geometric point above \(s\), \(\widetilde{s}\) a geometric point of \(U\) and \(\overline{s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\widetilde{s}\) a specialization morphism, the canonical morphism

\[ F_{\widetilde{s}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F_{\overline{s}} \]

is bijective.

We consider the commutative diagrams

\[ \xymatrix{{(F_i)_{\widetilde{s}}}\ar[r]^-{\widetilde{a}_i}\ar[d]^{\wr}&{F_{\widetilde{s}}}\ar[r]^-{\widetilde{a}}\ar[d]_b&{G_{\widetilde{s}}}\ar[d]^{\wr}\\{(F_i)_{\overline{s}}}\ar[r]^-{\overline{a}_i}&{F_{\overline{s}}}\ar[r]^-{\overline{a}}&{G_{\overline{s}}}} \]

Let \(\overline{q}_i\) (resp. \(\widetilde{q}_i\)) be the inverse image of \(q_i\) in \(G_{\overline{s}}\) (resp. \(G_{\widetilde{s}}\)); the morphisms \(\overline{a}\) and \(\widetilde{a}\) are surjective, and the morphism \(\overline{a}_i\) (resp. \(\widetilde{a}_i\)) induces a bijection of \((F_i)_{\overline{s}}\) (resp. \((F_i)_{\widetilde{s}}\)) onto \(\overline{a}^{-1}(q_i)\) (resp. onto \(\widetilde{a}^{-1}(q_i)\)). It follows therefore from the diagram above that \(b\) is an isomorphism.

Corollary 2

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian scheme, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a proper morphism. Let \(\Phi\) be a \(1\)-constructible stack on \(X\), then \(f_*\Phi\) is a \(1\)-constructible stack.

The proof of 1 also proves the following result, taking into account 4 2).

Corollary 3

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian scheme, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism, \(D\) a divisor on \(X\) with normal crossings relatively to \(S\) (1), \(Y=\operatorname{Supp} D\), \(U=X-Y\), \(i\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion. Let \(\Phi\) be a stack on \(U\) given, locally for the étale topology on \(X\) and \(S\), as the inverse image of a \(1\)-constructible stack \(\Psi\) on \(S\). Then the stack \(i_*^{\mathrm{t}}\Phi\) is \(1\)-constructible.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian scheme, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism. If \(S'\) is an \(S\)-scheme, we denote by \(X'\) (resp. \(f'\), etc.) the inverse image of \(X\) (resp. \(f\), etc.) under the morphism \(S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\). Suppose that, for every scheme \(S'\) étale over \(S\), for every constructible sheaf of sets \(F\) on \(X'\), \(f'_*F\) is constructible, and that, for every constructible sheaf of groups \(F\) on \(X'\), \(\mathrm{R}^{1}f'_*F\) is constructible. Let \(\Phi\) be a \(1\)-constructible stack on \(X\) (0). Then \(f_*\Phi\) is \(1\)-constructible.
For every scheme \(S'\) étale over \(S\) and for every object \(x\) of \((f_*\Phi)_{S'}\), we have an isomorphism
\[ \operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{S'}(x)\simeq f'_*\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{X'}(x) , \]

Let \(S\Phi\) be the sheaf of maximal subgerbes of \(\Phi\) [1]. Since \(f_*(S\Phi)\) is constructible, we may apply (SGA 4 IX 2.7) to it, and the fact that \(f_*\Phi\) is \(1\)-constructible then follows from the lemma below.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian scheme, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism, \(\Phi\) a stack on \(X\). Suppose given a sheaf on \(S\), representable by an étale \(S\)-scheme of finite type \(T\), a surjective morphism

\[ a\colon T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_*(S\Phi ) \]

and an object \(p\) of the fibre \(\Phi_{X_T}\) (where \(X_T=X\times_ST\)), defining in \(f_*(S\Phi )(T)= S\Phi (X_T)\) an element equal to the image \(q\) under \(a\) of the identity section of \(T(T)\). Let \(f_T\colon X_T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T\) be the canonical morphism and suppose that the sheaf \(\mathrm{R}^1f_{T*}(\operatorname{\mathbf{Aut}}_{X_T}(p))\) is constructible; then the same holds for \(S(f_*\Phi )\).
The canonical morphism \(f^*f_*\Phi\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\Phi\) gives a morphism
\[ S(f^*f_*\Phi )\simeq f^*(S(f_*\Phi ))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\Phi , \]
whence a canonical morphism
\[ \varphi\colon S(f_*\Phi )\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f_*(S\Phi ). \]

It suffices to show that, for every point \(s\) of \(S\), there exists a non-empty open \(U\) of \(s\) such that \(F|U\) is locally constant constructible. Let \(s\in S\), \(\overline{s}\) a geometric point above \(s\), \(\overline{q}_1,\dots,\overline{q}_n\) the elements of \(G_{\overline{s}}\). By definition of \(T\), there exist \(S\)-morphisms \(h_i\colon\overline{s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) such that we have \(\overline{q}_i=h_i^*(q)\). Let \(S'\) be the fibre product over \(S\) of \(n\) schemes isomorphic to \(T\), \(\overline{s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S'\) the fibre product of the \(h_i\), \(X'=X\times_SS'\), \(q_i\) (resp. \(p_i\)) the inverse image of \(q\) (resp. \(p\)) under the \(i\)-th projection of \(S'\) onto \(T\). In particular the canonical injection \(\Psi_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\Phi |X'\) gives a morphism
\[ \alpha_i\colon F_i\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F|S'. \]

We shall show that \(\alpha_i\) is a bijection of \(F_i\) onto the inverse image of \(q_i\) in \(F|S'\). For every scheme \(S''\) étale over \(S'\) every section \(y\) of \(F_i(S'')\) has as image \(q_i|S''\) in \(F(S'')\), because, locally for the étale topology on \(S''\), \(y\) is defined by an object \(x\) of \(\Phi_{X''}\) which is isomorphic to \(p_i|X''\). Conversely, if \(y\in F(S'')\) has as image \(q_i|S''\) in \(F(S'')\), locally for the étale topology on \(S''\), \(y\) is defined by an object \(x\) of \(\Phi_{X''}\) which is isomorphic to \(p_i\); consequently \(x\) is an object of \(\Psi_{iX''}\) and consequently \(y\in F_i(S'')\).

The proof is completed by using 1.2 below. Indeed one can find an open neighbourhood \(U'\) of \(s\) such that \(q_1|U',\dots,q_n|U'\) are sections of \(G(U')\) and generate this sheaf. Since the \(F_i|U'\) and \(G|U'\) are constructible, the same holds for \(F|U'\) by 1.2; possibly replacing \(U\) by a smaller open, \(F|U\) is locally constant, which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian scheme, \(F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) a surjective morphism of sheaves of groups on \(S\). Let \(q_i\) be a finite family of sections of \(G\) over \(X\) which generate \(G\), and, for each \(i\), let \(F_i\) be the subsheaf of \(F\) inverse image of \(q_i\). Then, if \(G\) and the \(F_i\) are constructible, the same holds for \(F\).

To prove that \(F\) is constructible, it suffices to show that, for every point \(s\) of \(S\), there exists an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\) such that \(F|U\) is locally constant constructible. So let \(s\) be a point of \(S\). Since the sheaves \(F_i\) and \(G\) are constructible, one can find an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\) such that \(F_i|U\) and \(G|U\) are locally constant. Let us then show that \(F|U\) is locally constant. By (SGA 4 IX 2.13 (i)), it suffices to see that, if \(\overline{s}\) is a geometric point above \(s\), \(\widetilde{s}\) a geometric point of \(U\) and \(\overline{s}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\widetilde{s}\) a specialization morphism, the canonical morphism
\[ F_{\widetilde{s}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F_{\overline{s}} \]
is bijective.
We consider the commutative diagrams

\[ \xymatrix{{(F_i)_{\widetilde{s}}}\ar[r]^-{\widetilde{a}_i}\ar[d]^{\wr}&{F_{\widetilde{s}}}\ar[r]^-{\widetilde{a}}\ar[d]_b&{G_{\widetilde{s}}}\ar[d]^{\wr}\\{(F_i)_{\overline{s}}}\ar[r]^-{\overline{a}_i}&{F_{\overline{s}}}\ar[r]^-{\overline{a}}&{G_{\overline{s}}}} \]

Let \(\overline{q}_i\) (resp. \(\widetilde{q}_i\)) be the inverse image of \(q_i\) in \(G_{\overline{s}}\) (resp. \(G_{\widetilde{s}}\)); the morphisms \(\overline{a}\) and \(\widetilde{a}\) are surjective, and the morphism \(\overline{a}_i\) (resp. \(\widetilde{a}_i\)) induces a bijection of \((F_i)_{\overline{s}}\) (resp. \((F_i)_{\widetilde{s}}\)) onto \(\overline{a}^{-1}(q_i)\) (resp. onto \(\widetilde{a}^{-1}(q_i)\)). It follows therefore from the diagram above that \(b\) is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian scheme, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a proper morphism. Let \(\Phi\) be a \(1\)-constructible stack on \(X\), then \(f_*\Phi\) is a \(1\)-constructible stack.
The proof of 1 also proves the following result, taking into account 4 2).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian scheme, \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism, \(D\) a divisor on \(X\) with normal crossings relatively to \(S\) (1), \(Y=\operatorname{Supp} D\), \(U=X-Y\), \(i\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion. Let \(\Phi\) be a stack on \(U\) given, locally for the étale topology on \(X\) and \(S\), as the inverse image of a \(1\)-constructible stack \(\Psi\) on \(S\). Then the stack \(i_*^{\mathrm{t}}\Phi\) is \(1\)-constructible.
